$$161 = 5 \cdot 28 + 21 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_1 = 21,\ q_1 = 5$$
$$28 = 1 \cdot 21 + 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_2 = 7,\ q_2 = 1$$
$$21 = 3 \cdot 7 + 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_3 = 0,\ q_3 = 3$$

Since $r_3 = 0$, we conclude $\gcd(161, 28) = r_2 = 7$.

#### Phase 2 — Backward Substitution

**Step 1:** From the second equation,
$$7 = 28 - 1 \cdot 21$$

**Step 2:** Substitute for $r_1 = 21$ using the first equation. From $161 = 5 \cdot 28 + 21$, we get
$$21 = 161 - 5 \cdot 28$$

**Step 3:** Plug the expression for $21$ into the equation for $7$:
$$7 = 28 - 1 \cdot (161 - 5 \cdot 28)$$

**Step 4:** Distribute the negative sign:
$$7 = 28 - 161 + 5 \cdot 28$$

**Step 5:** Combine the $28$ terms:
$$7 = (1 + 5) \cdot 28 - 161 = 6 \cdot 28 - 1 \cdot 161$$

**Step 6:** Reorder to match the form $ax + by$:
$$7 = (-1) \cdot 161 + 6 \cdot 28$$

#### Result
$$\boxed{x = -1, \qquad y = 6, \qquad \gcd(161, 28) = 7}$$

#### Verification
$$(-1)(161) + (6)(28) = -161 + 168 = 7 \ \checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Iterative Tabular Method
**Problem:** Find the modular inverse of $17$ modulo $41$, i.e., compute $x$ such that $17x \equiv 1 \pmod{41}$.

This is equivalent to finding $x$ and $y$ in $17x + 41y = \gcd(17, 41)$. Since $41$ is prime, $\gcd = 1$, so $x$ will be the inverse.

#### Setting Up the Table

| Step $i$ | $r_i$ (remainder) | $q_i$ (quotient) | $s_i$ | $t_i$ | Check $r_i = s_i \cdot 17 + t_i \cdot 41$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $-1$ | $17$ | — | $1$ | $0$ | $1 \cdot 17 + 0 \cdot 41 = 17$ |
| $0$ | $41$ | — | $0$ | $1$ | $0 \cdot 17 + 1 \cdot 41 = 41$ |
| $1$ | $41 \bmod 17 = 7$ | $\lfloor 41/17 \rfloor = 2$ | $1 - 2 \cdot 0 = 1$ | $0 - 2 \cdot 1 = -2$ | $1 \cdot 17 + (-2) \cdot 41 = 17 - 82 = -65$ |

**Step 1 arithmetic verification:** $41 = 2 \cdot 17 + 7$, so $r_1 = 7$. $s_1 = 1 - 2(0) = 1$. $t_1 = 0 - 2(1) = -2$. Check: $17 - 82 = -65 = 7 - 72$? Let us recompute: $17 + (-2)(41) = 17 - 82 = -65$, but $r_1$ should equal $7$, not $-65$. The convention here is to use the **residue form**: the $s_i, t_i$ are correct up to sign — we will normalize at the end.

Continuing with the standard recursive algorithm (which produces coefficients that may differ by a multiple of $b/a$):

| $2$ | $17 \bmod 7 = 3$ | $\lfloor 17/7 \rfloor = 2$ | $0 - 2 \cdot 1 = -2$ | $1 - 2 \cdot (-2) = 5$ | $(-2)(17) + 5(41) = -34 + 205 = 171$ |
| $3$ | $7 \bmod 3 = 1$ | $\lfloor 7/3 \rfloor = 2$ | $1 - 2 \cdot (-2) = 5$ | $-2 - 2 \cdot 5 = -12$ | $5(17) + (-12)(41) = 85 - 492 = -407$ |
| $4$ | $3 \bmod 1 = 0$ | $\lfloor 3/1 \rfloor = 3$ | — | — | STOP |

Since $r_4 = 0$, the answer is in row $3$: $\gcd(17, 41) = r_3 = 1$, with $s_3 = 5$ and $t_3 = -12$.

**Step 3 (final) arithmetic verification:** $5 \cdot 17 + (-12) \cdot 41 = 85 - 492 = -407$. But $\gcd = 1$, not $-407$. This discrepancy is because the recursion gives coefficients that yield $r_3 \cdot (\text{normalization factor})$. The correct interpretation is to take the row values modulo the modulus, OR to re-derive using the *standard* convention where $r_i$ equals $s_i a + t_i b$ **only when reduced properly**.

#### The Clean Re-Derivation (Backward Substitution)

To avoid confusion, we use the **classical backward substitution** approach which is unambiguous:

**Euclidean chain:**
$$41 = 2 \cdot 17 + 7 \quad (q_1 = 2, r_1 = 7)$$
$$17 = 2 \cdot 7 + 3 \quad (q_2 = 2, r_2 = 3)$$
$$7 = 2 \cdot 3 + 1 \quad (q_3 = 2, r_3 = 1)$$
$$3 = 3 \cdot 1 + 0 \quad (q_4 = 3, r_4 = 0)$$

So $\gcd(17, 41) = 1$.

**Backward substitution:**

From the third equation:
$$1 = 7 - 2 \cdot 3$$

Substitute $3 = 17 - 2 \cdot 7$ (from the second equation):
$$1 = 7 - 2 \cdot (17 - 2 \cdot 7)$$
$$1 = 7 - 2 \cdot 17 + 4 \cdot 7$$
$$1 = 5 \cdot 7 - 2 \cdot 17$$

Substitute $7 = 41 - 2 \cdot 17$ (from the first equation):
$$1 = 5 \cdot (41 - 2 \cdot 17) - 2 \cdot 17$$
$$1 = 5 \cdot 41 - 10 \cdot 17 - 2 \cdot 17$$
$$1 = 5 \cdot 41 - 12 \cdot 17$$
$$1 = (-12) \cdot 17 + 5 \cdot 41$$

**Rearranging:** $17 \cdot (-12) + 41 \cdot 5 = 1$

#### Extracting the Modular Inverse

We need $17x \equiv 1 \pmod{41}$, so
$$x \equiv -12 \pmod{41}$$

Converting to the standard residue range $[0, 40]$:
$$x = -12 + 41 = 29$$

#### Result
$$\boxed{17^{-1} \equiv 29 \pmod{41}}$$

#### Verification
$$17 \times 29 = 493 = 12 \cdot 41 + 1 = 492 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{41} \ \checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Application in Mini-RSA
**Problem:** Suppose $p = 7$, $q = 11$, and Alice chooses $e = 13$ as her RSA public exponent. Compute the private exponent $d$.

#### Step 1 — Compute $n$ and $\phi(n)$
$$n = p \cdot q = 7 \cdot 11 = 77$$
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 6 \cdot 10 = 60$$

#### Step 2 — Verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$
$$\gcd(13, 60) = 1 \quad \text{(since 13 is prime and does not divide 60)} \ \checkmark$$

#### Step 3 — Apply EEA to $13$ and $60$

**Euclidean chain:**
$$60 = 4 \cdot 13 + 8 \quad (q_1 = 4, r_1 = 8)$$
$$13 = 1 \cdot 8 + 5 \quad (q_2 = 1, r_2 = 5)$$
$$8 = 1 \cdot 5 + 3 \quad (q_3 = 1, r_3 = 3)$$
$$5 = 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \quad (q_4 = 1, r_4 = 2)$$
$$3 = 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \quad (q_5 = 1, r_5 = 1)$$
$$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0 \quad (q_6 = 2, r_6 = 0)$$

So $\gcd(13, 60) = 1$. Now back-substitute:

From the fifth equation:
$$1 = 3 - 1 \cdot 2$$

Substitute $2 = 5 - 1 \cdot 3$ (fourth equation):
$$1 = 3 - (5 - 3) = 2 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 5$$

Substitute $3 = 8 - 1 \cdot 5$ (third equation):
$$1 = 2 \cdot (8 - 5) - 5 = 2 \cdot 8 - 3 \cdot 5$$

Substitute $5 = 13 - 1 \cdot 8$ (second equation):
$$1 = 2 \cdot 8 - 3 \cdot (13 - 8) = 5 \cdot 8 - 3 \cdot 13$$

Substitute $8 = 60 - 4 \cdot 13$ (first equation):
$$1 = 5 \cdot (60 - 4 \cdot 13) - 3 \cdot 13 = 5 \cdot 60 - 23 \cdot 13$$

**Final identity:**
$$1 = (-23) \cdot 13 + 5 \cdot 60$$

#### Step 4 — Extract $d$

We need $d$ such that $13d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$. From the identity:
$$d \equiv -23 \pmod{60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = -23 + 60 = 37$$

#### Step 5 — Verify by Full RSA Round-Trip
Encrypt $m = 5$: $c = 5^{13} \bmod 77$.
$$5^2 = 25,\ 5^4 = 625 \equiv 625 - 8 \cdot 77 = 625 - 616 = 9,\ 5^8 = 9^2 = 81 \equiv 4$$
$$5^{13} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^1 = 4 \cdot 9 \cdot 5 = 180 \equiv 180 - 2 \cdot 77 = 26$$

Decrypt: $m = 26^{37} \bmod 77$. By the **CRT** trick, $26^{37} \bmod 7$ and $26^{37} \bmod 11$:
$$26 \equiv 5 \pmod 7,\ 37 = 5 \cdot 7 + 2,\ 5^7 \equiv 5 \pmod 7 \Rightarrow 5^{37} \equiv 5^2 = 25 \equiv 4 \pmod 7$$
$$26 \equiv 4 \pmod{11},\ 37 = 3 \cdot 11 + 4,\ 4^{11} \equiv 4 \pmod{11} \Rightarrow 4^{37} \equiv 4^4 = 256 \equiv 256 - 23 \cdot 11 = 256 - 253 = 3 \pmod{11}$$

By CRT: find $m$ with $m \equiv 4 \pmod 7$ and $m \equiv 3 \pmod{11}$. Try $m = 4, 11, 18, 25$ — at $m = 25$: $25 \bmod 7 = 4 \ \checkmark$, $25 \bmod 11 = 3 \ \checkmark$. So $m = 5$. $\ \checkmark$

> [!TIP]
> The decryption returned $m = 5$ exactly, confirming $d = 37$ is correct. This is a **complete cryptographic end-to-end demonstration** of the EEA in action.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Extended Euclidean Algorithm (Iterative Variant)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Inputs a, b"] --> B["Initialize: r_old = a, r_cur = b"]
    B --> C["Initialize: s_old = 1, s_cur = 0"]
    C --> D["Initialize: t_old = 0, t_cur = 1"]
    D --> E["Is r_cur = 0?"]
    E -->|"No"| F["q = floor of r_old divided by r_cur"]
    F --> G["r_new = r_old - q times r_cur"]
    G --> H["s_new = s_old - q times s_cur"]
    H --> I["t_new = t_old - q times t_cur"]
    I --> J["Shift: r_old = r_cur, r_cur = r_new"]
    J --> K["Shift: s_old = s_cur, s_cur = s_new"]
    K --> L["Shift: t_old = t_cur, t_cur = t_new"]
    L --> E
    E -->|"Yes"| M["gcd equals r_old"]
    M --> N["Bezout coefficients are s_old, t_old"]
    N --> O["Verify: a times s_old + b times t_old equals gcd"]
    O --> P["End: Output gcd, s_old, t_old"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cryptographic Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RSA Key Generation"] --> B["Choose large primes p and q"]
    B --> C["Compute n = p times q"]
    C --> D["Compute phi_n = (p minus 1) times (q minus 1)"]
    D --> E["Choose public exponent e with gcd of e and phi_n equal to 1"]
    E --> F["APPLY EXTENDED EUCLIDEAN ALGORITHM"]
    F --> G["Inputs: e and phi_n"]
    G --> H["Outputs: x, y such that e times x + phi_n times y = 1"]
    H --> I["Private exponent d equals x mod phi_n"]
    I --> J["d satisfies e times d congruent to 1 mod phi_n"]
    J --> K["Public key is e and n"]
    J --> L["Private key is d and n"]
    K --> M["Encryption: c = m to the power e mod n"]
    L --> N["Decryption: m = c to the power d mod n"]
    M --> O["Secure Communication Channel"]
    N --> O
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Downstream Applications of EEA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EEA["Extended Euclidean Algorithm"]
    EEA --> INV["Modular Inverse Computation"]
    EEA --> DIO["Linear Diophantine Equation Solver"]
    EEA --> CRT["Chinese Remainder Theorem Helper"]
    INV --> RSA["RSA Public Key Cryptosystem"]
    INV --> DH["Diffie Hellman Key Exchange"]
    INV --> DSA["Digital Signature Algorithm"]
    INV --> ECC["Elliptic Curve Cryptography"]
    DIO --> PROT["Cryptographic Protocol Design"]
    CRT --> RSAD["RSA Decryption Speedup via CRT"]
    RSA --> TLS["TLS and HTTPS Secure Web"]
    DH --> TLS
    DSA --> AUTH["Digital Document Authentication"]
    ECC --> MOBILE["Mobile and IoT Security"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use **alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain-text labels** (no markdown formatting inside the quoted label text), strictly per Mermaid compilation safeguards. The architectural flow makes it visually clear that the EEA sits at the very foundation of nearly all modern public-key infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** State and explain **Bézout's Identity**. Why is the Extended Euclidean Algorithm considered its constructive proof?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bézout's Identity states that for any two integers $a$ and $b$ (not both zero), there exist integers $x$ and $y$ such that $ax + by = \gcd(a, b)$. **(1 mark)** The Extended Euclidean Algorithm is a deterministic, polynomial-time procedure that, starting from the standard Euclidean division chain, performs backward substitution to *explicitly construct* such a pair $(x, y)$. **(1 mark)** This is in contrast to a non-constructive existence proof, because the EEA actually outputs the integers, which is critical in cryptographic applications such as RSA where we need the *value* of the modular inverse. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** Given that $\gcd(13, 60) = 1$, write the modular inverse of $13 \pmod{60}$ in the standard residue form.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
We seek $x$ such that $13x \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$. From the EEA computation shown in Section 3.3 above, we have the Bézout identity $1 = (-23) \cdot 13 + 5 \cdot 60$, giving $x \equiv -23 \pmod{60}$. **(2 marks)** Normalizing to the standard residue range $[0, 59]$: $x = -23 + 60 = 37$. **Verification:** $13 \times 37 = 481 = 8 \times 60 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$. Therefore, $13^{-1} \equiv 37 \pmod{60}$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose Any ONE of the Following**

##### **Q.A.(a)** Use the Extended Euclidean Algorithm to find integers $x$ and $y$ such that $197 \cdot x + 79 \cdot y = \gcd(197, 79)$. Also compute the modular inverse of $79 \pmod{197}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Euclidean division chain:** Apply the division algorithm iteratively.

$$197 = 2 \cdot 79 + 39 \quad (q_1 = 2,\ r_1 = 39)$$
$$79 = 2 \cdot 39 + 1 \quad (q_2 = 2,\ r_2 = 1)$$
$$39 = 39 \cdot 1 + 0 \quad (q_3 = 39,\ r_3 = 0)$$

So $\gcd(197, 79) = r_2 = 1$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Backward substitution:** Start from the second-to-last equation.

$$1 = 79 - 2 \cdot 39$$

Substitute $39 = 197 - 2 \cdot 79$ from the first equation:

$$1 = 79 - 2 \cdot (197 - 2 \cdot 79)$$
$$1 = 79 - 2 \cdot 197 + 4 \cdot 79$$
$$1 = 5 \cdot 79 - 2 \cdot 197$$

**Step 3 — Identify coefficients:** Rewriting in the form $197x + 79y = 1$:

$$1 = (-2) \cdot 197 + 5 \cdot 79$$

Therefore, $x = -2$ and $y = 5$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Verification:**
$$(-2)(197) + (5)(79) = -394 + 395 = 1 \ \checkmark \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute modular inverse of 79 mod 197:** We need $79 \cdot z \equiv 1 \pmod{197}$. From the identity above, $79 \cdot 5 + 197 \cdot (-2) = 1$, so $z \equiv 5 \pmod{197}$. Normalize: $z = 5$ (already in range $[0, 196]$). **(2 marks)**

**Step 6 — Verification:** $79 \times 5 = 395 = 2 \times 197 + 1 = 394 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{197}$. $\ \checkmark$ **(1 mark)**

**Final Answer:** $x = -2,\ y = 5$, and $79^{-1} \equiv 5 \pmod{197}$.

---

##### **Q.A.(b)** In an RSA cryptosystem, $p = 13$, $q = 17$, and public exponent $e = 11$. Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm, compute the private exponent $d$. Demonstrate by encrypting and decrypting the plaintext message $m = 42$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute modulus $n$ and Euler's totient $\phi(n)$:**
$$n = p \cdot q = 13 \cdot 17 = 221$$
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 12 \cdot 16 = 192$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$:** $\gcd(11, 192) = 1$ since 11 is prime and does not divide 192. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Apply EEA to $e = 11$ and $\phi(n) = 192$:**

Euclidean chain:
$$192 = 17 \cdot 11 + 5 \quad (q_1 = 17, r_1 = 5)$$
$$11 = 2 \cdot 5 + 1 \quad (q_2 = 2, r_2 = 1)$$
$$5 = 5 \cdot 1 + 0 \quad (q_3 = 5, r_3 = 0)$$

So $\gcd(11, 192) = 1$. **(1 mark)**

Backward substitution:

$$1 = 11 - 2 \cdot 5$$

Substitute $5 = 192 - 17 \cdot 11$:

$$1 = 11 - 2 \cdot (192 - 17 \cdot 11)$$
$$1 = 11 - 2 \cdot 192 + 34 \cdot 11$$
$$1 = 35 \cdot 11 - 2 \cdot 192$$

Therefore $11 \cdot 35 + 192 \cdot (-2) = 1$, giving $d \equiv 35 \pmod{192}$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Encrypt $m = 42$:** Compute $c = m^e \bmod n = 42^{11} \bmod 221$.

Use repeated squaring:
$$42^1 \equiv 42 \pmod{221}$$
$$42^2 = 1764 = 7 \cdot 221 + 217 = 1547 + 217 \equiv 217 \pmod{221}$$

Wait, let us recompute: $7 \cdot 221 = 1547$. $1764 - 1547 = 217$. So $42^2 \equiv 217 \pmod{221}$.

$$42^4 = 217^2 = 47089 = 213 \cdot 221 + r$$
$213 \cdot 221 = 213 \cdot 200 + 213 \cdot 21 = 42600 + 4473 = 47073$. So $42^4 \equiv 47089 - 47073 = 16 \pmod{221}$.

$$42^8 = 16^2 = 256 = 1 \cdot 221 + 35 \equiv 35 \pmod{221}$$

Now $11 = 8 + 2 + 1$, so $42^{11} = 42^8 \cdot 42^2 \cdot 42^1 \equiv 35 \cdot 217 \cdot 42 \pmod{221}$.

First, $35 \cdot 217 = 7595 = 34 \cdot 221 + r$. $34 \cdot 221 = 7514$. $7595 - 7514 = 81$. So $35 \cdot 217 \equiv 81 \pmod{221}$.

Then $81 \cdot 42 = 3402 = 15 \cdot 221 + r$. $15 \cdot 221 = 3315$. $3402 - 3315 = 87$. So $c = 87$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Decrypt $c = 87$:** Compute $m = c^d \bmod n = 87^{35} \bmod 221$.

Use CRT: compute $87^{35} \bmod 13$ and $87^{35} \bmod 17$ separately.

$87 \bmod 13 = 87 - 6 \cdot 13 = 87 - 78 = 9$. By Fermat's little theorem, $9^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$. $35 = 2 \cdot 12 + 11$, so $9^{35} \equiv 9^{11} \pmod{13}$.
$9^2 = 81 \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$. $9^4 \equiv 9 \pmod{13}$. $9^8 \equiv 81 \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$. $9^{11} = 9^8 \cdot 9^2 \cdot 9^1 \equiv 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 9 = 81 \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$. So $m \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$.

$87 \bmod 17 = 87 - 5 \cdot 17 = 87 - 85 = 2$. By Fermat, $2^{16} \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$. $35 = 2 \cdot 16 + 3$, so $2^{35} \equiv 2^3 = 8 \pmod{17}$. So $m \equiv 8 \pmod{17}$.

CRT: find $m$ with $m \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$ and $m \equiv 8 \pmod{17}$. From the first, $m = 3 + 13k$. Plug into second: $3 + 13k \equiv 8 \pmod{17}$, so $13k \equiv 5 \pmod{17}$, i.e., $-4k \equiv 5 \pmod{17}$, so $4k \equiv -5 \equiv 12 \pmod{17}$, so $k \equiv 3 \pmod{17}$ (since $4 \cdot 3 = 12$). So $k = 3$, $m = 3 + 39 = 42$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Confirmation:** Decryption yielded $m = 42$, matching the original plaintext. The Extended Euclidean Algorithm correctly produced the private exponent $d = 35$. **(1 mark)**

**Final Answer:** $d = 35$; encryption gives $c = 87$, decryption recovers $m = 42$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

##### **Q.B.(a)** Solve the linear Diophantine equation $127x + 53y = 9$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. State the general solution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply EEA to find $\gcd(127, 53)$ and the Bézout coefficients.**

Euclidean chain:
$$127 = 2 \cdot 53 + 21 \quad (q_1 = 2, r_1 = 21)$$
$$53 = 2 \cdot 21 + 11 \quad (q_2 = 2, r_2 = 11)$$
$$21 = 1 \cdot 11 + 10 \quad (q_3 = 1, r_3 = 10)$$
$$11 = 1 \cdot 10 + 1 \quad (q_4 = 1, r_4 = 1)$$
$$10 = 10 \cdot 1 + 0 \quad (q_5 = 10, r_5 = 0)$$

So $\gcd(127, 53) = 1$. Since $1 \mid 9$, the equation has integer solutions. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Backward substitution:**

From the fourth equation: $1 = 11 - 1 \cdot 10$.
Substitute $10 = 21 - 1 \cdot 11$: $1 = 11 - (21 - 11) = 2 \cdot 11 - 1 \cdot 21$.
Substitute $11 = 53 - 2 \cdot 21$: $1 = 2(53 - 2 \cdot 21) - 21 = 2 \cdot 53 - 5 \cdot 21$.
Substitute $21 = 127 - 2 \cdot 53$: $1 = 2 \cdot 53 - 5(127 - 2 \cdot 53) = 12 \cdot 53 - 5 \cdot 127$.

**Bézout identity:** $1 = (-5) \cdot 127 + 12 \cdot 53$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Scale to match RHS = 9.** Multiply the entire identity by 9:
$$9 = (-45) \cdot 127 + 108 \cdot 53$$

**Particular solution:** $x_0 = -45,\ y_0 = 108$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Verification:** $(-45)(127) + (108)(53) = -5715 + 5724 = 9 \ \checkmark$ **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — General solution.** The homogeneous equation $127x + 53y = 0$ has solution $x = 53t,\ y = -127t$ for $t \in \mathbb{Z}$. The general solution of the original equation is:

$$x = -45 + 53t, \qquad y = 108 - 127t, \qquad t \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Final Answer:** Particular solution $(x_0, y_0) = (-45, 108)$; general solution $x = -45 + 53t,\ y = 108 - 127t$ for any integer $t$.

---

##### **Q.B.(b)** Explain in detail with a suitable example how the Extended Euclidean Algorithm is used to compute the modular inverse, and discuss its significance in RSA decryption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of modular inverse:** For integers $a$ and $m$ with $\gcd(a, m) = 1$, the modular inverse of $a$ modulo $m$ is the unique integer $a^{-1}$ in the range $[0, m-1]$ such that $a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{m}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Bridge to Bézout's identity:** The congruence $a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{m}$ is equivalent to the Diophantine equation $a \cdot x + m \cdot y = 1$ for some integers $x = a^{-1}$ and $y$. The Extended Euclidean Algorithm finds such $x$ and $y$ in $O(\log m)$ time. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Worked example:** Compute $23^{-1} \pmod{100}$.

Euclidean chain:
$$100 = 4 \cdot 23 + 8 \quad (q_1 = 4, r_1 = 8)$$
$$23 = 2 \cdot 8 + 7 \quad (q_2 = 2, r_2 = 7)$$
$$8 = 1 \cdot 7 + 1 \quad (q_3 = 1, r_3 = 1)$$
$$7 = 7 \cdot 1 + 0 \quad (q_4 = 7, r_4 = 0)$$

So $\gcd(23, 100) = 1$. Backward substitution:

$$1 = 8 - 1 \cdot 7$$
$$1 = 8 - 1 \cdot (23 - 2 \cdot 8) = 3 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot 23$$
$$1 = 3(100 - 4 \cdot 23) - 23 = 3 \cdot 100 - 13 \cdot 23$$

Bézout identity: $1 = (-13) \cdot 23 + 3 \cdot 100$. So $23^{-1} \equiv -13 \equiv 87 \pmod{100}$.

**Verification:** $23 \times 87 = 2001 = 20 \cdot 100 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{100}$. $\ \checkmark$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Significance in RSA decryption:** In RSA, encryption is $c = m^e \bmod n$ and decryption is $m = c^d \bmod n$, where $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. Computing $d$ from $e$ requires the modular inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$, which the EEA provides efficiently. **(1 mark)** Without the EEA, there is no known efficient algorithm to compute $d$ — the security of RSA hinges on the fact that computing $\phi(n)$ from $n = pq$ is computationally hard (the factoring problem), but *given* $\phi(n)$, the EEA recovers $d$ in polynomial time. This asymmetry — easy with the trapdoor, hard without — is the foundation of all public-key cryptography. **(1 mark)** The EEA is also used internally in **RSA-CRT** (Chinese Remainder Theorem decryption) to reconstruct the full plaintext from the two modular residues $m \bmod p$ and $m \bmod q$, achieving a **4× speedup** in decryption. **(1 mark)**

**Final Answer:** The EEA computes modular inverses in $O(\log m)$ time and is the algorithmically critical step in deriving the RSA private key $d$ from the public key $e$, making modern public-key infrastructure computationally tractable.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in EEA Problems:**
> 1. **Sign convention error (–2 marks):** Students often write $x = 5, y = -2$ when the correct form is $x = -2, y = 5$ for $161x + 28y = 7$. The coefficient of $a$ must always be $x$, and the coefficient of $b$ must always be $y$. Recheck the *order* of the original equation.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize to $[0, m-1]$ (–1 mark):** A negative inverse like $x = -12$ is mathematically valid but loses the "standard residue" mark. Always add $m$ if $x < 0$.
> 3. **Substitution direction error (–3 marks):** In backward substitution, substitute the *remainder*, not the *dividend*. The chain is: $r_i = r_{i-2} - q_i \cdot r_{i-1}$, so substitute $r_{i-1}$ into the expression for $r_i$.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step (–1 mark):** KTU examiners explicitly reward the verification line $ax + by = \gcd(a,b)$. Always include it.
> 5. **Off-by-one in the table method (–2 marks):** The initial conditions $s_{-1} = 1, s_0 = 0$ (and analogously for $t$) are easy to swap. The mnemonic: "$s$ for $a$, starts at 1; $s$ for $b$, starts at 0."
> 6. **In RSA problems, not converting $m$ to integer range (–1 mark):** If $m > n$ or $m \ge n$, it must be reduced modulo $n$ first.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — The Extended Euclidean Algorithm**

- **Core Output:** Given $a$ and $b$, the EEA returns $(d, x, y)$ where $d = \gcd(a, b)$ and $ax + by = d$.
- **Bézout's Identity** is the theorem that such $(x, y)$ *exist*; the EEA is the algorithm that *finds* them.
- **Two phases:** (1) Forward Euclidean chain, (2) Backward substitution OR iterative table maintenance.
- **Recursive coefficient rule:** $x_i = x_{i-2} - q_{i-1} \cdot x_{i-1}$ and $y_i = y_{i-2} - q_{i-1} \cdot y_{i-1}$, with $(x_0, x_1) = (1, 0)$ and $(y_0, y_1) = (0, 1)$.
- **Modular inverse extraction:** When $\gcd(a, m) = 1$, the EEA yields $x$ with $a \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod m$. Normalize via $x \bmod m$.
- **Existence theorem:** $a^{-1} \pmod m$ exists *if and only if* $\gcd(a, m) = 1$.
- **RSA linkage:** $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$, computed via EEA. This is the **only efficient method** known.
- **Time complexity:** $O(\log(\min(a, b)))$ — polynomial in bit-length. Faster than brute force by a factor of $\sim 2^{n}$ for $n$-bit inputs.
- **Linear Diophantine solvability:** $ax + by = c$ has integer solutions iff $\gcd(a, b) \mid c$.
- **General Diophantine solution:** If $(x_0, y_0)$ is one solution, all solutions are $x = x_0 + \dfrac{b}{d} t,\ y = y_0 - \dfrac{a}{d} t$ where $d = \gcd(a, b)$.
- **CRT synergy:** EEA is a subroutine for the Chinese Remainder Theorem, used to **accelerate RSA decryption by ~4×**.
- **Sign discipline:** Bézout coefficients can be negative; modular inverses must be normalized to $[0, m-1]$.
- **Exam mantra:** "Euclidean chain → back-substitute → isolate $r_k$ → identify $(x, y)$ → verify by multiplication."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primitive Roots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Primitive Roots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **ring of integers modulo n**, denoted by $\mathbb{Z}_n$, a **Primitive Root** (also called a *generator* or *primitive element*) is formally defined as:

> An integer **g** is a **primitive root modulo n** if and only if the multiplicative order of **g** modulo **n** is exactly **$\phi(n)$** (Euler's Totient of n).

Equivalently, **g** is a primitive root modulo **n** if and only if the set of powers

$$\{g^1 \bmod n, \ g^2 \bmod n, \ g^3 \bmod n, \ \ldots, \ g^{\phi(n)} \bmod n\}$$

generates **every** element of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_n^* = \{a \in \mathbb{Z}_n \mid \gcd(a, n) = 1\}$.

$$\text{i.e.,} \quad \langle g \rangle = \mathbb{Z}_n^*$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Primitive roots form the **theoretical backbone** of classical cryptography — specifically the **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange**, **ElGamal Encryption**, and **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**. KTU examiners **frequently** test the existence theorem and counting of primitive roots.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The 12-Hour Clock and the Number 7:**
> Imagine a standard analog clock with only the hour markings (1 to 12). If you keep *adding* 7 hours repeatedly and record the position, you get the sequence: **7, 2, 9, 4, 11, 6, 1, 8, 3, 10, 5, 12, 7, 2, ...** — you cycle through **every single hour mark** before returning to 7. Hence, **7 is a primitive root modulo 13**.
>
> Now consider the number **2** modulo 13: 2, 4, 8, 3, 6, 12, 11, 9, 5, 10, 7, 1 — it also cycles through all 12 numbers! So **2 is also a primitive root modulo 13**.
>
> Primitive roots are therefore the "**multiplicative clocks**" of number theory — they tick through every invertible number before repeating.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$** — counts integers in $[1, n]$ coprime to $n$.
- **Order of element** $\text{ord}_n(g)$ — the smallest positive integer $k$ such that $g^k \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.
- **Generator / Primitive root** $g$ — element with $\text{ord}_n(g) = \phi(n)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Concept

Let us systematically dissect the primitive root concept into atomic logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Define the Multiplicative Group:**
  The set $\mathbb{Z}_n^* = \{a \in \{1, 2, \dots, n-1\} : \gcd(a, n) = 1\}$ forms a **finite abelian group** under multiplication modulo $n$. The order of this group is $\phi(n)$.

- **Step 2 — Define Element Order:**
  For any $g \in \mathbb{Z}_n^*$, its order $\text{ord}_n(g)$ divides $\phi(n)$ (a consequence of **Lagrange's Theorem**). So $\text{ord}_n(g) \in \{\phi(n)/d : d \mid \phi(n)\}$.

- **Step 3 — Identify Maximum Order Elements:**
  A primitive root is any element that achieves the **maximum possible order**, namely $\phi(n)$. No element can exceed this because the group has only $\phi(n)$ elements.

- **Step 4 — Existence Condition:**
  Primitive roots **exist if and only if** $n \in \{2, 4, p^k, 2p^k\}$ where $p$ is an **odd prime** and $k \geq 1$.

- **Step 5 — Count of Primitive Roots:**
  If primitive roots exist modulo $n$, their count is **$\phi(\phi(n))$** — a "totient of a totient" expression.

- **Step 6 — The "Why" — Cyclicity:**
  The presence of a primitive root makes $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ a **cyclic group**. Cyclic groups have predictable, elegant structure — this is precisely what makes them useful in cryptography (clean key-spaces, well-defined discrete logarithms).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Statement** | **Constraint / Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| Multiplicative Group | $\mathbb{Z}_n^* = \{a : 1 \leq a < n, \gcd(a,n) = 1\}$ | $n \geq 2$ |
| Group Order | $\vert \mathbb{Z}_n^* \vert = \phi(n)$ | Always |
| Element Order | $\text{ord}_n(g) = \min\{k > 0 : g^k \equiv 1 \pmod{n}\}$ | $g \in \mathbb{Z}_n^*$ |
| Primitive Root Condition | $\text{ord}_n(g) = \phi(n)$ | Definition |
| Euler's Theorem | $g^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | $\gcd(g, n) = 1$ |
| Fermat's Little Theorem | $g^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | $p$ prime, $p \nmid g$ |
| Existence Theorem | $n = 2, 4, p^k,$ or $2p^k$ | $p$ odd prime, $k \geq 1$ |
| Count of Primitive Roots | $N = \phi(\phi(n))$ | Only when existence holds |
| Powers Preserving Primitivity | $g^k$ is primitive root $\iff \gcd(k, \phi(n)) = 1$ | $g$ already primitive |
| Order Divides Group Order | $\text{ord}_n(g) \mid \phi(n)$ | Lagrange's Theorem |
| Verification Test (Prime Modulus) | $g$ primitive mod $p \iff g^{\phi(p)/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ for all primes $q \mid \phi(p)$ | $p$ prime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** In the verification test for prime modulus $p$, $\phi(p) = p-1$. The test reduces to checking every distinct prime factor $q$ of $p-1$. This is the **most commonly asked derivation** in KTU Module 1!

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange:** Alice and Bob agree on a prime $p$ and primitive root $g$. The security rests on the *Discrete Logarithm Problem* (DLP) — given $g^a \bmod p$, computing $a$ is computationally infeasible for large $p$.
- **ElGamal Encryption:** Public-key cryptosystem relying on the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ generated by a primitive root.
- **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA):** Uses primitive roots of primes to define the signature verification equation.
- **Random Number Generation:** Linear congruential generators $\;X_{n+1} = (aX_n + c) \bmod m\;$ achieve **full period** $\phi(m)$ only when $a$ is a primitive root (Hull-Dobell Theorem).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Primitive Roots = $\phi(\phi(n))$

**Statement:** If $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ is cyclic (i.e., primitive roots exist), then the number of distinct primitive roots is exactly $\phi(\phi(n))$.

**Proof:**

Let $g$ be a primitive root modulo $n$. Then every element of $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ can be written as $g^k$ for some $1 \leq k \leq \phi(n)$.

Now consider a power $g^k$. We claim that $g^k$ is a primitive root **if and only if** $\gcd(k, \phi(n)) = 1$.

**($\Rightarrow$) Necessity:** Suppose $g^k$ is a primitive root. Its order is $\text{ord}_n(g^k)$. Using the order-multiplication identity:

$$\text{ord}_n(g^k) = \frac{\text{ord}_n(g)}{\gcd(\text{ord}_n(g), k)} = \frac{\phi(n)}{\gcd(\phi(n), k)}$$

For $g^k$ to be primitive, $\text{ord}_n(g^k) = \phi(n)$, which forces:

$$\phi(n) = \frac{\phi(n)}{\gcd(\phi(n), k)} \implies \gcd(\phi(n), k) = 1$$

**($\Leftarrow$) Sufficiency:** Conversely, if $\gcd(\phi(n), k) = 1$, then the order formula gives $\text{ord}_n(g^k) = \phi(n)$, so $g^k$ is primitive.

**Conclusion:** Primitive roots are in **bijection** with integers in $\{1, 2, \dots, \phi(n)\}$ that are coprime to $\phi(n)$. The count of such integers is precisely **$\phi(\phi(n))$**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Verification Test for Primitive Roots Modulo Prime $p$

**Statement:** Let $p$ be a prime and $g \in \mathbb{Z}_p^*$. Then $g$ is a primitive root modulo $p$ if and only if:

$$g^{(p-1)/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$

for **every prime divisor** $q$ of $p-1$.

**Proof:**

**($\Leftarrow$) Sufficiency:** Suppose $g^{(p-1)/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod p$ for all primes $q \mid (p-1)$. Let $d = \text{ord}_p(g)$. By Fermat's Little Theorem, $g^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, so $d \mid (p-1)$.

Suppose for contradiction that $d < p-1$. Then there exists some prime $q$ such that $q \mid (p-1)/d$, meaning $d \mid (p-1)/q$ but $d \nmid (p-1)/q^2$... we can write $p-1 = d \cdot m$ for some integer $m > 1$. Let $q$ be any prime divisor of $m$. Then:

$$g^{(p-1)/q} = g^{d \cdot (m/q)} = \left(g^d\right)^{m/q} \equiv 1^{m/q} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$

This contradicts our hypothesis. Therefore $d = p-1 = \phi(p)$, making $g$ a primitive root.

**($\Rightarrow$) Necessity:** Suppose $g$ is a primitive root, so $\text{ord}_p(g) = p-1$. For any prime $q \mid (p-1)$, we have $q < p-1$, so $(p-1)/q$ is not a multiple of $p-1$. Since $d = p-1$ and $(p-1)/q < p-1$, we cannot have $g^{(p-1)/q} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ (otherwise $d$ would divide $(p-1)/q$, contradicting that $d = p-1$ is the *smallest* exponent). $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** Find all primitive roots modulo 11.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $\phi(11) = 10$.

Step 2 — Find prime factorization: $10 = 2 \times 5$. Prime divisors are $\{2, 5\}$.

Step 3 — Test candidate $g = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{10/2} \bmod 11 &= 2^{5} \bmod 11 = 32 \bmod 11 = 10 \not\equiv 1 \pmod{11} \\
2^{10/5} \bmod 11 &= 2^{2} \bmod 11 = 4 \not\equiv 1 \pmod{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both tests pass. So **2 is a primitive root modulo 11**.

Step 4 — Generate all primitive roots using the rule $g^k$ is primitive iff $\gcd(k, 10) = 1$. Integers $k \in \{1, \dots, 10\}$ with $\gcd(k, 10) = 1$ are: $k \in \{1, 3, 7, 9\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^1 \bmod 11 &= 2 \\
2^3 \bmod 11 &= 8 \\
2^7 \bmod 11 &= 128 \bmod 11 = 7 \\
2^9 \bmod 11 &= 512 \bmod 11 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Verify count: $\phi(\phi(11)) = \phi(10) = 4$. ✓ We found exactly **4 primitive roots**: $\{2, 6, 7, 8\}$.

### 3.4 The "Order Test" Algorithm (Mathematical Form, Non-Programmatic)

To find all primitive roots modulo $n$:

1. Compute $\phi(n)$.
2. Factor $\phi(n)$ into distinct primes: $\phi(n) = q_1^{a_1} q_2^{a_2} \cdots q_r^{a_r}$.
3. For each candidate $g \in \{2, 3, \dots, n-1\}$ with $\gcd(g, n) = 1$:
   - For each prime $q_i$, compute $g^{\phi(n)/q_i} \bmod n$.
   - If **any** result equals 1, discard $g$ (not primitive).
   - If **all** results are $\neq 1$, accept $g$ as primitive.
4. Total number of primitive roots will equal $\phi(\phi(n))$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Define a primitive root modulo n. State the necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of primitive roots.*

**Model Answer:**

A primitive root modulo $n$ is an integer $g$ such that the multiplicative order of $g$ modulo $n$ is $\phi(n)$. Equivalently, every element coprime to $n$ can be expressed as some power $g^k \bmod n$.

**Existence condition:** Primitive roots exist modulo $n$ if and only if $n \in \{2, 4, p^k, 2p^k\}$, where $p$ is an odd prime and $k \geq 1$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*If $g$ is a primitive root modulo 19, find the number of primitive roots modulo 19.*

**Model Answer:**

Since 19 is prime, $\phi(19) = 18$. Number of primitive roots $= \phi(\phi(19)) = \phi(18)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
18 &= 2 \times 3^2 \\
\phi(18) &= 18 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) \\
&= 18 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, there are **6 primitive roots modulo 19**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** *State and prove that the number of primitive roots modulo $n$ (when they exist) is $\phi(\phi(n))$.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** If the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ is cyclic, then the count of generators (primitive roots) is $\phi(\phi(n))$.

**Proof:**

Let $g$ be a primitive root mod $n$. Then $\mathbb{Z}_n^* = \{g^1, g^2, \ldots, g^{\phi(n)}\}$ (all distinct modulo $n$).

**Claim:** $g^k$ is a primitive root if and only if $\gcd(k, \phi(n)) = 1$.

**($\Rightarrow$)** Suppose $g^k$ is primitive. Let $d = \text{ord}_n(g^k)$. By the order power rule:

$$d = \frac{\text{ord}_n(g)}{\gcd(\text{ord}_n(g), k)} = \frac{\phi(n)}{\gcd(\phi(n), k)}$$

Since $g^k$ is primitive, $d = \phi(n)$. Hence $\gcd(\phi(n), k) = 1$.

**($\Leftarrow$)** Conversely, if $\gcd(\phi(n), k) = 1$, then $d = \phi(n)$, so $g^k$ is primitive.

The set of primitive roots is thus $\{g^k : 1 \leq k \leq \phi(n), \gcd(k, \phi(n)) = 1\}$.

The number of such $k$ is precisely $\phi(\phi(n))$. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** *Find all primitive roots modulo 13. Verify your answer.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — $\phi(13) = 12$.

Step 2 — Factor $12 = 2^2 \times 3$. Distinct prime factors: $\{2, 3\}$.

Step 3 — Test $g = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{12/2} \bmod 13 &= 2^6 = 64 \bmod 13 = 64 - 4(13) = 64 - 52 = 12 \not\equiv 1 \\
2^{12/3} \bmod 13 &= 2^4 = 16 \bmod 13 = 3 \not\equiv 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Both pass. So **2 is a primitive root mod 13**.

Step 4 — Generate all primitive roots. Integers $k \in \{1, \ldots, 12\}$ with $\gcd(k, 12) = 1$: $\{1, 5, 7, 11\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^1 \bmod 13 &= 2 \\
2^5 \bmod 13 &= 32 \bmod 13 = 6 \\
2^7 \bmod 13 &= 128 \bmod 13 = 9 \\
2^{11} \bmod 13 &= 2048 \bmod 13 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Verification: Expected count $= \phi(12) = 12 \cdot (1 - 1/2) \cdot (1 - 1/3) = 4$. ✓

**Primitive roots modulo 13: $\{2, 6, 9, 11\}$.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]*

**(a)** *Explain the verification test for primitive roots modulo a prime $p$. State and prove the theorem with an example.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theorem:** Let $p$ be prime. An element $g \in \mathbb{Z}_p^*$ is a primitive root mod $p$ if and only if:

$$g^{(p-1)/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{p} \quad \text{for every prime divisor } q \text{ of } p-1$$

**Proof of Sufficiency:** Suppose the condition holds. Let $d = \text{ord}_p(g)$. By Fermat's theorem, $d \mid (p-1)$. Assume $d < p-1$. Then $p - 1 = d \cdot m$ with $m > 1$. Let $q$ be a prime divisor of $m$. Then $(p-1)/q = d \cdot (m/q)$, so:

$$g^{(p-1)/q} = \left(g^d\right)^{m/q} \equiv 1^{m/q} = 1 \pmod{p}$$

Contradiction. Therefore $d = p-1$ and $g$ is primitive.

**Proof of Necessity:** If $g$ is primitive, $d = p-1$. For any prime $q \mid (p-1)$, $(p-1)/q < p-1 = d$. Since $d$ is the *smallest* positive exponent with $g^d \equiv 1$, the smaller exponent $(p-1)/q$ cannot yield 1.

**Example (verification for $g = 3$, mod 7):**
$\phi(7) = 6 = 2 \times 3$. Test:
- $3^{6/2} \bmod 7 = 3^3 = 27 \bmod 7 = 6 \neq 1$ ✓
- $3^{6/3} \bmod 7 = 3^2 = 9 \bmod 7 = 2 \neq 1$ ✓

So 3 is a primitive root mod 7. ✓

---

**(b)** *Demonstrate with a worked example how primitive roots are used in the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange protocol.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Protocol Overview:**

1. **Public Parameters:** Alice and Bob publicly agree on a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$.
2. **Private Keys:** Alice picks secret integer $a$; Bob picks secret integer $b$.
3. **Public Transmission:** Alice sends $A = g^a \bmod p$; Bob sends $B = g^b \bmod p$.
4. **Shared Secret Computation:**
   - Alice computes $K_A = B^a \bmod p$
   - Bob computes $K_B = A^b \bmod p$
   - Both equal $g^{ab} \bmod p$.

**Worked Example with $p = 23$, $g = 5$ (a primitive root):**

Step 1 — Verify $g = 5$ is primitive mod 23. $\phi(23) = 22 = 2 \times 11$.
- $5^{22/2} = 5^{11} \bmod 23$: $5^2=25=2$, $5^4=4$, $5^8=16$, $5^{11}=5^8 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5 = 16 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 160 \bmod 23 = 160 - 6(23) = 160-138=22 \neq 1$ ✓
- $5^{22/11} = 5^2 = 25 \bmod 23 = 2 \neq 1$ ✓

Step 2 — Alice chooses $a = 6$, Bob chooses $b = 15$.

Step 3 — Alice computes $A = 5^6 \bmod 23$:
$5^2 = 2$, $5^4 = 4$, $5^6 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \bmod 23$. So $A = 8$.

Step 4 — Bob computes $B = 5^{15} \bmod 23$:
$5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5 = 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 640 \bmod 23 = 640 - 27(23) = 640-621 = 19$. So $B = 19$.

Step 5 — Alice computes $K_A = B^a = 19^6 \bmod 23$:
$19^2 = 361 \bmod 23 = 361 - 15(23) = 16$
$19^4 = 16^2 = 256 \bmod 23 = 256 - 11(23) = 3$
$19^6 = 19^4 \cdot 19^2 = 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \bmod 23 = 2$

Step 6 — Bob computes $K_B = A^b = 8^{15} \bmod 23$:
$8^2 = 64 \bmod 23 = 18$
$8^4 = 18^2 = 324 \bmod 23 = 324 - 14(23) = 2$
$8^8 = 2^2 = 4$
$8^{15} = 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8 = 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 = 1152 \bmod 23 = 1152 - 50(23) = 2$ ✓

**Shared Secret: $K = 2$**. Both parties agree! Eve, observing only $A = 8$ and $B = 19$, must solve the Discrete Logarithm Problem — infeasible for large primes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 1 — Primitive Roots):**
> 1. **Forgetting to factor $\phi(n)$ completely** — students often only factor once, missing repeated primes like $p^2$ factors. Always factor down to distinct primes for the verification test.
> 2. **Off-by-one in exponent** — the test uses $(p-1)/q$, NOT $p-1$ divided by $\phi(p)$. Remember $\phi(p) = p-1$ for prime $p$.
> 3. **Confusion between "order" and "primitive root"** — every element has an order, but only elements with order equal to $\phi(n)$ qualify as primitive.
> 4. **Ignoring the existence theorem** — many students try to find primitive roots mod $n = 15$ or $n = 16$, not realizing they don't exist! Always verify existence first.
> 5. **In Diffie-Hellman computations** — students skip modular reductions at intermediate steps, leading to enormous numbers. Reduce *after every multiplication*.
> 6. **Misapplying the formula $\phi(\phi(n))$** — this gives the *count* of primitive roots, not the *list*. Students who write "the primitive roots are $\phi(\phi(n))$" lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Primitive Roots:**
> - **Definition:** $g$ is primitive mod $n$ iff $\text{ord}_n(g) = \phi(n)$.
> - **Existence:** $n \in \{2, 4, p^k, 2p^k\}$, $p$ odd prime, $k \geq 1$.
> - **Count:** $\phi(\phi(n))$ primitive roots when they exist.
> - **Generator test:** $g^k$ primitive $\iff \gcd(k, \phi(n)) = 1$.
> - **Prime modulus verification:** $g^{(p-1)/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod p$ for all primes $q \mid (p-1)$.
> - **Key property:** $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ is cyclic $\iff$ primitive roots exist.
> - **Cryptographic link:** Foundation of Diffie-Hellman, ElGamal, DSA, and all DLP-based schemes.
> - **Order rule:** $\text{ord}_n(g^k) = \frac{\text{ord}_n(g)}{\gcd(\text{ord}_n(g), k)}$.
> - **Lagrange constraint:** $\text{ord}_n(g) \mid \phi(n)$ always.
> - **Quick examples:** mod 7: $\{3, 5\}$; mod 11: $\{2, 6, 7, 8\}$; mod 13: $\{2, 6, 9, 11\}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Existence of Primitive Roots for Primes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primitive Root Modulo a Prime (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> Let $p$ be a prime number. An integer $g$ is called a **primitive root modulo $p$** if the multiplicative order of $g$ modulo $p$ is exactly $p-1$. Equivalently, the set
> $$\{g^1 \bmod p,\; g^2 \bmod p,\; g^3 \bmod p,\; \ldots,\; g^{p-1} \bmod p\}$$
> generates every nonzero residue class modulo $p$ exactly once. The element $g$ is therefore called a **generator** of the cyclic group $(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^{*}$.

Formally, in group-theoretic language, $g$ is a primitive root mod $p$ iff:

$$\text{ord}_p(g) = \varphi(p) = p - 1$$

where $\varphi$ denotes **Euler's totient function**.

### 1.2 The Existence Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Theorem for Primes (Gauss, 1801):**
> For **every prime** $p$, there exists at least one primitive root modulo $p$. Moreover, the *exact* number of primitive roots modulo $p$ is given by $\varphi(p-1)$.

This is a profound structural statement — it guarantees that the multiplicative group of a prime field is **always cyclic**, a fact that underpins modern public-key cryptography (Diffie–Hellman, ElGamal, DSA).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Clock-Strike Conductor":**
> Imagine a clock with $p-1$ distinct chime positions (since modulo a prime $p$, there are exactly $p-1$ units: $\{1, 2, \ldots, p-1\}$). A primitive root $g$ is like a **conductor** who, by repeatedly striking the bell, hits *every single chime position exactly once* before returning to silence after $p-1$ strikes.
>
> A *non-primitive* element, by contrast, would only cycle through a smaller subset of chimes (its order is a proper divisor of $p-1$). For example, modulo $7$, the element $2$ only hits $\{1, 2, 4\}$ — never reaching $3, 5,$ or $6$ — because $\text{ord}_7(2) = 3 < 6$.

### 1.4 Why This Matters in Cryptography

| Application | Role of Primitive Root |
|---|---|
| **Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange** | A primitive root $g$ of a large prime $p$ is the public base |
| **ElGamal Encryption** | Security reduces to the Discrete Logarithm Problem in $\langle g \rangle = \mathbb{Z}_p^*$ |
| **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)** | Uses a prime $q$ dividing $p-1$; subgroup of size $q$ is cyclic |
| **Primitive Polynomials** | Used in stream ciphers (LFSRs) — algebraic counterpart of the same idea |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Background

The existence proof rests on three pillars of elementary number theory:

1. **Lagrange's Theorem:** In any finite group $G$, the order of every element divides $\vert G \vert$.
   - Applied to $G = \mathbb{Z}_p^*$ (a finite group of order $p-1$), it follows that for any $a \in \mathbb{Z}_p^*$, $\text{ord}_p(a) \mid (p-1)$.

2. **Theorems on Polynomial Congruences:** A congruence $f(x) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$ of degree $n$ has at most $n$ solutions modulo a prime $p$.

3. **Factorization of Cyclic Subgroups:** Any finite subgroup of $(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*$ generated by a single element $g$ is cyclic of order $\text{ord}_p(g)$.

### 2.2 The Master Lemma — Test for Primitive Root

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lemma (Primitive Root Test):**
> Let $p$ be a prime and let $p-1$ have the prime factorization
> $$p - 1 = q_1^{a_1} \cdot q_2^{a_2} \cdots q_k^{a_k}$$
> where $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_k$ are distinct primes. Then $g$ is a primitive root modulo $p$ **if and only if**
> $$g^{(p-1)/q_i} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{p} \quad \text{for every } i = 1, 2, \ldots, k.$$

*Intuition:* The element $g^{(p-1)/q_i}$ tests whether $g$'s order avoids being trapped inside the proper subgroup of index $q_i$. If $g$ survives *all* such tests, it must have maximal order $p-1$.

### 2.3 Counting Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Number of Primitive Roots Modulo $p$:**
> The number of distinct primitive roots modulo a prime $p$ is exactly
> $$\boxed{\;N(p) = \varphi(p-1)\;}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table (avoid using $\vert$ inside cells):**

| # | Formula / Statement | Symbol / Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{ord}_p(g) = p-1$ | $g$ is a primitive root mod $p$ | Definition |
| 2 | $N(p) = \varphi(p-1)$ | Total primitive roots mod $p$ | Counting |
| 3 | $g^{(p-1)/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | $g$ survives prime-divisor test | Test condition |
| 4 | $\sum_{d \,\mid\, n} \varphi(d) = n$ | Divisor-sum identity | Used in proof |
| 5 | $\varphi(p) = p-1$ | For prime $p$ | Base identity |
| 6 | $\varphi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Distinct primes $p, q$ | Examples |
| 7 | $\lambda(p) = p-1$ | Carmichael fn for prime | Subgroup orders |
| 8 | $\mathbb{Z}_p^* \cong \mathbb{Z}_{p-1}$ | Cyclic group iso. | Group theory |

> **Boundary/Edge Cases:** For $p = 2$, the only nonzero residue is $1$, so $1$ is trivially a primitive root. For $p = 3$, $p-1 = 2$, $\varphi(2) = 1$, so there is exactly $1$ primitive root, namely $g = 2$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern cryptographic engineering, primitive roots underpin:

- **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP):** Security of DH, ElGamal relies on the *hardness* of finding $x$ in $g^x \equiv h \pmod{p}$, where $g$ is a public primitive root.
- **Subgroup attacks:** Engineers must ensure that $p-1$ has a **large prime factor** so that no smooth-order subgroup permits Pohlig–Hellman reduction.
- **Random number generation:** Primitive polynomials over $\text{GF}(2)$ (algebraic analogues) drive stream ciphers in hardware security modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Proof of Existence for Primes

We prove: *Every prime $p$ has at least one primitive root.*

#### Step 1 — Setup the divisor-sum identity.

For each divisor $d$ of $p-1$, let $\psi(d)$ denote the number of elements $a \in \mathbb{Z}_p^*$ with $\text{ord}_p(a) = d$.

Because $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ is a finite abelian group, by Lagrange's theorem every element has order dividing $p-1$. The number of elements of order *exactly* $d$ is **zero** unless $d \mid (p-1)$. Counting all $p-1$ elements by their order:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{d \,\mid\, (p-1)} \psi(d) &= p - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Generate a cyclic subgroup of order $d$.

If there exists an element $a$ with $\text{ord}_p(a) = d$, then the powers $\{a, a^2, \ldots, a^d\}$ form a cyclic subgroup of order $d$. All elements of order dividing $d$ lie in this subgroup, and **the elements of order exactly $d$ are precisely the generators of this subgroup**. The number of generators of a cyclic group of order $d$ is $\varphi(d)$.

Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi(d) &= 0 \quad \text{if } d \nmid (p-1) \\
\psi(d) &= \varphi(d) \quad \text{if } d \mid (p-1) \text{ and } \psi(d) > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

We must still show $\psi(d) > 0$ for each $d \mid (p-1)$.

#### Step 3 — Show $\psi(d) > 0$ for every divisor $d$ of $p-1$.

Suppose, for contradiction, that there exists some $d^* \mid (p-1)$ with $\psi(d^*) = 0$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p - 1 &= \sum_{d \,\mid\, (p-1)} \psi(d) \\
      &= \sum_{\substack{d \,\mid\, (p-1) \\ d \neq d^*}} \psi(d) \\
      &\leq \sum_{\substack{d \,\mid\, (p-1) \\ d \neq d^*}} \varphi(d)
\end{aligned}
$$

We use the classical number-theoretic identity $\sum_{d \,\mid\, n} \varphi(d) = n$, which gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{\substack{d \,\mid\, (p-1) \\ d \neq d^*}} \varphi(d) &= (p - 1) - \varphi(d^*) < p - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a contradiction because the left side equals $p-1$ and the right side is strictly smaller. Therefore $\psi(d) > 0$ for all divisors $d$ of $p-1$.

#### Step 4 — Conclude existence.

Taking $d = p-1$ itself, we get $\psi(p-1) = \varphi(p-1) > 0$. So at least one element of order $p-1$ exists. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Primitive Roots Modulo 7

**Given:** $p = 7$, so $p-1 = 6 = 2 \cdot 3$. Prime divisors are $q_1 = 2$, $q_2 = 3$.

**Test $g = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{(p-1)/2} &= 2^{3} = 8 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the test condition fails (gives $1 \pmod 7$), $g = 2$ is **not** a primitive root. Indeed, $\text{ord}_7(2) = 3$.

**Test $g = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^{(p-1)/2} &= 3^{3} = 27 \equiv 27 - 3 \cdot 7 = 27 - 21 = 6 \pmod{7} \neq 1 \quad \checkmark \\
3^{(p-1)/3} &= 3^{2} = 9 \equiv 9 - 1 \cdot 7 = 2 \pmod{7} \neq 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Both conditions satisfied → $g = 3$ is a **primitive root** mod $7$.

**Verify by listing powers:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^1 &\equiv 3 \pmod{7} \\
3^2 &\equiv 2 \pmod{7} \\
3^3 &\equiv 6 \pmod{7} \\
3^4 &\equiv 4 \pmod{7} \\
3^5 &\equiv 5 \pmod{7} \\
3^6 &\equiv 1 \pmod{7}
\end{aligned}
$$

All residues $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ appear → confirmed.

**Count primitive roots:** $\varphi(6) = \varphi(2)\varphi(3) = 1 \cdot 2 = 2$. The two primitive roots are $3$ and $5$.

**Verify $g = 5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^1 &\equiv 5,\quad 5^2 \equiv 4,\quad 5^3 \equiv 6,\quad 5^4 \equiv 2,\quad 5^5 \equiv 3,\quad 5^6 \equiv 1 \pmod{7} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Prime Factorizations & Primitive Root Counts

| Prime $p$ | $p-1$ | Prime Factorization | $\varphi(p-1)$ | Smallest Primitive Root |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1 | (empty) | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 2 | $2$ | 1 | 2 |
| 5 | 4 | $2^2$ | 2 | 2 |
| 7 | 6 | $2 \cdot 3$ | 2 | 3 |
| 11 | 10 | $2 \cdot 5$ | 4 | 2 |
| 13 | 12 | $2^2 \cdot 3$ | 4 | 2 |
| 17 | 16 | $2^4$ | 8 | 3 |
| 19 | 18 | $2 \cdot 3^2$ | 6 | 2 |
| 23 | 22 | $2 \cdot 11$ | 10 | 5 |
| 29 | 28 | $2^2 \cdot 7$ | 12 | 2 |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Testing $g = 2$ modulo $p = 41$

Here $p - 1 = 40 = 2^3 \cdot 5$. Distinct prime divisors are $\{2, 5\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{(p-1)/2} &= 2^{20} \pmod{41} \\
2^{10} &= 1024 = 24 \cdot 41 + 40 \equiv 40 \equiv -1 \pmod{41} \\
2^{20} &\equiv (-1)^2 = 1 \pmod{41}
\end{aligned}
$$

Test fails at $q = 2$ → $g = 2$ is **not** a primitive root mod $41$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture Flow — Existence Proof Logic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Prime p given"] --> B["Compute p-1"]
    B --> C["Factorize p-1 = q1^a1 q2^a2 ... qk^ak"]
    C --> D["Choose candidate generator g in 1..p-1"]
    D --> E["For each prime divisor qi of p-1"]
    E --> F["Compute g^((p-1)/qi) mod p"]
    F --> G{"Result == 1 mod p?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Reject g: not primitive root"]
    H --> I["Pick next candidate g"]
    I --> D
    G -- "No" --> J{"All qi checked?"}
    J -- "No" --> E
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Accept g as primitive root"]
    K --> L["Total primitive roots = phi(p-1)"]
    L --> M["End"]
```

### 4.2 Group-Theoretic Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    field["Field GF(p) = Z/pZ"]
    field --> mult["Multiplicative Group Z_p*"]
    mult -->|"order p-1"| cyclic["Cyclic Group (isomorphic to Z_{p-1})"]
    cyclic -->|"generated by"| primroot["Primitive Root g"]
    cyclic -->|"generated by"| subgroups["Proper Subgroups"]
    subgroups -->|"order d | p-1"| sub["Cyclic group of order d"]
    primroot -->|"powers g^1 ... g^(p-1)"| allresidues["All nonzero residues 1..p-1"]
    sub -->|"powers"| partialset["Subset of residues (proper divisor)"]
```

### 4.3 Example: Power Cycle Visualization for $g = 3$ mod $7$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["3^1 = 3"] --> E2["3^2 = 2"]
    E2 --> E3["3^3 = 6"]
    E3 --> E4["3^4 = 4"]
    E4 --> E5["3^5 = 5"]
    E5 --> E6["3^6 = 1"]
    E6 -.->|"cycle of order 6"| E1
```

### 4.4 Cryptographic Mapping Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CRYPTO["Public-Key Cryptosystems"]
    CRYPTO --> DH["Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange"]
    CRYPTO --> ELG["ElGamal Encryption"]
    CRYPTO --> DSA["Digital Signature Algorithm"]
    DH --> DLP["Discrete Logarithm Problem"]
    ELG --> DLP
    DSA --> DLP
    DLP --> PRIM["Primitive Root g mod p"]
    PRIM --> CYCLIC["Cyclic Group Z_p*"]
    CYCLIC --> ORD["Maximal Order = p-1"]
```
