## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define a primitive root modulo a prime $p$. Show that $g = 3$ is a primitive root modulo $7$.

**Model Answer:**

A primitive root modulo a prime $p$ is an integer $g$ such that $\text{ord}_p(g) = p-1$, i.e., the powers of $g$ generate all nonzero residues mod $p$.

For $g = 3$, $p = 7$, $p-1 = 6 = 2 \cdot 3$. Test:

- $3^{6/2} = 3^3 = 27 \equiv 6 \pmod{7} \neq 1 \checkmark$
- $3^{6/3} = 3^2 = 9 \equiv 2 \pmod{7} \neq 1 \checkmark$

Both conditions satisfied, so $3$ is a primitive root mod $7$. Powers: $3, 2, 6, 4, 5, 1$ — all six nonzero residues appear. $\blacksquare$

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** State the theorem on the number of primitive roots modulo a prime $p$. Compute the number of primitive roots mod $23$.

**Model Answer:**

**Theorem:** The number of primitive roots modulo a prime $p$ is $\varphi(p-1)$.

For $p = 23$: $p-1 = 22 = 2 \cdot 11$.

$$
\varphi(22) = \varphi(2) \cdot \varphi(11) = 1 \cdot 10 = 10
$$

Hence there are exactly **10 primitive roots** modulo 23. $\blacksquare$

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** skip the prime factorization step of $p-1$ — valuators deduct 2 marks if it is missing.
> - **Do NOT** confuse $\varphi(p)$ with $\varphi(p-1)$. The first counts nonzero residues mod $p$; the second counts primitive roots.
> - **Always state** that $g \in \{1, 2, \ldots, p-1\}$ before testing, to avoid negative residue ambiguity.
> - **Always verify** by listing all powers if the marks scheme explicitly asks for it.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and prove the existence theorem for primitive roots modulo a prime $p$. Hence show that $5$ is a primitive root modulo $13$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Find all primitive roots modulo $13$. Verify your answer by listing the powers of each.

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Theorem:** For every prime $p$, there exists an integer $g$ with $\text{ord}_p(g) = p-1$, called a primitive root. The number of primitive roots is $\varphi(p-1)$.

**Proof Sketch:**

Let $d$ run over divisors of $p-1$. Define $\psi(d) = $ number of elements in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ of order exactly $d$. If $a$ has order $d$, the subgroup $\langle a \rangle$ has $\varphi(d)$ generators, all of order $d$. Hence either $\psi(d) = 0$ or $\psi(d) = \varphi(d)$.

Total count:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{d \,\mid\, (p-1)} \psi(d) = p-1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sum_{d \,\mid\, (p-1)} \varphi(d) = p-1$ and each term in the first sum is $\leq$ the corresponding term in the second, we must have $\psi(d) = \varphi(d)$ for all $d \mid (p-1)$. In particular $\psi(p-1) = \varphi(p-1) \geq 1$, establishing existence. $\blacksquare$

**Show $5$ is primitive root mod $13$:**

$p - 1 = 12 = 2^2 \cdot 3$. Distinct prime divisors: $\{2, 3\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{12/2} = 5^6 &\equiv ? \pmod{13} \\
5^2 = 25 &\equiv 12 \equiv -1 \pmod{13} \\
5^4 \equiv (-1)^2 &= 1 \pmod{13} \\
5^6 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 &\equiv 1 \cdot (-1) = -1 \equiv 12 \pmod{13} \neq 1 \quad \checkmark \\
5^{12/3} = 5^4 &\equiv 1 \pmod{13} \quad \text{Wait — this equals 1!}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction — recompute carefully:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^1 &\equiv 5 \pmod{13} \\
5^2 &\equiv 25 - 13 = 12 \equiv -1 \pmod{13} \\
5^3 &\equiv 5 \cdot (-1) = -5 \equiv 8 \pmod{13} \\
5^4 &\equiv 5 \cdot 8 = 40 \equiv 40 - 3(13) = 1 \pmod{13}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $5^4 \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$. Hence $5^{12/3} = 5^4 \equiv 1$, which **fails** the test condition. Therefore $g = 5$ is **NOT** a primitive root mod $13$; its order is $4$.

*Note for students:* Always recompute the powers carefully — the question's premise can be a trap, and identifying the falsehood is itself a valid answer.

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

$\varphi(12) = \varphi(4)\varphi(3) = 2 \cdot 2 = 4$. So there are 4 primitive roots.

Test candidates in order:

**$g = 2$:** $p-1 = 12$, primes $\{2, 3\}$.

$$
2^{6} = 64 = 4(13) + 12 \equiv 12 \equiv -1 \pmod{13} \neq 1 \checkmark
$$

$$
2^{4} = 16 \equiv 3 \pmod{13} \neq 1 \checkmark
$$

So $2$ **is** a primitive root mod $13$. Powers: $2, 4, 8, 3, 6, 12, 11, 9, 5, 10, 7, 1$ — all 12 nonzero residues appear. $\checkmark$

The other three primitive roots are obtained by raising $2$ to powers coprime to $12$: namely $k$ with $\gcd(k, 12) = 1$, i.e., $k \in \{1, 5, 7, 11\}$ (these are the elements of order 12 in $\mathbb{Z}_{12}^*$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^1 &\equiv 2 \pmod{13} \\
2^5 = 32 &\equiv 32 - 2(13) = 6 \pmod{13} \\
2^7 = 128 &\equiv 128 - 9(13) = 128 - 117 = 11 \pmod{13} \\
2^{11} = 2^7 \cdot 2^4 &\equiv 11 \cdot 3 = 33 \equiv 7 \pmod{13}
\end{aligned}
$$

**All four primitive roots mod $13$:** $\{2, 6, 7, 11\}$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] With necessary lemma, prove that for a prime $p$, the number of integers $g \in \{1, 2, \ldots, p-1\}$ that are primitive roots modulo $p$ is $\varphi(p-1)$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Determine all primitive roots modulo $19$. Show all computational steps.

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Lemma:** Let $p$ be prime. An integer $g$ with $1 \leq g \leq p-1$ is a primitive root mod $p$ if and only if for every prime divisor $q$ of $p-1$,
$$g^{(p-1)/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{p}.$$

*Proof of Lemma ($\Rightarrow$):* If $g$ is a primitive root, $\text{ord}_p(g) = p-1$. If $g^{(p-1)/q} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, then $(p-1)/q$ would be a multiple of $\text{ord}_p(g)$, which is impossible since $(p-1)/q < p-1$. Contradiction.

*Proof ($\Leftarrow$):* Let $d = \text{ord}_p(g)$. By Lagrange, $d \mid (p-1)$. Write $p-1 = d \cdot m$. If $d < p-1$, then $m > 1$ and $m$ has some prime divisor $q$. Then $(p-1)/q$ is a multiple of $d$, so $g^{(p-1)/q} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, contradicting the hypothesis. Hence $d = p-1$. $\blacksquare$

**Main Theorem:** The number of primitive roots mod $p$ equals $\varphi(p-1)$.

**Proof:** By the lemma, the primitive roots are exactly those $g$ such that for every prime $q \mid (p-1)$, the residue $g^{(p-1)/q} \neq 1 \pmod{p}$.

Group theory fact: in a cyclic group of order $n$, the number of generators is $\varphi(n)$. Since $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ is cyclic of order $p-1$ (proven by the existence theorem), the number of generators — i.e., primitive roots — is exactly $\varphi(p-1)$. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

$p = 19$, so $p-1 = 18 = 2 \cdot 3^2$. Distinct prime divisors: $\{2, 3\}$. Test candidates.

**Test $g = 2$:**

$$
2^{18/2} = 2^9 \pmod{19}
$$

Compute stepwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^1 &= 2 \\
2^2 &= 4 \\
2^4 &= 16 \equiv -3 \pmod{19} \\
2^8 &= (-3)^2 = 9 \pmod{19} \\
2^9 &= 2^8 \cdot 2 = 18 \equiv -1 \pmod{19} \neq 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
2^{18/3} = 2^6 = (2^3)^2 = 8^2 = 64 = 3(19) + 7 \equiv 7 \pmod{19} \neq 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

Both conditions satisfied → $g = 2$ **is a primitive root** mod $19$.

**Count:** $\varphi(18) = \varphi(2)\varphi(3^2) = 1 \cdot 6 = 6$.

**Generate all primitive roots:** Raise $2$ to powers $k$ with $\gcd(k, 18) = 1$, i.e., $k \in \{1, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^1 &\equiv 2 \pmod{19} \\
2^5 &\equiv 32 - 19 = 13 \pmod{19} \\
2^7 &\equiv 2^5 \cdot 2^2 = 13 \cdot 4 = 52 = 2(19) + 14 \equiv 14 \pmod{19} \\
2^{11} &\equiv 2^7 \cdot 2^4 = 14 \cdot 16 = 224 = 11(19) + 15 \equiv 15 \pmod{19} \\
2^{13} &\equiv 2^{11} \cdot 2^2 = 15 \cdot 4 = 60 = 3(19) + 3 \equiv 3 \pmod{19} \\
2^{17} &\equiv 2^{13} \cdot 2^4 = 3 \cdot 16 = 48 = 2(19) + 10 \equiv 10 \pmod{19}
\end{aligned}
$$

**All 6 primitive roots mod $19$:** $\{2, 3, 10, 13, 14, 15\}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** $g$ is a primitive root mod prime $p$ iff $\text{ord}_p(g) = p - 1$.
- **Existence:** Every prime has **at least one** primitive root (Gauss's theorem).
- **Count:** Exactly $\varphi(p-1)$ primitive roots modulo $p$.
- **Test Algorithm:** Factor $p-1 = q_1^{a_1} \cdots q_k^{a_k}$; check $g^{(p-1)/q_i} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ for each distinct prime $q_i$.
- **Group view:** $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ is a **cyclic group** of order $p-1$; primitive roots are its generators.
- **Generation trick:** Once one primitive root $g$ is found, all others are $g^k$ where $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$.
- **Edge cases:** $p = 2$: only primitive root is $1$. $p = 3$: only primitive root is $2$. $p = 5$: primitive roots are $\{2, 3\}$.
- **Crypto link:** DH, ElGamal, DSA all require a public primitive root $g$ of a large prime $p$.
- **Pitfall 1:** $\varphi(p)$ is NOT the count of primitive roots; $\varphi(p-1)$ is.
- **Pitfall 2:** Always test **every** prime divisor of $p-1$, not every divisor.
- **Pitfall 3:** Powers are computed mod $p$ — reduce intermediate results to keep numbers small.
- **Pitfall 4:** Negative residues like $-1$ are valid and unequal to $1 \pmod{p}$ (for $p > 2$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermat's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fermat's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (Fermat's Little Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fermat's Little Theorem (1640)**: Let $p$ be a **prime number** and let $a$ be any **integer** such that $\gcd(a, p) = 1$ (i.e., $p$ does **not** divide $a$). Then:
> $$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$
> An equivalent formulation valid for **every** integer $a$ (including multiples of $p$) is:
> $$a^{p} \equiv a \pmod{p}$$

This theorem is one of the foundational pillars of **public-key cryptography** (RSA, Diffie–Hellman, ElGamal) and **primality testing** algorithms. KTU examiners almost always refer to this as simply "Fermat's Theorem" in cryptography modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Prime Clock"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: A prime-numbered clock with `p` hour marks (0, 1, 2, …, p-1).**
>
> 1. Stand on mark `1` of a clock that has exactly **p** marks (where **p** is prime).
> 2. Take jumps of size **a** around the clock.
> 3. The order of the jumps (the size of the loop you trace) is **always** a divisor of **p − 1**.
> 4. Therefore, after exactly **p − 1** jumps, you are guaranteed to land back on mark `1`.
>
> This is *exactly* what $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ states: multiplying by `a` exactly `p−1` times brings you back to the starting remainder.

**Plain English Summary**: For any prime "modulus" $p$ and any number $a$ that is "coprime" to $p$, raising $a$ to the power $(p-1)$ leaves a remainder of **1** when divided by $p$. It is the modular equivalent of saying that "after completing a full prime cycle, you return to the origin."

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Terms

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Modulus (p)** | The prime number in Fermat's theorem. |
| **Base (a)** | The integer being exponentiated. |
| **Coprime** | $\gcd(a, p) = 1$ (no common factors with $p$). |
| **Euler's Totient φ(p)** | For prime $p$, $\phi(p) = p-1$. |
| **Carmichael Number** | Composite $n$ for which $a^{n-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ holds for *all* $a$ coprime to $n$. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why Fermat's Theorem Works

The theorem rests on three deep properties of **prime numbers** in modular arithmetic:

- **Property 1 — Closure under multiplication**: The set $\mathbb{Z}_p^* = \{1, 2, \ldots, p-1\}$ is closed under multiplication mod $p$ (the product of two non-zero residues mod a prime is still a non-zero residue).
- **Property 2 — Invertibility**: Every element of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ has a unique multiplicative inverse, so the set forms a **finite multiplicative group** of order $p-1$.
- **Property 3 — Lagrange's Theorem (Group Theory)**: The order of any element divides the order of the group. Since the group order is $p-1$, the order of any element $a$ divides $p-1$, meaning $a^{p-1} = e = 1$ in the group.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it fails for composite moduli**: The set $\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$ is **not** generally a group under multiplication mod $n$ when $n$ is composite. Elements can be **zero-divisors** (e.g., $2 \times 3 \equiv 0 \pmod{6}$), which destroys the group property. This is why the primality of $p$ is non-negotiable.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Core Fermat identity (requires $\gcd(a,p)=1$, $p$ prime) |
| 2 | $a^{p} \equiv a \pmod{p}$ | General form (works for **all** integers $a$) |
| 3 | $a^{k(p-1)} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Fermat exponent reduction: any multiple of $p-1$ collapses to 1 |
| 4 | $a^{x} \equiv a^{x \bmod (p-1)} \pmod{p}$ | Modular exponent reduction rule |
| 5 | $a^{-1} \equiv a^{p-2} \pmod{p}$ | Modular inverse (a critical tool in RSA key generation) |
| 6 | $\phi(p) = p - 1$ | Euler's totient of a prime — substitutes into Euler's theorem |
| 7 | Fermat Primality Test: $a^{n-1} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{n} \Rightarrow n$ is composite | Probabilistic primality testing |

> **Anti-Table-Break Rule Note**: All absolute-value and divisibility expressions are written using `\vert` instead of the raw `|` symbol to preserve KTU markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of Fermat's Theorem |
|---|---|
| **RSA Encryption** | Reduces $m^{ed} \bmod n$ to $m \bmod n$ using $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ — a direct generalization of Fermat. |
| **Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange** | Enables computation of discrete logarithms in prime-modulus groups. |
| **Digital Signatures (DSA, ElGamal)** | Modular exponentiation hardness assumption relies on Fermat-group structure. |
| **Hash Functions / Checksums** | Modular reduction using Fermat-style exponents. |
| **Fermat Primality Test** | Quick filter for primality in prime-generation algorithms (Miller–Rabin, AKS). |
| **Random Number Generation** | Blum-Blum-Shub PRNG uses $x_{n+1} = x_n^2 \bmod m$ with $m$ a product of two primes. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of Fermat's Little Theorem (Combinatorial Method)

We prove: If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.

**Step 1 — Define the residue set.**
Consider the set of non-zero residues modulo $p$:
$$S = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, p-1\}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply every element by $a$.**
Compute the set $T = \{a \cdot 1, a \cdot 2, \ldots, a \cdot (p-1)\} \bmod p$.

**Step 3 — Show that $T$ is a permutation of $S$.**
Since $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, multiplication by $a$ is an **invertible** operation in $\mathbb{Z}_p$. Therefore $T$ contains exactly $p-1$ distinct elements, all lying in $\{1, 2, \ldots, p-1\}$. Hence $T = S$.

**Step 4 — Take the product of all elements.**
$$\prod_{x \in T} x \equiv \prod_{x \in S} x \pmod{p}$$

This expands to:
$$a \cdot 1 \cdot a \cdot 2 \cdot a \cdot 3 \cdots a \cdot (p-1) \equiv 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdots (p-1) \pmod{p}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $a$ from the left-hand side.**
The left-hand side contains the factor $a$ exactly $(p-1)$ times:
$$a^{p-1} \cdot (1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdots (p-1)) \equiv (p-1)! \pmod{p}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $(p-1)!$ from both sides.**
Because $p$ is prime, $(p-1)!$ is **not divisible** by $p$ (none of its factors equal $p$), so $(p-1)!$ has a multiplicative inverse modulo $p$. Cancel both sides:
$$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Fermat Modular Reduction

**Problem**: Compute $5^{122} \bmod 11$ using Fermat's theorem.

**Step 1 — Identify the prime.**
Here $p = 11$ (prime) and $a = 5$. Verify $\gcd(5, 11) = 1$ ✓.

**Step 2 — Apply Fermat's theorem.**
Since $p = 11$, we have $p - 1 = 10$. By Fermat's theorem:
$$5^{10} \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$$

**Step 3 — Reduce the exponent modulo 10.**
We need $5^{122} \bmod 11$. Compute the exponent modulo 10:
$$122 = 12 \times 10 + 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 122 \equiv 2 \pmod{10}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the reduction rule.**
$$5^{122} = 5^{12 \cdot 10 + 2} = \left(5^{10}\right)^{12} \cdot 5^{2} \equiv 1^{12} \cdot 25 \pmod{11}$$

**Step 5 — Final modular reduction.**
$$25 \bmod 11 = 25 - 2 \cdot 11 = 25 - 22 = 3$$

$$\boxed{5^{122} \equiv 3 \pmod{11}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Fermat Primality Test

**Problem**: Test whether $n = 221$ is prime using base $a = 5$.

**Step 1 — Set up the test.**
Compute $5^{220} \bmod 221$. If the result is **not 1**, then $n = 221$ is **definitely composite**. If the result **is 1**, $n$ is *probably* prime.

**Step 2 — Reduce the exponent.**
$220 = 2 \cdot 110$, so:
$$5^{220} = \left(5^{110}\right)^2 \bmod 221$$

**Step 3 — Compute $5^{110} \bmod 221$.**
Split: $5^{110} = 5^{100} \cdot 5^{10}$.
- $5^{10} = 9765625$. Now $9765625 \bmod 221$: $221 \cdot 44187 = 9765327$, remainder = $9765625 - 9765327 = 298$. But $298 \bmod 221 = 77$. So $5^{10} \equiv 77 \pmod{221}$.
- Iterating the square-and-multiply method gives $5^{110} \equiv 168 \pmod{221}$ (intermediate steps omitted for brevity — KTU students may use repeated squaring).

**Step 4 — Square the result.**
$$5^{220} \equiv 168^2 \pmod{221}$$
$$168^2 = 28224$$
$$28224 \bmod 221: \quad 221 \cdot 127 = 28067, \quad 28224 - 28067 = 157$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**
$$5^{220} \equiv 157 \pmod{221} \neq 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: Since $5^{220} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{221}$, the number $n = 221$ is **definitely composite** (in fact, $221 = 13 \times 17$).

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Computing Modular Inverse

**Problem**: Find the inverse of $3$ modulo $7$ (i.e., $3^{-1} \bmod 7$).

**Step 1 — Apply Fermat's inverse formula.**
For prime $p = 7$, the inverse of $a = 3$ is $a^{p-2} = 3^5 \bmod 7$.

**Step 2 — Compute via repeated squaring.**
$$3^1 \equiv 3 \pmod{7}$$
$$3^2 = 9 \equiv 2 \pmod{7}$$
$$3^4 = (3^2)^2 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{7}$$
$$3^5 = 3^4 \cdot 3^1 \equiv 4 \cdot 3 = 12 \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$$

**Step 3 — Verify.**
$$3 \cdot 5 = 15 \equiv 1 \pmod{7} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{3^{-1} \equiv 5 \pmod{7}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Solutions

| Given | Use This | Resulting Identity |
|---|---|---|
| $a^{p-1} \bmod p$ with $p$ prime | Direct Fermat | $= 1$ |
| $a^{N} \bmod p$ with large $N$ | Reduce $N \bmod (p-1)$ | $a^{N \bmod (p-1)} \bmod p$ |
| Find $a^{-1} \bmod p$ | $a^{p-2} \bmod p$ | Modular inverse |
| Test $n$ for primality | Compute $a^{n-1} \bmod n$ | If $\neq 1$, composite |
| $a^{kp + r} \bmod p$ | Fermat: $a^{p} \equiv a$ | $a^{r} \bmod p$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Fermat's Theorem Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Prime p and Integer a] --> B{Check gcd of a and p}
    B -- gcd is 1 --> C[Apply Fermat Identity]
    B -- gcd is not 1 --> Z[Fermat Not Directly Applicable]
    C --> D[a raised to power p-1]
    D --> E[Compute modulo p]
    E --> F[Result equals 1 mod p]
    F --> G[Use as Primality Witness]
    G --> H{Test Result Equals 1}
    H -- Yes --> I[Number is Probably Prime]
    H -- No --> J[Number is Definitely Composite]
    I --> K[Watch out for Carmichael Numbers]
    K --> L[Use Miller Rabin Test for Confirmation]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Modular Exponent Reduction Using Fermat

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Large Exponent N] --> B[Compute N mod p minus 1]
    B --> C[Reduced Exponent r]
    C --> D[Compute a raised to r]
    D --> E[Take modulo p]
    E --> F[Final Result]
    style A fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: RSA Cryptography Pipeline (Using Fermat's Theorem)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Two Large Primes p and q] --> B[Compute n equals p times q]
    B --> C[Compute phi of n equals p minus 1 times q minus 1]
    C --> D[Choose Public Exponent e coprime to phi n]
    D --> E[Compute Private Exponent d = e inverse mod phi n]
    E --> F[Encryption: C = M raised to e mod n]
    F --> G[Decryption: M = C raised to d mod n]
    G --> H[Fermat Identity Restores Original Message]
    style H fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Fermat Primality Test Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Candidate Number n] --> B[Pick Random Base a between 2 and n minus 2]
    B --> C[Compute a raised to n minus 1 mod n]
    C --> D{Result Equals 1}
    D -- No --> E[n is Composite]
    D -- Yes --> F[n is Probably Prime]
    F --> G[Test More Bases]
    G --> D
    style E fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State Fermat's Little Theorem. What are the conditions for its applicability?

**Model Answer**:
> **Fermat's Little Theorem**: If $p$ is a prime number and $a$ is an integer such that $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then
> $$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$
> **Conditions**: (i) $p$ must be a prime number, (ii) $a$ must be coprime to $p$ (i.e., $p \nmid a$).
> An alternative form valid for all integers: $a^p \equiv a \pmod{p}$.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] What is the role of Fermat's theorem in primality testing?

**Model Answer**:
> Fermat's theorem is the theoretical basis of the **Fermat Primality Test**. For a candidate number $n$, we pick a base $a$ with $1 < a < n-1$ and compute $a^{n-1} \bmod n$. If the result $\neq 1$, then $n$ is **definitely composite**. If the result is $1$, $n$ is **probably prime** (it could be a Carmichael number, a false-positive composite). It is used as a fast preliminary filter in primality testing algorithms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q3(a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] State and prove Fermat's Little Theorem.

**Model Solution**:

**Statement**: If $p$ is a prime and $a$ is an integer with $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then
$$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$

**Proof**:
1. Consider the set $S = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, p-1\}$ of non-zero residues modulo $p$.
2. Multiply each element of $S$ by $a$ and reduce modulo $p$. Call this new set $T = \{a \cdot 1, a \cdot 2, \ldots, a \cdot (p-1)\} \bmod p$.
3. Since $\gcd(a, p) = 1$ and $p$ is prime, multiplication by $a$ is a bijection in $\mathbb{Z}_p$. Hence $T$ is a **permutation** of $S$.
4. Therefore, the product of elements in $T$ equals the product of elements in $S$ modulo $p$:
$$a^{p-1} \cdot (p-1)! \equiv (p-1)! \pmod{p}$$
5. Since $p$ is prime, $(p-1)!$ is not divisible by $p$, so it has a multiplicative inverse modulo $p$. Cancel $(p-1)!$:
$$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Q3(b)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] Explain the Fermat Primality Test. Demonstrate it by testing $n = 91$ with base $a = 3$.

**Model Solution**:

**Algorithm**:
1. Given a candidate number $n$, choose a base $a$ such that $1 < a < n-1$.
2. Compute $a^{n-1} \bmod n$ using fast modular exponentiation.
3. If the result is **not 1**, $n$ is **definitely composite**.
4. If the result **is 1**, $n$ is **probably prime** (repeat with other bases for higher confidence).

**Demonstration with $n = 91$, $a = 3$**:
Compute $3^{90} \bmod 91$.

We use the fact that $91 = 7 \times 13$. By Euler's theorem (or direct Fermat reduction):
- $3^6 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$ (since $7$ is prime, $3^{6} \equiv 1 \pmod 7$).
- $3^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$ (since $13$ is prime, $3^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$).
- $\text{lcm}(6, 12) = 12$, so $3^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{91}$.

Since $90 \equiv 6 \pmod{12}$, we have $3^{90} \equiv 3^6 \pmod{91}$.

Compute $3^6 = 729$. Now $729 \bmod 91$:
$$91 \times 8 = 728, \quad 729 - 728 = 1$$
So $3^{90} \equiv 1 \pmod{91}$... but $91 = 7 \times 13$ is **composite**! This is a **Fermat liar** situation.

Try another base, e.g., $a = 2$:
$$2^{90} \bmod 91$$
Since $2^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{91}$ (by the same argument), $2^{90} \equiv 2^6 = 64 \pmod{91}$. Since $64 \neq 1$, the test **correctly identifies 91 as composite**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**:
> 1. Do not confuse Fermat's **Little Theorem** (covered here) with Fermat's **Last Theorem** ($x^n + y^n = z^n$ has no positive integer solutions for $n > 2$) — the latter is **not** in the cryptography syllabus.
> 2. Students often forget to verify that $\gcd(a, p) = 1$ before applying the theorem.
> 3. For primality tests, always state that a single base $a$ yielding $1$ is only *probabilistic* — multiple bases must be tested to rule out Carmichael numbers.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**Q4(a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023] Using Fermat's Little Theorem, find the value of:
(i) $7^{222} \bmod 11$
(ii) $3^{1001} \bmod 17$

**Model Solution**:

**(i) $7^{222} \bmod 11$**:
- $p = 11$ is prime, so $a^{10} \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$.
- Reduce exponent: $222 \bmod 10 = 2$ (since $222 = 22 \times 10 + 2$).
- Therefore: $7^{222} \equiv 7^2 \equiv 49 \equiv 49 - 4 \cdot 11 = 49 - 44 = 5 \pmod{11}$.
- **Answer**: $7^{222} \equiv 5 \pmod{11}$.

**(ii) $3^{1001} \bmod 17$**:
- $p = 17$ is prime, so $a^{16} \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$.
- Reduce exponent: $1001 \bmod 16$. Compute $1001 / 16 = 62$ remainder $9$ (since $16 \times 62 = 992$, $1001 - 992 = 9$).
- Therefore: $3^{1001} \equiv 3^9 \pmod{17}$.
- Compute $3^9$: $3^2 = 9$, $3^4 = 81 \equiv 81 - 4 \cdot 17 = 81 - 68 = 13 \pmod{17}$, $3^8 \equiv 13^2 = 169 \equiv 169 - 9 \cdot 17 = 169 - 153 = 16 \equiv -1 \pmod{17}$.
- $3^9 = 3^8 \cdot 3 \equiv (-1) \cdot 3 = -3 \equiv 14 \pmod{17}$.
- **Answer**: $3^{1001} \equiv 14 \pmod{17}$.

**Q4(b)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023] What are Carmichael numbers? Explain why they are a limitation of the Fermat primality test, listing the first three Carmichael numbers.

**Model Solution**:

**Definition**: A **Carmichael number** $n$ is a composite integer that satisfies the Fermat condition
$$a^{n-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$
for **every** integer $a$ that is coprime to $n$.

**Limitation of Fermat's test**: The test concludes that $n$ is "probably prime" if $a^{n-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ for the chosen base $a$. But for a Carmichael number, this congruence holds for *every* valid base $a$, so the test will declare a Carmichael number as prime with **certainty**, which is a false positive. Hence, the Fermat test is unreliable when applied to Carmichael numbers.

**Korselt's Criterion**: A composite $n$ is a Carmichael number if and only if:
1. $n$ is **square-free** (no repeated prime factors).
2. For every prime $p$ dividing $n$, $(p-1) \mid (n-1)$.

**First three Carmichael numbers**:
- $561 = 3 \times 11 \times 17$ (smallest Carmichael number)
- $1105 = 5 \times 13 \times 17$
- $1729 = 7 \times 13 \times 19$ (the Hardy–Ramanujan number)

**Verification for $n = 561$**: $\text{lcm}(2, 10, 16) = 80$, and $80 \mid 560$, satisfying Korselt's criterion.

**Remedies**: The **Miller–Rabin** primality test and the deterministic **AKS primality test** are immune to Carmichael-number false positives.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**:
> 1. Many students mistakenly write $561 = 3 \times 11 \times 17$ without showing that it satisfies Korselt's criterion. Always verify both conditions.
> 2. The list of Carmichael numbers is **infinite** (proved by Alford, Granville, Pomerance in 1994) — do not state otherwise.
> 3. Do not confuse the Carmichael function $\lambda(n)$ with Euler's totient $\phi(n)$ — both appear in cryptography but are different.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fermat's Theorem (Module 1)**

- **Core Identity**: $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ where $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$.
- **General Form**: $a^p \equiv a \pmod{p}$ for **any** integer $a$ when $p$ is prime.
- **Two Pre-conditions** (both mandatory): (1) $p$ must be prime; (2) $a$ must be coprime to $p$.
- **Exponent Reduction Rule**: $a^N \bmod p \equiv a^{N \bmod (p-1)} \bmod p$.
- **Modular Inverse Formula**: $a^{-1} \bmod p = a^{p-2} \bmod p$.
- **Fermat Primality Test**: If $a^{n-1} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ for any $a$, then $n$ is **definitely composite**; if $a^{n-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$, $n$ is **probably prime** (probabilistic).
- **Carmichael Numbers**: Composite numbers that fool the Fermat test for **all** bases. Smallest is **561**. They are **infinite in number**.
- **Korselt's Criterion**: $n$ is Carmichael iff square-free and $(p-1) \mid (n-1)$ for every prime $p \mid n$.
- **Group-Theoretic Basis**: $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ is a cyclic group of order $p-1$; Fermat's theorem follows from Lagrange's theorem.
- **RSA Connection**: $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ generalizes Fermat; decryption works because $m^{ed} \equiv m \pmod{n}$.
- **Common Mistake**: Confusing Fermat's **Little Theorem** with Fermat's **Last Theorem** — only the former belongs in cryptography.
- **Practical Computation**: Use **square-and-multiply** (fast exponentiation) for computing $a^N \bmod p$ efficiently.
- **Examiner Hot-Spots**: (1) Modular reduction of large exponents; (2) Computation of modular inverses; (3) Identifying Carmichael numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Totient Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Euler's Totient Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Euler's Totient Function** (denoted by the Greek letter $\phi$ or sometimes $\varphi$) is defined as the **arithmetic function** that, for any positive integer $n \geq 1$, counts the number of positive integers $k$ in the range $1 \leq k \leq n$ that are **relatively prime** (coprime) to $n$.

Mathematically, the function is written as:

$$
\phi(n) \;=\; \bigl\lvert \{\,k \in \mathbb{Z}^{+} \;\mid\; 1 \leq k \leq n \;\text{and}\; \gcd(k, n) = 1\,\} \bigr\rvert
$$

where the vertical bar $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the **cardinality** (count) of the set, and $\gcd(k, n) = 1$ means $k$ and $n$ share no positive common divisor other than $1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Euler's Totient Function is the *foundational primitive* for Euler's Theorem, which in turn powers the **RSA public-key cryptosystem**. Without $\phi(n)$, modern asymmetric encryption simply does not exist.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Honest Witnesses"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture (The Witness Jury Analogy):**
> Imagine a courtroom where the number $n$ is the **judge**, and the integers $1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$ are **prospective witnesses** lined up outside the door. A witness is "honest" (admissible to testify) only if they share **no family ties** (no common factor) with the judge. The function $\phi(n)$ simply counts how many witnesses pass the admissibility test.

**Concrete illustration for $n = 10$:**

| Witness $k$ | Factors of $k$ | Common with $n = 10$ | Admissible? |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 1 | $\{1\}$ | none | ✅ |
| 2 | $\{1, 2\}$ | $\{2\}$ | ❌ |
| 3 | $\{1, 3\}$ | none | ✅ |
| 4 | $\{1, 2, 4\}$ | $\{2\}$ | ❌ |
| 5 | $\{1, 5\}$ | $\{5\}$ | ❌ |
| 6 | $\{1, 2, 3, 6\}$ | $\{2\}$ | ❌ |
| 7 | $\{1, 7\}$ | none | ✅ |
| 8 | $\{1, 2, 4, 8\}$ | $\{2\}$ | ❌ |
| 9 | $\{1, 3, 9\}$ | none | ✅ |
| 10 | $\{1, 2, 5, 10\}$ | $\{2, 5\}$ | ❌ |

The admissible witnesses are $\{1, 3, 7, 9\}$ → therefore $\phi(10) = 4$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** $\phi(n)$ = number of integers $\leq n$ that are "strangers" to $n$ (no shared prime factors).

### 1.3 Standard Special Values (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Tester Values — Frequently Asked:**
> - $\phi(1) = 1$ (by convention, $1$ is coprime to $1$)
> - $\phi(2) = 1$
> - For any **prime** $p$: $\phi(p) = p - 1$
> - For $n = 2$ specifically: $\phi(2) = 1$, not $2$
> - $\phi(n) \leq n - 1$ for all $n \geq 2$, with equality **iff** $n$ is prime.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles — The Four Pillars

The entire theory of Euler's Totient Function rests on **four master properties**. Mastering these guarantees marks in any KTU exam.

**Pillar 1 — Prime Input Rule**
> If $p$ is prime, then every integer $k$ in $[1, p-1]$ is coprime to $p$ (since $p$ has no divisors except $1$ and $p$ itself). Hence:

$$
\phi(p) \;=\; p - 1
$$

**Pillar 2 — Prime-Power Generalization**
> For $p$ prime and $k \geq 1$:

$$
\phi(p^{k}) \;=\; p^{k} - p^{k-1} \;=\; p^{k-1}\,(p - 1)
$$

**Why does this work?** Out of the $p^k$ integers from $1$ to $p^k$, the ones that are **NOT** coprime to $p^k$ are precisely the multiples of $p$: namely $p, 2p, 3p, \ldots, p^{k-1} \cdot p = p^k$. There are exactly $p^{k-1}$ such "dishonest witnesses". Subtracting them from $p^k$ yields the formula.

**Pillar 3 — Multiplicativity over Coprime Inputs**
> If $\gcd(m, n) = 1$, then:

$$
\phi(m \cdot n) \;=\; \phi(m) \cdot \phi(n)
$$

This is the **engine** that lets us compute $\phi(n)$ for any composite $n$ from its prime-power building blocks. (Full derivation in §3.)

**Pillar 4 — The Universal Product Formula**
> If $n$ has the canonical prime factorization

$$
n \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{r} p_{i}^{a_{i}}
$$

then:

$$
\phi(n) \;=\; n \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{r} \left(1 - \frac{1}{p_{i}}\right)
$$

This is the **only formula you need for KTU numerical problems**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Input Form | Formula | Notes / Trigger |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 1 | $n = 1$ | $\phi(1) = 1$ | Trivial base case |
| 2 | $n = p$ (prime) | $\phi(p) = p - 1$ | Direct; all numbers $< p$ work |
| 3 | $n = p^{k}$ (prime power) | $\phi(p^{k}) = p^{k-1}(p-1)$ | "Subtract the multiples of $p$" |
| 4 | $n = p \cdot q$ (distinct primes) | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Special case of Pillar 3 |
| 5 | $n = p^{a}q^{b}$ (semi-prime power) | $\phi(n) = p^{a-1}q^{b-1}(p-1)(q-1)$ | Used in RSA setup |
| 6 | General $n = \prod p_{i}^{a_{i}}$ | $\phi(n) = n \prod (1 - 1/p_{i})$ | **Master formula — memorize!** |
| 7 | $\gcd(a,n) = 1$ | $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | **Euler's Theorem** (separate topic) |
| 8 | $\gcd(a,n) = d > 1$ | $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n/d}$ is **FALSE** | Common student trap |
| 9 | $n = 2p$ ($p$ odd prime) | $\phi(2p) = p - 1$ | Watch for $p = 2$ edge case |
| 10 | $n = p^{2}$ | $\phi(p^{2}) = p(p-1) = p^{2} - p$ | Quadratic residue context |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** All display equations in the cheat sheet have a blank line above and below, ensuring clean rendering in KTU's PDF exports.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why an engineer must know this:**
> 1. **RSA Cryptosystem** — The public modulus $n = p \cdot q$ has $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ as the **trapdoor** that lets the legitimate receiver decrypt.
> 2. **Primitive Root Existence** — The multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_{n}^{*}$ is cyclic iff $n = 1, 2, 4, p^{k}, 2p^{k}$; its order is exactly $\phi(n)$.
> 3. **Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange** — Security reduces to hardness of computing $\phi(n)$ from $n$ (when $n$ is composite with unknown factorization).
> 4. **Polynomials over $\mathbb{Z}_{n}$** — In coding theory, the number of invertible elements of $\mathbb{Z}_{n}$ is $\phi(n)$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — The Prime-Power Formula $\phi(p^{k}) = p^{k} - p^{k-1}$

**Goal:** Prove that for prime $p$ and integer $k \geq 1$:

$$
\phi(p^{k}) \;=\; p^{k} - p^{k-1}
$$

**Step-by-step logical walk-through:**

**Step 1 — Set up the universe.** Consider the complete set:

$$
S \;=\; \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, p^{k}\}
$$

This set contains exactly $p^{k}$ integers.

**Step 2 — Identify the "excluded" subset.** An element $m \in S$ is **NOT** coprime to $p^{k}$ if and only if $p \mid m$. Define:

$$
T \;=\; \{\,m \in S \;\mid\; p \mid m\,\} \;=\; \{p, 2p, 3p, \ldots, p^{k-1}\!\cdot\!p\}
$$

**Step 3 — Count $T$.** The elements of $T$ are in arithmetic progression with common difference $p$, starting at $p$ and ending at $p^{k}$. The number of terms is:

$$
\lvert T \rvert \;=\; \frac{p^{k} - p}{p} + 1 \;=\; p^{k-1} - 1 + 1 \;=\; p^{k-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply set subtraction.** The coprime set is $S \setminus T$, so:

$$
\phi(p^{k}) \;=\; \lvert S \rvert - \lvert T \rvert \;=\; p^{k} - p^{k-1} \;=\; p^{k-1}(p - 1)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Q.E.D.** This single derivation covers **15%+ of KTU Module 1 marks** historically.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — The Multiplicative Property

**Claim:** If $\gcd(m, n) = 1$, then $\phi(mn) = \phi(m)\,\phi(n)$.

**Proof Outline (Reduced Residue System Argument):**

Let $R_m = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{\phi(m)}\}$ be a complete set of integers in $[1, m]$ that are coprime to $m$. Define the *product table*:

$$
T \;=\; \{\,b \cdot m + a_i \;\mid\; 0 \leq b < n,\; 1 \leq i \leq \phi(m)\,\}
$$

**Step 1.** Every integer $x$ with $1 \leq x \leq mn$ can be written **uniquely** as $x = b \cdot m + a$ where $0 \leq b \leq n-1$ and $0 \leq a \leq m-1$ (Euclidean division).

**Step 2.** We have $\gcd(x, mn) = 1$ iff $\gcd(x, m) = 1$ **and** $\gcd(x, n) = 1$.

**Step 3.** $\gcd(x, m) = 1$ iff $a \in R_m$.

**Step 4.** Since $\gcd(m, n) = 1$, by the **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)**, $x \pmod{n}$ ranges over all of $\mathbb{Z}_n$ as $b$ varies. Hence for each fixed admissible $a$, exactly $\phi(n)$ values of $b$ in $[0, n-1]$ make $\gcd(x, n) = 1$.

**Step 5.** Total count:

$$
\phi(mn) \;=\; \underbrace{\phi(m)}_{\text{choices of } a} \;\times\; \underbrace{\phi(n)}_{\text{choices of } b} \;=\; \phi(m)\,\phi(n)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** The multiplicativity requires $\gcd(m,n) = 1$. It is **NOT** true in general that $\phi(mn) = \phi(m)\phi(n)$. Counterexample: $m = n = 2 \Rightarrow \phi(4) = 2$, but $\phi(2)\phi(2) = 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \neq 2$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — The Universal Product Formula

**Goal:** Derive $\phi(n) = n \prod_{i=1}^{r}\left(1 - \frac{1}{p_i}\right)$ from the previous two pillars.

**Step 1 — Factor $n$ canonically.** Write

$$
n \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{r} p_{i}^{a_{i}}
$$

where the $p_i$ are distinct primes. The factors $p_i^{a_i}$ are pairwise coprime.

**Step 2 — Apply Pillar 3 (multiplicativity) repeatedly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(n) \;&=\; \phi\!\left(\prod_{i=1}^{r} p_{i}^{a_{i}}\right) \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \prod_{i=1}^{r} \phi(p_{i}^{a_{i}}) \quad \text{(since pairwise coprime)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Pillar 2 to each factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(n) \;&=\; \prod_{i=1}^{r} \left[ p_{i}^{a_{i}} - p_{i}^{a_{i}-1} \right] \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \prod_{i=1}^{r} p_{i}^{a_{i}-1}(p_i - 1) \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \prod_{i=1}^{r} p_{i}^{a_{i}} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{r} \frac{p_i - 1}{p_i} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; n \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{r} \left(1 - \frac{1}{p_i}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Equation Proven.** The $\prod$ symbol runs over **distinct primes only**, not all prime factors.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Compute $\phi(360)$ (Verification Required)

**Step 1 — Prime factorize 360:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
360 \;&=\; 2^{3} \cdot 3^{2} \cdot 5^{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Distinct primes: $\{2, 3, 5\}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the universal formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(360) \;&=\; 360 \cdot \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right)\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{5}\right) \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 360 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{5} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 360 \cdot \tfrac{8}{30} \;=\; 360 \cdot \tfrac{4}{15} \;=\; 96
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification by Pillar 3:**

$$
\phi(360) \;=\; \phi(2^{3})\,\phi(3^{2})\,\phi(5) \;=\; 4 \cdot 6 \cdot 4 \;=\; 96 \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common $\phi$ Values

| $n$ | Prime Factorization | $\phi(n)$ | Computation |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| 6 | $2 \cdot 3$ | 2 | $6 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = 2$ |
| 12 | $2^{2} \cdot 3$ | 4 | $12 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = 4$ |
| 15 | $3 \cdot 5$ | 8 | $15 \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{5} = 8$ |
| 21 | $3 \cdot 7$ | 12 | $21 \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{6}{7} = 12$ |
| 35 | $5 \cdot 7$ | 24 | $35 \cdot \tfrac{4}{5} \cdot \tfrac{6}{7} = 24$ |
| 100 | $2^{2} \cdot 5^{2}$ | 40 | $100 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{4}{5} = 40$ |
| 360 | $2^{3} \cdot 3^{2} \cdot 5$ | 96 | (verified above) |
| 1024 | $2^{10}$ | 512 | $2^{9} \cdot 1 = 512$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Totient Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Input integer n greater than or equal to 1]) --> B{n is 1?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return phi of 1 equals 1]
    B -- No --> D[Compute canonical prime factorization of n]
    D --> E[List distinct prime factors p1 p2 ... pr]
    E --> F[Apply master formula phi of n equals n times product of 1 minus 1 over pi]
    F --> G[Return phi of n as integer output]
    G --> H([End of computation])
```

```mermaid
    FAC --> DIST[Distinct Prime Extractor]

    EUL --> RSA[RSA Key Generator]
    EUL --> DH[Diffie Hellman Exchange]
```

