# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
**Q: Define a deterministic primality algorithm. State the time complexity of the AKS algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **deterministic primality algorithm** is one that, for any input integer $n$, always returns the correct answer (**PRIME** or **COMPOSITE**) in a bounded number of computational steps, with **zero probability of error**. It uses no randomness and produces identical output for identical input.

The **AKS (Agrawal–Kayal–Saxena) algorithm**, published in 2002, is the first known unconditional deterministic polynomial-time primality test.

**Time Complexity:**
- Original (2002): $\mathcal{O}(\log^{12} n)$
- After improvements (Lenstra, Pomerance): $\mathcal{O}(\log^{6} n)$

The algorithm relies on verifying the polynomial identity:
$$(X+a)^n \equiv X^n + a \pmod{n, X^r - 1}$$
for suitable $r$ and all $a$ up to $\lfloor \sqrt{\phi(r)} \log n \rfloor$.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: State Fermat's Little Theorem. How does the AKS algorithm use it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fermat's Little Theorem (FLT):**
If $n$ is prime and $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, then:
$$a^{n-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$
Equivalently: $a^n \equiv a \pmod{n}$.

**Use in AKS:**
AKS generalises FLT from integers to **polynomials**. Replacing the integer $a$ with the polynomial $(X+a)$, AKS tests:
$$(X+a)^n \equiv X^n + a \pmod{n}$$
This must hold *exactly* for prime $n$ because, by the binomial theorem, all intermediate binomial coefficients $\binom{n}{k}$ are divisible by $n$ when $n$ is prime. The identity is then reduced modulo $(X^r - 1)$ to obtain a computationally efficient check.

If $n$ is **composite**, at least one $a$ (called a *witness*) makes the identity fail — proving $n$ is composite.

---

---

## 📗 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1]**

#### **Part (a) (7 marks)** — *Explain the AKS deterministic primality algorithm with the key polynomial identity.*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The AKS algorithm, proposed by Agrawal, Kayal, and Saxena in 2002, was the first *unconditional*, *deterministic*, *polynomial-time* primality test. It solved a long-standing open problem in computational number theory and algorithm design.

**Key Polynomial Identity (3 marks):**
The algorithm is based on the following identity. For prime $n$:
$$(X+a)^n \equiv X^n + a \pmod{n}$$
This is the **polynomial generalisation of Fermat's Little Theorem**. By the binomial theorem:
$$(X+a)^n = X^n + a^n + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \binom{n}{k} X^k a^{n-k}$$
For prime $n$, every binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ for $1 \le k \le n-1$ is divisible by $n$, and $a^n \equiv a \pmod{n}$ by FLT. Hence the identity holds.

To make this check computationally efficient, it is reduced modulo the polynomial $X^r - 1$ (where $r$ is a parameter with multiplicative-order properties):
$$(X+a)^n \equiv X^n + a \pmod{n, X^r - 1}$$

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Check if $n$ is a perfect power → return COMPOSITE if yes.
2. Find the smallest $r$ such that $o_r(n) > \log^2 n$.
3. If $n \le r$, verify by trial division.
4. For each $a = 1, 2, \ldots, r$: check $\gcd(a, n) = 1$.
5. For each $a = 1, 2, \ldots, \lfloor\sqrt{\phi(r)} \log n\rfloor$: verify the polynomial identity.
6. If all checks pass → return **PRIME**; otherwise → return **COMPOSITE**.

The complexity is $\mathcal{O}(\log^{12} n)$, later improved to $\mathcal{O}(\log^6 n)$.

---

#### **Part (b) (7 marks)** — *Apply the AKS polynomial test to $n = 11$ and determine its primality.*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $n = 11$. We need to determine whether $n$ is prime using the AKS identity.

**Step 1: Check perfect power.**
$11 = 11^1$ — not a perfect power ($k > 1$). ✓ Continue.

**Step 2: Find $r$.**
$\log_2(11) \approx 3.459$, so $\log^2(11) \approx 11.97$.
Test small $r$ values:

| $r$ | $o_r(11)$ | $> 11.97$? |
|-----|-----------|------------|
| 2 | 1 | No |
| 3 | 2 | No |
| 4 | 2 | No |
| 5 | 4 | No |
| 6 | 1 | No |
| 7 | 6 | No |
| 8 | 2 | No |
| 9 | 3 | No |
| 10 | 4 | No |
| 11 | undefined | skip |
| 12 | 2 | No |
| 13 | 6 | No |
| 14 | 6 | No |
| 15 | 4 | No |
| 16 | 4 | No |
| 17 | 16 | **Yes** ✓ |

So $r = 17$.

**Step 3: Bound check.**
$n = 11 \le r = 17$. So AKS verifies primality by **trial division** up to $\sqrt{11} \approx 3.317$.
- $11 / 2 = 5.5$ (not integer)
- $11 / 3 = 3.67$ (not integer)

No divisor found → **PRIME**.

**Conclusion:** $n = 11$ is **PRIME**. ✓

*(This illustrates an important practical feature: AKS reduces to trial division for small $n$.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2023, Model Paper Module 1]**

#### **Part (a) (7 marks)** — *Compare deterministic and probabilistic primality algorithms with examples.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- A **deterministic** primality algorithm always returns the correct answer with zero error probability. Examples: Trial Division, AKS Algorithm.
- A **probabilistic** primality algorithm may return an incorrect answer with a small, bounded probability. Examples: Solovay–Strassen, Miller–Rabin.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Deterministic | Probabilistic |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Correctness | Always correct | Correct w.h.p. |
| Randomness | None | Required |
| Speed (practical) | Moderate (AKS) | Very fast (Miller–Rabin) |
| Time Complexity | Polynomial (AKS) | Polynomial (Miller–Rabin) |
| Error Probability | 0 | $\le 1/4^k$ per round |
| Industry Use | Rare (theoretical) | Standard (OpenSSL) |
| Examples | AKS, Trial Division | Miller–Rabin, Solovay–Strassen |

**Miller–Rabin Example (1 mark):**
For $n = 221$ with base $a = 174$: $174^{110} \equiv 1 \pmod{221}$, but with $a = 137$: $137^{110} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{221}$ — 221 is **composite**.

**AKS Example (1 mark):**
For $n = 7$, AKS finds $r = 11$ and verifies $(X+1)^7 \equiv X^7 + 1 \pmod{7, X^{11} - 1}$ — confirms **prime**.

**Conclusion:** Deterministic algorithms provide mathematical certainty (essential for theory and formal verification), while probabilistic algorithms provide practical speed (essential for production systems).

---

#### **Part (b) (7 marks)** — *Explain why AKS reduces primality to a polynomial identity test, and state the role of cyclotomic polynomials.*

**Model Answer:**

**Reduction to Polynomial Identity (3 marks):**
The key insight of AKS is that primality of $n$ is *equivalent* to a polynomial identity holding modulo $n$:
$$(X+a)^n \equiv X^n + a \pmod{n} \quad \text{for all } a \in \mathbb{Z}$$
This is because Fermat's Little Theorem extends naturally to polynomials when $n$ is prime (via the binomial theorem and vanishing of binomial coefficients $\binom{n}{k} \pmod n$).

To make the test **efficient**, the identity is computed in the quotient ring $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}[X]/(X^r-1)$, where polynomial degrees are bounded by $r-1$. This allows the comparison to be done in polynomial time.

**Role of Cyclotomic Polynomials (3 marks):**
The cyclotomic polynomial $\Phi_r(X)$ is defined as:
$$\Phi_r(X) = \prod_{\substack{1 \le k \le r \\ \gcd(k,r)=1}} \left(X - e^{2\pi i k/r}\right)$$
Equivalently:
$$\Phi_r(X) = \prod_{d \mid r} (X^d - 1)^{\mu(r/d)}$$
where $\mu$ is the Möbius function.

AKS uses $\Phi_r(X)$ because:
1. It is **irreducible** over $\mathbb{Q}$, providing clean algebraic structure.
2. Its degree $\phi(r)$ bounds the number of independent polynomial congruences needed.
3. The size of the search space for witnesses $a$ becomes $\lfloor\sqrt{\phi(r)} \log n\rfloor$ — small enough for polynomial time.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
By combining FLT (correctness), polynomial reduction (efficiency), and cyclotomic polynomials (structural rigidity), AKS achieves the first unconditional deterministic polynomial-time primality test.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing AKS with Miller-Rabin.** Many students incorrectly attribute *deterministic polynomial-time* to Miller-Rabin. Remember: Miller-Rabin is **probabilistic**. AKS is the *deterministic* polynomial-time algorithm. (Loss: ~2 marks)
>
> 2. **Writing $a^{n-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ as the AKS identity.** This is Fermat's Little Theorem, NOT AKS. The AKS identity is the **polynomial** version: $(X+a)^n \equiv X^n + a \pmod{n, X^r-1}$. (Loss: ~3 marks)
>
> 3. **Stating AKS complexity as $O(n)$ instead of $O(\log^{12} n)$.** AKS is polynomial in $\log n$ (input size), not in $n$ itself. (Loss: ~1 mark)
>
> 4. **Forgetting the perfect-power pre-check.** AKS first checks if $n = a^k$ for some $a, k \ge 2$. Skipping this step makes the proof incomplete. (Loss: ~1 mark)
>
> 5. **Confusing "deterministic" with "exhaustive trial division."** Trial division IS deterministic, but it is NOT polynomial time. AKS is the only known deterministic *polynomial-time* test. (Loss: ~2 marks)
>
> 6. **Missing the role of cyclotomic polynomials.** Mentioning cyclotomic polynomials $\Phi_r(X)$ is a high-value addition that examiners look for. (Loss: ~1 mark)

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deterministic Primality Algorithm (AKS)**

- ✅ **Definition:** A deterministic primality algorithm *always* returns the correct answer with **zero error**.
- ✅ **AKS Algorithm:** Agrawal, Kayal, Saxena — **2002** — first unconditional deterministic polynomial-time primality test.
- ✅ **Core Identity:** $(X+a)^n \equiv X^n + a \pmod{n, X^r - 1}$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- ✅ **Foundation:** Polynomial generalisation of **Fermat's Little Theorem**.
- ✅ **Pre-check:** If $n = a^k$ ($k \ge 2$), output **COMPOSITE**.
- ✅ **Parameter $r$:** Smallest integer with $o_r(n) > \log^2 n$.
- ✅ **Witness range for $a$:** $a = 1, 2, \ldots, \lfloor\sqrt{\phi(r)} \log n\rfloor$.
- ✅ **Complexity:** Original $\mathcal{O}(\log^{12} n)$ → improved $\mathcal{O}(\log^{6} n)$.
- ✅ **Cyclotomic Polynomial:** $\Phi_r(X) = \prod_{d \mid r} (X^d - 1)^{\mu(r/d)}$ — bounds the search space.
- ✅ **Correctness:** Soundness (no false positives) + Completeness (no false negatives) = zero-error algorithm.
- ✅ **Comparison:** AKS is deterministic polynomial-time; Miller-Rabin is probabilistic polynomial-time; Trial Division is deterministic exponential-time.
- ✅ **Industry Status:** OpenSSL/libSodium use Miller-Rabin (faster in practice); AKS is theoretically superior.
- ✅ **Crypto Applications:** RSA key generation, Diffie–Hellman safe primes, ECC parameter validation.
- ✅ **Open Problem (Solved):** Before 2002, it was unknown if deterministic polynomial-time primality testing existed. AKS resolved it.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Viva:** Be ready to explain *why* AKS uses polynomials instead of integers — the answer is that polynomials carry *more structural information* (degrees, coefficient distributions) than scalar integers, allowing finer discrimination between primes and composites.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Logarithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete Logarithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete Logarithm (DL):** Let $G$ be a cyclic group of order $n$ with generator $\alpha$. For an element $\beta \in G$, the **discrete logarithm of $\beta$ with respect to the base $\alpha$** is the unique integer $x$, where $0 \le x \le n-1$, such that:
>
> $$\alpha^{x} \equiv \beta \pmod{n}$$

The discrete logarithm of $\beta$ to base $\alpha$ is denoted as:
$$x = \log_{\alpha} \beta \pmod{n}$$

or equivalently, $x = \text{ind}_{\alpha}(\beta)$ (the **index** of $\beta$ w.r.t. $\alpha$).

## 1.2 The Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP):** Given a prime $p$, a generator $\alpha$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, and an element $\beta \in \mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, find the unique integer $x$ such that $\alpha^x \equiv \beta \pmod{p}$.

The DLP is the cornerstone of modern public-key cryptography. Schemes such as **Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange**, **ElGamal Encryption**, and **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)** all derive their security from the computational hardness of solving the DLP in suitably chosen cyclic groups.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a circular stadium with $\mathbf{p-1}$ numbered doors (where $p$ is a large prime, say **2,048 bits long**). A magician starts at Door 0 and repeatedly jumps by a fixed number of doors (the generator $\alpha$) in a modular fashion.

- **Easy direction (Exponentiation):** Given the jump size ($\alpha$) and the number of jumps ($x$), predicting the final door number ($\beta$) is **computationally trivial** — just multiply and reduce.
- **Hard direction (Discrete Logarithm):** Given the starting door, the jump size, and the final door number, **figuring out how many jumps were made is computationally infeasible** — even for the fastest supercomputers, when $p$ is large.

This asymmetry between easy multiplication and hard reversal is the **engine of asymmetric cryptography**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Prime modulus | $p$ | A large prime defining the finite field $\mathbb{Z}_p$ |
| Generator (primitive root) | $\alpha$ | An element whose powers generate the entire group $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ |
| Group order | $p-1$ | Total number of elements in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ |
| Exponent | $x$ | The unknown discrete logarithm |
| Target element | $\beta$ | The result of $\alpha^x \bmod p$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why primes?** Working modulo a prime $p$ guarantees that $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ forms a **cyclic group** of order $p-1$, ensuring a primitive root $\alpha$ always exists. This gives the DL problem its well-defined mathematical structure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Group Theory Behind Discrete Logarithms

The discrete logarithm is defined over a **cyclic group**. A cyclic group $G = \langle \alpha \rangle$ is generated by a single element $\alpha$ such that every element $\beta \in G$ can be written as $\alpha^x$ for some $x$.

**For the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$:**
- The group has order $n = p - 1$.
- A **primitive root** (or generator) $\alpha$ is an element of order $n$, meaning $\alpha$ has period $n$ under repeated multiplication.
- Every non-zero element $1, 2, \ldots, p-1$ appears exactly once in the sequence $\alpha^1, \alpha^2, \ldots, \alpha^{p-1}$.

## 2.2 Properties of Discrete Logarithms

Let $x = \log_{\alpha} \beta$ and $y = \log_{\alpha} \gamma$. Then:

1. **Multiplicative Property of Arguments:**
$$\log_{\alpha}(\beta \cdot \gamma) \equiv \log_{\alpha} \beta + \log_{\alpha} \gamma \pmod{p-1}$$

2. **Power Rule:**
$$\log_{\alpha}(\beta^k) \equiv k \cdot \log_{\alpha} \beta \pmod{p-1}$$

3. **Change of Base:** If $\beta = \alpha^x$, then for any $\gamma$:
$$\log_{\beta} \alpha \cdot \log_{\alpha} \beta \equiv 1 \pmod{p-1}$$

4. **Exponentiation of Base:**
$$\log_{\alpha^k} \beta \equiv \log_{\alpha} \beta \cdot k^{-1} \pmod{p-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The modulus in all these properties is **$p-1$**, NOT $p$. This is because Fermat's Little Theorem states $\alpha^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, so exponents repeat every $p-1$ steps.

## 2.3 Existence and Uniqueness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uniqueness Theorem:** For a primitive root $\alpha$ modulo $p$, the discrete logarithm $\log_{\alpha} \beta$ exists and is unique modulo $(p-1)$.

Proof sketch: Since $\alpha$ is a generator of $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, every $\beta \in \mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ can be written as $\alpha^x$ for some $x \in \{1, 2, \ldots, p-1\}$. If $\alpha^x = \alpha^y$, then $\alpha^{x-y} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, which by order properties forces $x \equiv y \pmod{p-1}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\alpha^x \equiv \beta \pmod{p}$ | Forward computation (easy) |
| 2 | $x = \log_{\alpha} \beta \pmod{p-1}$ | Inverse computation (hard) |
| 3 | $\alpha^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Fermat's Little Theorem |
| 4 | $\phi(p) = p-1$ (for prime $p$) | Euler's Totient |
| 5 | $\alpha^{\phi(p)} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Euler's Theorem |
| 6 | $\text{ord}(\alpha) \vert (p-1)$ | Order divides group order |
| 7 | $\log_{\alpha}(\beta \gamma) \equiv \log_{\alpha}\beta + \log_{\alpha}\gamma \pmod{p-1}$ | Multiplicative to additive |
| 8 | $\log_{\alpha}(\beta^k) \equiv k \cdot \log_{\alpha}\beta \pmod{p-1}$ | Power rule |
| 9 | $x = a \cdot \log_{\alpha}\beta + b \pmod{p-1}$ | Linear combination |

## 2.5 Algorithmic Complexity Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Asymmetry:**
> - Computing $\beta = \alpha^x \bmod p$ using **fast modular exponentiation** (square-and-multiply): $O(\log x \cdot \log^2 p)$ bit operations — **polynomial time**.
> - Solving $x = \log_{\alpha} \beta \bmod p$ using **brute force**: $O(p)$ — **exponential time** (infeasible for 2048-bit primes).
> - Best known classical algorithm: **Index Calculus / Number Field Sieve**, sub-exponential but still infeasible at recommended key sizes.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of DLP |
|-------------|-------------|
| **Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange** | Two parties compute shared secret $\alpha^{ab}$ without revealing $a$ or $b$ |
| **ElGamal Encryption** | Ciphertext security relies on DLP hardness in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ |
| **Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)** | Signatures use DLP over prime-order subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ |
| **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)** | Generalized DLP over elliptic curve groups $\rightarrow$ ECDLP |
| **SSH / TLS / IPsec** | Forward-secret key agreement using ephemeral DH |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Computing Forward Direction (Easy)

**Problem:** Given $p = 17$, $\alpha = 3$, and $x = 12$, compute $\beta = 3^{12} \bmod 17$.

**Step 1 — Verify that 3 is a primitive root mod 17.**
The group $\mathbb{Z}_{17}^*$ has order $16$. For 3 to be a primitive root, $3^{16/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{17}$ for every prime divisor $q$ of 16. Prime divisors are $q = 2$.

Check $3^{16/2} = 3^{8} \bmod 17$:
$$3^{2} = 9, \quad 3^{4} = 9^{2} = 81 \equiv 81 - 4(17) = 81 - 68 = 13 \pmod{17}$$
$$3^{8} = 13^{2} = 169 \equiv 169 - 9(17) = 169 - 153 = 16 \equiv -1 \pmod{17}$$

Since $3^{8} \not\equiv 1$, **3 is a primitive root mod 17**. ✓

**Step 2 — Apply square-and-multiply for $3^{12} \bmod 17$.**

Convert 12 to binary: $12 = 1100_2 = 8 + 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^{1} &\equiv 3 \pmod{17} \\
3^{2} &\equiv 9 \pmod{17} \\
3^{4} &\equiv 9^{2} = 81 \equiv 13 \pmod{17} \\
3^{8} &\equiv 13^{2} = 169 \equiv 16 \equiv -1 \pmod{17}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply relevant powers.** We need $3^{8+4} = 3^{8} \cdot 3^{4}$:
$$\beta = 3^{12} \equiv 3^{8} \cdot 3^{4} \equiv (-1) \cdot 13 \equiv -13 \equiv 17 - 13 = 4 \pmod{17}$$

**Result:** $\beta = 4$. Therefore, $\log_{3} 4 \equiv 12 \pmod{16}$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Solving the Discrete Logarithm (Brute Force)

**Problem:** Given $p = 17$, $\alpha = 3$, $\beta = 4$, find $x = \log_{3} 4 \bmod 16$.

**Step 1 — Build the powers table of 3 modulo 17.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3^{1} &\equiv 3 \pmod{17} \\
3^{2} &\equiv 9 \pmod{17} \\
3^{3} &\equiv 27 \equiv 10 \pmod{17} \\
3^{4} &\equiv 30 \equiv 13 \pmod{17} \\
3^{5} &\equiv 39 \equiv 5 \pmod{17} \\
3^{6} &\equiv 15 \pmod{17} \\
3^{7} &\equiv 45 \equiv 11 \pmod{17} \\
3^{8} &\equiv 33 \equiv 16 \equiv -1 \pmod{17} \\
3^{9} &\equiv -3 \equiv 14 \pmod{17} \\
3^{10} &\equiv 42 \equiv 8 \pmod{17} \\
3^{11} &\equiv 24 \equiv 7 \pmod{17} \\
3^{12} &\equiv 21 \equiv 4 \pmod{17} \quad \longleftarrow \text{MATCH!}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Match found:** $\beta = 4$ appears at exponent $x = 12$.

**Verification:** $3^{12} \bmod 17 = 4$ ✓

**Result:** $x = \log_{3} 4 \equiv 12 \pmod{16}$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Using DL Properties for Indirect Computation

**Problem:** Let $p = 41$, $\alpha = 6$ (a primitive root). Given that $\log_{6} 11 \equiv 13 \pmod{40}$ and $\log_{6} 7 \equiv 15 \pmod{40}$, compute $\log_{6}(11 \cdot 7) \bmod 40$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Multiplicative Property of Arguments:**
$$\log_{6}(11 \cdot 7) \equiv \log_{6} 11 + \log_{6} 7 \pmod{40}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**
$$\log_{6}(77) \equiv 13 + 15 \equiv 28 \pmod{40}$$

**Step 3 — Reduce 77 modulo 41:**
$$77 = 1 \cdot 41 + 36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 77 \equiv 36 \pmod{41}$$

So $\log_{6} 36 \equiv 28 \pmod{40}$.

**Verification:** $6^{28} \bmod 41$ should equal 36.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Using the Power Rule

**Problem:** Given $p = 43$, $\alpha = 3$, and $\log_{3} 25 \equiv 7 \pmod{42}$, find $\log_{3}(25^5) \bmod 42$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Power Rule:**
$$\log_{3}(25^{5}) \equiv 5 \cdot \log_{3} 25 \pmod{42}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$5 \cdot 7 = 35$$

**Step 3 — Reduce modulo 42:**
$$35 < 42 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \log_{3}(25^{5}) \equiv 35 \pmod{42}$$

**Verification:** $3^{35} \equiv 25^5 \pmod{43}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Problem Type | Given | Compute | Method | Difficulty |
|--------------|-------|---------|--------|------------|
| Forward | $\alpha, x, p$ | $\beta = \alpha^x \bmod p$ | Square-and-multiply | Trivial |
| Reverse (small $p$) | $\alpha, \beta, p$ | $x = \log_{\alpha}\beta$ | Brute-force table | Easy |
| Reverse (large $p$) | $\alpha, \beta, p$ | $x = \log_{\alpha}\beta$ | Baby-step Giant-step | Moderate |
| Reverse (cryptographic) | $\alpha, \beta, p$ (2048-bit) | $x = \log_{\alpha}\beta$ | Best known attacks | Infeasible |
| Indirect via product | $\log_{\alpha}\beta, \log_{\alpha}\gamma$ | $\log_{\alpha}(\beta\gamma)$ | Add logs mod $p-1$ | Easy |
| Indirect via power | $\log_{\alpha}\beta, k$ | $\log_{\alpha}(\beta^k)$ | Multiply by $k$ | Easy |

## 3.6 Cryptographic Bit-Size Recommendations

| Security Level | Group Type | Recommended Size |
|----------------|------------|------------------|
| 80-bit security | $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ | $p \ge 1024$ bits |
| 128-bit security | $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ | $p \ge 3072$ bits |
| 192-bit security | $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ | $p \ge 7680$ bits |
| 256-bit security | $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ | $p \ge 15360$ bits |
| 128-bit security | Elliptic Curve | Curve of 256 bits (e.g., secp256r1) |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: The Asymmetry of Discrete Logarithms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Private Exponent x"] -->|"Exponentiation alpha pow x mod p"| B["Public Element beta"]
    B -->|"Discrete Log Problem"| C["Recover x"]
    C -->|"Infeasible for large p"| D["Cross Marked Hard Path"]
    A -->|"Trivial Forward Path"| B
    B -->|"DLP Result"| E["Cryptographic Security"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DLP-Based Cryptosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        P1["Prime Modulus p"]
        P2["Generator alpha"]
        P3["Private Key x"]
    end
    subgraph KEYGEN["KEY GENERATION STAGE"]
        K1["Compute beta = alpha pow x mod p"]
        K2["Public Key = p, alpha, beta"]
        K3["Private Key = x"]
    end
    subgraph CRYPTO["CRYPTOGRAPHIC OPERATIONS"]
        O1["Diffie Hellman Shared Secret"]
        O2["ElGamal Encrypt or Decrypt"]
        O3["DSA Sign or Verify"]
    end
    subgraph ATTACK["ADVERSARY CHALLENGE"]
        A1["Observed Public Key p alpha beta"]
        A2["Solve DLP to recover x"]
        A3["Computationally Infeasible"]
    end
    P1 --> K1
    P2 --> K1
    P3 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    P3 --> K3
    K2 --> O1
    K2 --> O2
    K2 --> O3
    K2 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
```

## 4.3 Computation Flow: Square-and-Multiply vs. Brute-Force DLP

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EASY["EASY DIRECTION EXPONENTIATION"]
        E1["Start with result = 1"]
        E2["Read bit of x from MSB to LSB"]
        E3["Square result mod p"]
        E4{"Bit is 1?"}
        E5["Multiply result by alpha mod p"]
        E6["Output beta in O of log x steps"]
        E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4
        E4 -->|"Yes"| E5 --> E2
        E4 -->|"No"| E2
        E5 --> E6
    end
    subgraph HARD["HARD DIRECTION DISCRETE LOG"]
        H1["Start with candidate = 1"]
        H2["Compute alpha pow candidate mod p"]
        H3{"Equals beta?"}
        H4["Increment candidate by 1"]
        H5["Match Found Output x"]
        H6["No Match Infeasible Continue"]
        H1 --> H2 --> H3
        H3 -->|"Yes"| H5
        H3 -->|"No"| H4 --> H1
    end
```

## 4.4 Mapping Between Discrete and Ordinary Logarithms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REAL["REAL LOGARITHM"]
        R1["y = log base a of x"]
        R2["a to the y = x over real numbers"]

    end
    subgraph DISCRETE["DISCRETE LOGARITHM"]
        D1["x = log base alpha of beta mod p"]
        D2["alpha to the x is congruent to beta mod p"]

        D4["Inverse of modular exponentiation over Z sub p"]
    end
    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    R1 -. ANALOGY .-> D1
    R2 -. ANALOGY .-> D2
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Define the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP). Why is it considered computationally hard for large primes?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **DLP Definition:** Given a prime $p$, a generator $\alpha$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, and an element $\beta \in \mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, find integer $x$ such that $\alpha^x \equiv \beta \pmod{p}$, where $0 \le x \le p-2$.

**Why computationally hard:**
1. The function $f(x) = \alpha^x \bmod p$ is a **one-way function** — easy to compute forward via square-and-multiply in $O(\log x \cdot \log^2 p)$ bit operations.
2. The inverse operation requires solving the DLP. Brute force needs $O(p)$ operations.
3. Best known classical algorithms (Index Calculus, Number Field Sieve) achieve only **sub-exponential** time $O(e^{c \cdot \sqrt{\log p \cdot \log \log p}})$.
4. For cryptographically large primes ($p \ge 2048$ bits), this remains **computationally infeasible** even for nation-state adversaries with current technology.
5. The security of Diffie–Hellman, ElGamal, and DSA schemes is fundamentally rooted in this hardness assumption.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**State and prove the multiplicative property of discrete logarithms.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** If $x_1 = \log_{\alpha} \beta_1$ and $x_2 = \log_{\alpha} \beta_2$, then $\log_{\alpha}(\beta_1 \beta_2) \equiv x_1 + x_2 \pmod{p-1}$.

**Proof:**

By definition:
$$\alpha^{x_1} \equiv \beta_1 \pmod{p} \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha^{x_2} \equiv \beta_2 \pmod{p}$$

Multiply both congruences:
$$\alpha^{x_1} \cdot \alpha^{x_2} \equiv \beta_1 \cdot \beta_2 \pmod{p}$$

Using exponent law $\alpha^a \cdot \alpha^b = \alpha^{a+b}$:
$$\alpha^{x_1 + x_2} \equiv \beta_1 \cdot \beta_2 \pmod{p}$$

By the uniqueness of the discrete logarithm:
$$\log_{\alpha}(\beta_1 \beta_2) \equiv x_1 + x_2 \pmod{p-1}$$

The reduction is modulo $p-1$ because $\alpha^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ by Fermat's Little Theorem. ∎

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]` — 14 Marks

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compute Discrete Logarithm Using the Properties

Given prime $p = 41$, generator $\alpha = 6$, and the following known values:
- $\log_{6} 10 \equiv 16 \pmod{40}$
- $\log_{6} 13 \equiv 7 \pmod{40}$

Compute the following without brute force:
1. $\log_{6}(10 \cdot 13) \pmod{40}$
2. $\log_{6}(10^{5}) \pmod{40}$
3. $\log_{6}(10 / 13) \pmod{40}$  *(use the fact that $13 \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{41}$ has solution $x = 19$, since $13 \cdot 19 = 247 = 6 \cdot 41 + 1$)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1:** By multiplicative property:
$$\log_{6}(10 \cdot 13) \equiv \log_{6} 10 + \log_{6} 13 \equiv 16 + 7 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$$

So $\log_{6}(10 \cdot 13) \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$.

Verification: $10 \cdot 13 = 130 \equiv 130 - 3(41) = 7 \pmod{41}$. Hence $\log_{6} 7 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$.

**Part 2:** By power rule with $k = 5$:
$$\log_{6}(10^{5}) \equiv 5 \cdot \log_{6} 10 \equiv 5 \cdot 16 \equiv 80 \equiv 80 - 2(40) = 0 \pmod{40}$$

So $\log_{6}(10^{5}) \equiv 0 \pmod{40}$. This means $10^5 \equiv 1 \pmod{41}$, which by Fermat's Little Theorem is true since $\gcd(10, 41) = 1$.

**Part 3:** Using multiplicative property on $10 = 13 \cdot (10 \cdot 13^{-1})$ where $13^{-1} \equiv 19 \pmod{41}$:
$$\log_{6} 10 \equiv \log_{6} 13 + \log_{6}(13^{-1}) \pmod{40}$$
$$16 \equiv 7 + \log_{6} 19 \pmod{40}$$
$$\log_{6} 19 \equiv 16 - 7 \equiv 9 \pmod{40}$$

So $\log_{6}(10 / 13) \equiv 9 \pmod{40}$.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Demonstrate Brute-Force DLP Computation

Given $p = 23$, $\alpha = 5$, $\beta = 13$, find $x = \log_{5} 13 \pmod{22}$.

**Step 1 — Verify 5 is a primitive root mod 23.**
Order of group is $22 = 2 \cdot 11$. Check $5^{22/2} = 5^{11}$ and $5^{22/11} = 5^{2}$ modulo 23.

$$5^{2} = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$

Since $5^{2} \neq 1$, 5 is a primitive root mod 23. ✓

**Step 2 — Build powers table.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{1} &\equiv 5 \pmod{23} \\
5^{2} &\equiv 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23} \\
5^{3} &\equiv 2 \cdot 5 = 10 \pmod{23} \\
5^{4} &\equiv 10 \cdot 5 = 50 \equiv 50 - 2(23) = 4 \pmod{23} \\
5^{5} &\equiv 4 \cdot 5 = 20 \pmod{23} \\
5^{6} &\equiv 20 \cdot 5 = 100 \equiv 100 - 4(23) = 8 \pmod{23} \\
5^{7} &\equiv 8 \cdot 5 = 40 \equiv 40 - 23 = 17 \pmod{23} \\
5^{8} &\equiv 17 \cdot 5 = 85 \equiv 85 - 3(23) = 16 \pmod{23} \\
5^{9} &\equiv 16 \cdot 5 = 80 \equiv 80 - 3(23) = 11 \pmod{23} \\
5^{10} &\equiv 11 \cdot 5 = 55 \equiv 55 - 2(23) = 9 \pmod{23} \\
5^{11} &\equiv 9 \cdot 5 = 45 \equiv 45 - 23 = 22 \equiv -1 \pmod{23} \\
5^{12} &\equiv -1 \cdot 5 = -5 \equiv 18 \pmod{23} \\
5^{13} &\equiv 18 \cdot 5 = 90 \equiv 90 - 3(23) = 21 \pmod{23} \\
5^{14} &\equiv 21 \cdot 5 = 105 \equiv 105 - 4(23) = 13 \pmod{23} \quad \longleftarrow \text{MATCH!}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Result:** $x = \log_{5} 13 \equiv 14 \pmod{22}$.

**Verification:** $5^{14} \equiv 13 \pmod{23}$ ✓.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]` — 14 Marks

#### (a) [7 Marks] Solve a Direct Computation and Apply Forward Cryptographic Direction

In a Diffie–Hellman key exchange setup, Alice and Bob publicly agree on $p = 467$, $\alpha = 2$.

1. Alice chooses private key $a = 5$. Compute her public key $A = \alpha^a \bmod p$.
2. Bob chooses private key $b = 9$. Compute his public key $B = \alpha^b \bmod p$.
3. Verify that both compute the **same shared secret** $K = A^b \equiv B^a \pmod{p}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Alice's public key:**
$$A = 2^{5} \bmod 467 = 32 \bmod 467 = 32$$

**Step 2 — Compute Bob's public key:**
$$B = 2^{9} \bmod 467 = 512 \bmod 467 = 512 - 467 = 45$$

**Step 3 — Compute Alice's view of shared secret:**
$$K_{A} = B^{a} \bmod 467 = 45^{5} \bmod 467$$

Compute step by step:
$$45^{2} = 2025, \quad 2025 \bmod 467 = 2025 - 4(467) = 2025 - 1868 = 157$$
$$45^{4} = 157^{2} = 24649, \quad 24649 \bmod 467 = 24649 - 52(467) = 24649 - 24284 = 365$$
$$45^{5} = 365 \cdot 45 = 16425, \quad 16425 \bmod 467 = 16425 - 35(467) = 16425 - 16345 = 80$$

So $K_{A} = 80$.

**Step 4 — Compute Bob's view of shared secret:**
$$K_{B} = A^{b} \bmod 467 = 32^{9} \bmod 467$$

$$32^{2} = 1024 \bmod 467 = 1024 - 2(467) = 90$$
$$32^{4} = 90^{2} = 8100 \bmod 467 = 8100 - 17(467) = 8100 - 7939 = 161$$
$$32^{8} = 161^{2} = 25921 \bmod 467 = 25921 - 55(467) = 25921 - 25685 = 236$$
$$32^{9} = 236 \cdot 32 = 7552 \bmod 467 = 7552 - 16(467) = 7552 - 7472 = 80$$

So $K_{B} = 80$. ✓

**Result:** $K_A = K_B = 80$. Both parties share the same secret **without ever transmitting it**.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Analyze the Security Argument Based on DLP

**(i)** Explain why an eavesdropper who intercepts $p, \alpha, A, B$ cannot compute the shared secret in polynomial time.

**(ii)** What is the minimum prime size $p$ recommended for 128-bit security? Justify.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Security Argument:**
The eavesdropper observes $p = 467$, $\alpha = 2$, $A = 32$, $B = 45$.

To compute $K = \alpha^{ab} \bmod p$, the attacker would need to recover either $a$ from $A = \alpha^a$ or $b$ from $B = \alpha^b$. Both are instances of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.

The brute-force search space is $O(p)$ in the worst case, but for cryptographically sized $p$ (≥ 2048 bits), the search space is astronomically large. The best known classical algorithm (Number Field Sieve) has sub-exponential complexity $L_p[1/3, c]$, which still exceeds $2^{128}$ operations for 3072-bit primes.

Hence, computing the shared secret without knowing $a$ or $b$ is **computationally infeasible**.

**(ii) Recommended Prime Size:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **128-bit security** in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, NIST recommends a prime $p$ of at least **3072 bits**.

Justification: The Number Field Sieve has complexity approximately $L_p[1/3, 1.923] = e^{1.923 \cdot (\ln p)^{1/3} \cdot (\ln \ln p)^{2/3}}$. To ensure this exceeds $2^{128}$ operations, we need $p \ge 2^{3072}$. Equivalently, ECC over a 256-bit curve achieves the same 128-bit security because the Elliptic Curve DLP (ECDLP) has fully exponential complexity $O(\sqrt{q})$ under Pollard's rho, allowing much smaller parameters.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing the DL modulus as $p$ instead of $p-1$. **Marks deducted: 1–2.**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to reduce the final answer modulo $p-1$ in properties (multiplicative, power). **Marks deducted: 1.**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the order of $\alpha$ with $p$. The group order is $p-1$, NOT $p$. **Marks deducted: 2.**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In brute-force tables, skipping intermediate steps or writing wrong modular reductions. **Marks deducted: 1 per wrong step.**
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Claiming "DLP is unbreakable" — DLP is **computationally hard**, not mathematically impossible. **Marks deducted: 1.**
> - **Common Mistake 6:** In Diffie–Hellman, students forget to verify $K_A = K_B$. Always verify both sides. **Marks deducted: 1.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Discrete Logarithms**

- [ ] Discrete logarithm $\log_{\alpha}\beta \bmod p$ is defined only when $\alpha$ is a **generator (primitive root)** of the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$.
- [ ] The modulus for the **result** of a discrete logarithm is **$p-1$** (NOT $p$), since $\alpha^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.
- [ ] Forward computation ($\alpha^x \bmod p$) is **easy** — use **square-and-multiply** in $O(\log x)$ multiplications.
- [ ] Inverse computation ($\log_{\alpha}\beta$) is **hard** — brute force $O(p)$, best attack sub-exponential.
- [ ] **Multiplicative property:** $\log_{\alpha}(\beta\gamma) \equiv \log_{\alpha}\beta + \log_{\alpha}\gamma \pmod{p-1}$.
- [ ] **Power rule:** $\log_{\alpha}(\beta^k) \equiv k \cdot \log_{\alpha}\beta \pmod{p-1}$.
- [ ] **Fermat's Little Theorem:** $\alpha^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ for $\gcd(\alpha, p) = 1$.
- [ ] **Euler's Theorem:** $\alpha^{\phi(p)} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$; for prime $p$, $\phi(p) = p-1$.
- [ ] **Security:** Use $p \ge 3072$ bits for 128-bit security in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, or 256-bit elliptic curves for equivalent security.
- [ ] **Real-world use:** Diffie–Hellman, ElGamal, DSA — all rely on DLP hardness.
- [ ] Always **reduce intermediate values** modulo $p$ (during exponentiation) and modulo $p-1$ (during logarithm manipulation).
- [ ] The DLP is a **one-way function**: trivial forward, infeasible backward, with a **trapdoor** (the secret key $x$).
- [ ] Brute-force DLP table should be presented systematically for partial marks even if not fully completed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chinese Remainder Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chinese Remainder Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT):** Let $n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k$ be positive integers that are **pairwise relatively prime** (i.e., $\gcd(n_i, n_j) = 1$ for all $i \neq j$). Then for any given integers $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k$, the system of simultaneous congruences
> $$x \equiv a_1 \pmod{n_1}, \quad x \equiv a_2 \pmod{n_2}, \quad \dots, \quad x \equiv a_k \pmod{n_k}$$
> has a **unique solution modulo** $N = n_1 n_2 \cdots n_k$, meaning there exists a unique integer $x_0 \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$ satisfying all $k$ congruences simultaneously.

The theorem guarantees both **existence** (at least one solution) and **uniqueness** (exactly one solution within the residue class modulo $N$). The condition of pairwise coprimality is mathematically necessary — without it, the system may either have zero solutions or infinitely many.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three Clocks" Intuition:**
> Imagine a room containing three broken analog clocks. Clock A completes one full revolution every **3 hours**, Clock B every **5 hours**, and Clock C every **7 hours**. All three clocks started ticking at time $t = 0$.
> You walk into the room and observe: Clock A shows **2 hours past**, Clock B shows **3 hours past**, and Clock C shows **2 hours past**.
> **Question:** *What time is it?*
> You need to find a single value $x$ such that:
> - $x \equiv 2 \pmod 3$
> - $x \equiv 3 \pmod 5$
> - $x \equiv 2 \pmod 7$
> CRT tells you that because the moduli $\{3, 5, 7\}$ are pairwise coprime, there is **exactly one answer** in the range $[0, 105)$. The theorem not only promises that an answer exists, but gives you a *systematic recipe* to compute it — much like how a master locksmith can reconstruct a single "master" key from multiple partial key fragments.

## 1.3 Physical & Cryptographic Significance

- **Modular product size:** $N = n_1 n_2 \cdots n_k$ is the *effective modulus*.
- **Pairwise coprime condition:** $\gcd(n_i, n_j) = 1$ for every distinct pair $(i, j)$.
- **Cryptographic applications include RSA decryption speedup, secret sharing (Asmuth–Bloom), and the Pohlig–Hellman discrete logarithm algorithm.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How CRT Reconstructs the Solution

The CRT construction is a *reconstruction kernel* that combines partial residues into one. The logic unfolds in **four precise stages**:

**Stage 1 — Verify the Pairwise Coprime Condition:**
Check that $\gcd(n_i, n_j) = 1$ for all $i \neq j$. If violated, the theorem does not apply directly.

**Stage 2 — Compute the Master Modulus:**
$N = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$. This is the size of the solution space.

**Stage 3 — Compute Partial Moduli and Multiplicative Inverses:**
For each index $i$:
- Compute the *partial product* $N_i = N / n_i$.
- Compute the *modular inverse* $y_i \equiv N_i^{-1} \pmod{n_i}$, i.e., the integer satisfying $N_i \cdot y_i \equiv 1 \pmod{n_i}$. This inverse exists **because** $\gcd(N_i, n_i) = 1$ (a consequence of the pairwise coprime condition).

**Stage 4 — Reconstruct the Solution via the Master Formula:**
$$x \equiv \sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i \cdot N_i \cdot y_i \pmod{N}$$
This weighted sum, reduced modulo $N$, is the unique CRT solution.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind the Reconstruction:**
> Each term $a_i \cdot N_i \cdot y_i$ is engineered so that when reduced modulo $n_j$:
> - If $i = j$: $\ N_i \cdot y_i \equiv 1 \pmod{n_i}$, so the term contributes $a_i \pmod{n_i}$.
> - If $i \neq j$: $\ N_i$ contains $n_j$ as a factor, so the term vanishes to $0 \pmod{n_j}$.
> This "selective contribution" is what makes the formula magically satisfy all $k$ congruences simultaneously.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Meaning | Mathematical Statement |
|---|---|---|
| $n_i$ | $i$-th pairwise coprime modulus | $\gcd(n_i, n_j) = 1, \ \forall i \neq j$ |
| $a_i$ | $i$-th given residue | $0 \leq a_i < n_i$ |
| $N$ | Master modulus (CRT product) | $N = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ |
| $N_i$ | Partial product (excluding $n_i$) | $N_i = N / n_i$ |
| $y_i$ | Modular inverse of $N_i$ mod $n_i$ | $N_i \cdot y_i \equiv 1 \pmod{n_i}$ |
| $x$ | CRT reconstructed solution | $x \equiv \sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i N_i y_i \pmod{N}$ |
| Uniqueness range | Canonical residue range | $0 \leq x < N$ |
| Existence condition | Pairwise coprimality | $\gcd(n_i, n_j) = 1$ |
| Garner's recurrence | Alternative recursive form | $x_k = a_k + n_k \cdot (x_{k-1} - a_k) \cdot (n_k^{-1} \bmod n_{k-1})$ |
| Extended Euler | Inverse computation tool | $y_i \equiv N_i^{\phi(n_i) - 1} \pmod{n_i}$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RSA Decryption Speedup:** RSA with moduli $p, q$ allows CRT to reconstruct $x \bmod n$ from $x \bmod p$ and $x \bmod q$ — giving a $\approx 4\times$ speedup.
- **Secret Sharing Schemes:** Asmuth–Bloom's secret sharing uses CRT to recover a secret from threshold-based shares.
- **Error-Correcting Codes:** Reed–Solomon and certain interleaved codes use CRT-like residue reconstructions.
- **Pohlig–Hellman Algorithm:** Computes discrete logarithms modulo composite $N$ by reducing to prime-power subgroups.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The Three Clocks Problem

> **Problem:** Solve the simultaneous system:
> $$x \equiv 2 \pmod{3}, \quad x \equiv 3 \pmod{5}, \quad x \equiv 2 \pmod{7}$$

### Step 1: Verify Pairwise Coprimality

Check each pair:

- $\gcd(3, 5) = 1$ ✓
- $\gcd(3, 7) = 1$ ✓
- $\gcd(5, 7) = 1$ ✓

Since all pairs are coprime, CRT applies.

### Step 2: Compute the Master Modulus

$$N = 3 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 = 105$$

### Step 3: Compute Partial Products $N_i$

$$N_1 = \frac{N}{n_1} = \frac{105}{3} = 35$$

$$N_2 = \frac{N}{n_2} = \frac{105}{5} = 21$$

$$N_3 = \frac{N}{n_3} = \frac{105}{7} = 15$$

### Step 4: Compute Modular Inverses $y_i = N_i^{-1} \pmod{n_i}$

**Inverse $y_1 = 35^{-1} \pmod{3}$:**

$$35 \equiv 35 - 11 \cdot 3 = 35 - 33 = 2 \pmod{3}$$

So we need $y_1$ such that $2 y_1 \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$. By inspection: $y_1 = 2$ since $2 \cdot 2 = 4 \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$.

$$y_1 = 2$$

**Inverse $y_2 = 21^{-1} \pmod{5}$:**

$$21 \equiv 21 - 4 \cdot 5 = 21 - 20 = 1 \pmod{5}$$

So $1 \cdot y_2 \equiv 1 \pmod{5}$, giving $y_2 = 1$.

$$y_2 = 1$$

**Inverse $y_3 = 15^{-1} \pmod{7}$:**

$$15 \equiv 15 - 2 \cdot 7 = 15 - 14 = 1 \pmod{7}$$

So $1 \cdot y_3 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$, giving $y_3 = 1$.

$$y_3 = 1$$

### Step 5: Apply the Master Reconstruction Formula

$$x \equiv \sum_{i=1}^{3} a_i N_i y_i \pmod{N}$$

$$x \equiv (a_1 N_1 y_1) + (a_2 N_2 y_2) + (a_3 N_3 y_3) \pmod{105}$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$x \equiv (2 \cdot 35 \cdot 2) + (3 \cdot 21 \cdot 1) + (2 \cdot 15 \cdot 1) \pmod{105}$$

Evaluate each product:

$$x \equiv 140 + 63 + 30 \pmod{105}$$

$$x \equiv 233 \pmod{105}$$

### Step 6: Reduce to the Canonical Range $[0, 105)$

$$233 = 2 \cdot 105 + 23 = 210 + 23$$

$$233 - 210 = 23$$

$$x = 23$$

### Step 7: Verification

Confirm each congruence holds for $x = 23$:

- $23 = 7 \cdot 3 + 2 \Rightarrow 23 \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$ ✓
- $23 = 4 \cdot 5 + 3 \Rightarrow 23 \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$ ✓
- $23 = 3 \cdot 7 + 2 \Rightarrow 23 \equiv 2 \pmod{7}$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Solution:** The unique solution in $[0, 105)$ is $\boxed{x = 23}$.

## 3.2 Second Analytical Example — A 4-Modulus System

> **Problem:** Solve
> $$x \equiv 1 \pmod{4}, \quad x \equiv 2 \pmod{9}, \quad x \equiv 3 \pmod{5}, \quad x \equiv 0 \pmod{7}$$

### Step 1: Verify Pairwise Coprimality

- $\gcd(4, 9) = 1$ ✓
- $\gcd(4, 5) = 1$ ✓
- $\gcd(4, 7) = 1$ ✓
- $\gcd(9, 5) = 1$ ✓
- $\gcd(9, 7) = 1$ ✓
- $\gcd(5, 7) = 1$ ✓

All six pairs coprime — CRT applies.

### Step 2: Master Modulus

$$N = 4 \cdot 9 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 = 1260$$

### Step 3: Partial Products

$$N_1 = 1260 / 4 = 315$$
$$N_2 = 1260 / 9 = 140$$
$$N_3 = 1260 / 5 = 252$$
$$N_4 = 1260 / 7 = 180$$

### Step 4: Modular Inverses

**$y_1 = 315^{-1} \pmod{4}$:**

$315 \equiv 315 - 78 \cdot 4 = 315 - 312 = 3 \pmod{4}$, so $3 y_1 \equiv 1 \pmod 4 \Rightarrow y_1 = 3$ (since $3 \cdot 3 = 9 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$).

**$y_2 = 140^{-1} \pmod{9}$:**

$140 \equiv 140 - 15 \cdot 9 = 140 - 135 = 5 \pmod 9$, so $5 y_2 \equiv 1 \pmod 9 \Rightarrow y_2 = 2$ (since $5 \cdot 2 = 10 \equiv 1 \pmod 9$).

**$y_3 = 252^{-1} \pmod{5}$:**

$252 \equiv 252 - 50 \cdot 5 = 252 - 250 = 2 \pmod 5$, so $2 y_3 \equiv 1 \pmod 5 \Rightarrow y_3 = 3$ (since $2 \cdot 3 = 6 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$).

**$y_4 = 180^{-1} \pmod{7}$:**

$180 \equiv 180 - 25 \cdot 7 = 180 - 175 = 5 \pmod 7$, so $5 y_4 \equiv 1 \pmod 7 \Rightarrow y_4 = 3$ (since $5 \cdot 3 = 15 \equiv 1 \pmod 7$).

### Step 5: Reconstruct via Master Formula

$$x \equiv (1 \cdot 315 \cdot 3) + (2 \cdot 140 \cdot 2) + (3 \cdot 252 \cdot 3) + (0 \cdot 180 \cdot 3) \pmod{1260}$$

$$x \equiv 945 + 560 + 2268 + 0 \pmod{1260}$$

$$x \equiv 3773 \pmod{1260}$$

Reduce: $3773 = 2 \cdot 1260 + 1253 = 2520 + 1253$, so $3773 - 2520 = 1253$.

$$x = 1253$$

### Step 6: Verification

- $1253 = 313 \cdot 4 + 1 \Rightarrow 1253 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$ ✓
- $1253 = 139 \cdot 9 + 2 \Rightarrow 1253 \equiv 2 \pmod 9$ ✓
- $1253 = 250 \cdot 5 + 3 \Rightarrow 1253 \equiv 3 \pmod 5$ ✓
- $1253 = 179 \cdot 7 + 0 \Rightarrow 1253 \equiv 0 \pmod 7$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Solution:** $\boxed{x = 1253}$ (unique in $[0, 1260)$).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Garner's Algorithm (Recursive CRT)

For efficiency, Garner's algorithm avoids computing modular inverses at every step:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= a_1 \\
x_2 &= a_2 + n_2 \cdot ((a_1 - a_2) \cdot (n_2^{-1} \bmod n_1)) \bmod n_1 n_2 \\
x_3 &= a_3 + n_3 \cdot ((x_2 - a_3) \cdot (n_3^{-1} \bmod (n_1 n_2))) \bmod n_1 n_2 n_3 \\
&\vdots \\
x_k &= a_k + n_k \cdot ((x_{k-1} - a_k) \cdot (n_k^{-1} \bmod (n_1 \cdots n_{k-1}))) \bmod N
\end{aligned}$$

Garner's method is preferred in cryptographic hardware implementations because it requires only **one inversion** per step instead of $k$ inversions overall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CRT Reconstruction Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: System of k congruences] --> B{Verify pairwise coprimality}
    B -- "All gcd ni,nj = 1" --> C[Compute Master Modulus N = product of all ni]
    B -- "Not coprime" --> BX[STOP: CRT not directly applicable]
    C --> D[For each i: compute partial product Ni = N divided by ni]
    D --> E[For each i: compute inverse yi such that Ni times yi congruent 1 mod ni]
    E --> F[Apply master formula x equals sum of ai times Ni times yi]
    F --> G[Reduce x modulo N to canonical range 0 to N minus 1]
    G --> H[OUTPUT: Unique solution x in range 0 to N minus 1]
    H --> I[Verification: check each congruence holds]
    I -- "All satisfied" --> J[SOLUTION CONFIRMED]
    I -- "Mismatch" --> K[ERROR: Recompute inverses]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — When CRT Applies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Given system of congruences] --> Q1{Is the number of congruences k >= 2?}
    Q1 -- No --> Out1[Not a CRT problem; trivial]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Are all moduli ni strictly positive?}
    Q2 -- No --> Out2[Invalid input]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is gcd of every pair equal to 1?}
    Q3 -- No --> Out3[CRT requires reformulation or generalized CRT]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Apply[Apply CRT reconstruction formula]
    Apply --> Done[Unique solution in range 0 to N minus 1]
```

## 4.3 Garner vs Standard CRT — Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG[STANDARD CRT]
        A1[Compute all Ni] --> B1[Compute all inverses yi]
        B1 --> C1[Single sum formula]
        C1 --> D1[Final reduction mod N]
    end
    subgraph GG[GARNER ALGORITHM]
        A2[Recursive accumulation] --> B2[Compute one inverse per step]
        B2 --> C2[Iterative modular arithmetic]
        C2 --> D2[Final reconstruction]
    end
    SG -.->|Higher upfront cost| Compare[Performance tradeoff]
    GG -.->|Lower per-step cost| Compare
    Compare --> Choice{Choose based on k and hardware}
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** State the Chinese Remainder Theorem. What is the necessary condition for the moduli?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Chinese Remainder Theorem states that if $n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k$ are **pairwise coprime** positive integers, then the system of simultaneous congruences
$$x \equiv a_i \pmod{n_i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, k$$
has a **unique solution modulo** $N = n_1 n_2 \cdots n_k$. The necessary condition is that $\gcd(n_i, n_j) = 1$ for every pair $i \neq j$. *(1 mark — statement; 1 mark — uniqueness modulo N; 1 mark — pairwise coprime condition.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> **Q:** List any **two** cryptographic applications of CRT.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **RSA decryption acceleration** — CRT decomposes $x^d \bmod n$ into two smaller exponentiations mod $p$ and mod $q$, then reconstructs the result, giving roughly a $4\times$ speedup. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Pohlig–Hellman discrete logarithm algorithm** — Reduces $\log_g h \bmod p$ (for composite $p$) into subproblems over prime-power factors, each solved individually and combined via CRT. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common mistake:** Students **forget to verify pairwise coprimality** before applying the formula — leading to a wrong solution space and an invalid answer. Always do this *first*.
> - **Second most common mistake:** Forgetting to reduce the **partial products** $N_i$ modulo $n_i$ before computing inverses (e.g., treating $315$ as the residue instead of $315 \bmod 4 = 3$).
> - **Third common mistake:** Not reducing the final answer into the canonical range $[0, N)$; partial credit is **lost** if the final answer is not normalized.
> - **Always** show the verification step — it often earns a "free" mark even if intermediate steps have small arithmetic errors.

---

### **Question B — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

#### (a) Solve the simultaneous system using CRT (7 marks):
$$x \equiv 4 \pmod{5}, \quad x \equiv 5 \pmod{11}, \quad x \equiv 7 \pmod{13}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify coprimality:**

$\gcd(5, 11) = 1$, $\gcd(5, 13) = 1$, $\gcd(11, 13) = 1$ ✓ *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Master modulus:**

$$N = 5 \cdot 11 \cdot 13 = 715 \quad \text{*(1 mark)*}$$

**Step 3 — Partial products:**

$$N_1 = \frac{715}{5} = 143, \quad N_2 = \frac{715}{11} = 65, \quad N_3 = \frac{715}{13} = 55 \quad \text{*(1 mark)*}$$

**Step 4 — Compute inverses:**

- $y_1 = 143^{-1} \bmod 5$: $143 = 28 \cdot 5 + 3 \Rightarrow 143 \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$. Need $3 y_1 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$. By inspection: $y_1 = 2$ (since $3 \cdot 2 = 6 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$). ✓ *(1 mark)*
- $y_2 = 65^{-1} \bmod 11$: $65 = 5 \cdot 11 + 10 \Rightarrow 65 \equiv 10 \pmod{11}$. Need $10 y_2 \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$. Since $10 \equiv -1$, then $y_2 = -1 \equiv 10 \pmod{11}$. ✓ *(1 mark)*
- $y_3 = 55^{-1} \bmod 13$: $55 = 4 \cdot 13 + 3 \Rightarrow 55 \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$. Need $3 y_3 \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$. Since $3 \cdot 9 = 27 \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$, $y_3 = 9$. ✓ *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Master formula:**

$$x \equiv (4 \cdot 143 \cdot 2) + (5 \cdot 65 \cdot 10) + (7 \cdot 55 \cdot 9) \pmod{715}$$

$$x \equiv 1144 + 3250 + 3465 \pmod{715}$$

$$x \equiv 7859 \pmod{715}$$

**Step 6 — Final reduction:**

$7859 = 10 \cdot 715 + 609 = 7150 + 709$. Wait, recompute: $7859 / 715 \approx 10.99$, so $10 \cdot 715 = 7150$, and $7859 - 7150 = 709$. So $x \equiv 709 \pmod{715}$. ✓ *(1 mark)*

**Verification:** $709 = 141 \cdot 5 + 4 \Rightarrow 709 \equiv 4 \pmod 5$ ✓; $709 = 64 \cdot 11 + 5 \Rightarrow 709 \equiv 5 \pmod{11}$ ✓; $709 = 54 \cdot 13 + 7 \Rightarrow 709 \equiv 7 \pmod{13}$ ✓.

**Answer:** $\boxed{x = 709}$

---

#### (b) Use Garner's algorithm to solve the same system above (7 marks):

**Model Solution:**

Garner's recurrence:

$$x_1 = a_1 = 4$$

For $i = 2$:

$$x_2 = a_2 + n_2 \cdot ((x_1 - a_2) \cdot (n_2^{-1} \bmod n_1)) \bmod (n_1 n_2)$$

Compute $n_2^{-1} \bmod n_1 = 11^{-1} \bmod 5$: $11 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$, so $1 \cdot y \equiv 1 \pmod 5 \Rightarrow y = 1$. *(1 mark)*

$$x_2 = 5 + 11 \cdot ((4 - 5) \cdot 1) \bmod 55 = 5 + 11 \cdot (-1) = -6 \equiv 49 \pmod{55}$$ *(1 mark)*

For $i = 3$:

Compute $n_3^{-1} \bmod (n_1 n_2) = 13^{-1} \bmod 55$. Extended Euclidean: $\gcd(13, 55)$: $55 = 4 \cdot 13 + 3$, $13 = 4 \cdot 3 + 1$, so $1 = 13 - 4 \cdot 3 = 13 - 4(55 - 4 \cdot 13) = 17 \cdot 13 - 4 \cdot 55$. Thus $13^{-1} \equiv 17 \pmod{55}$. *(2 marks)*

$$x_3 = 7 + 13 \cdot ((49 - 7) \cdot 17) \bmod 715$$

$$x_3 = 7 + 13 \cdot (42 \cdot 17) = 7 + 13 \cdot 714 = 7 + 9282 = 9289$$

$9289 \bmod 715$: $9289 / 715 \approx 12.99$, $12 \cdot 715 = 8580$, $9289 - 8580 = 709$. ✓ *(2 marks)*

**Answer:** $x = 709$ — matches Choice A. *(1 mark for verification/matching)*

---

### **Question B — Choice B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`

#### (a) Using CRT, find $x$ satisfying (7 marks):
$$x \equiv 3 \pmod{8}, \quad x \equiv 1 \pmod{9}, \quad x \equiv 5 \pmod{5}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify coprimality:**

$\gcd(8, 9) = 1$ ✓, $\gcd(8, 5) = 1$ ✓, $\gcd(9, 5) = 1$ ✓ *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Master modulus:**

$$N = 8 \cdot 9 \cdot 5 = 360 \quad \text{*(1 mark)*}$$

**Step 3 — Partial products:**

$$N_1 = 45, \quad N_2 = 40, \quad N_3 = 72 \quad \text{*(1 mark)*}$$

**Step 4 — Inverses:**

- $y_1 = 45^{-1} \bmod 8$: $45 \equiv 5 \pmod 8$. $5 y_1 \equiv 1 \pmod 8 \Rightarrow y_1 = 5$ (since $5 \cdot 5 = 25 \equiv 1$). ✓ *(1 mark)*
- $y_2 = 40^{-1} \bmod 9$: $40 \equiv 4 \pmod 9$. $4 y_2 \equiv 1 \pmod 9 \Rightarrow y_2 = 7$ (since $4 \cdot 7 = 28 \equiv 1$). ✓ *(1 mark)*
- $y_3 = 72^{-1} \bmod 5$: $72 \equiv 2 \pmod 5$. $2 y_3 \equiv 1 \pmod 5 \Rightarrow y_3 = 3$ (since $2 \cdot 3 = 6 \equiv 1$). ✓ *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Master formula:**

$$x \equiv (3 \cdot 45 \cdot 5) + (1 \cdot 40 \cdot 7) + (5 \cdot 72 \cdot 3) \pmod{360}$$

$$x \equiv 675 + 280 + 1080 \pmod{360}$$

$$x \equiv 2035 \pmod{360}$$

$2035 = 5 \cdot 360 + 235 = 1800 + 235$. So $x = 235$. ✓ *(1 mark)*

**Verification:** $235 = 29 \cdot 8 + 3 \Rightarrow 235 \equiv 3 \pmod 8$ ✓; $235 = 26 \cdot 9 + 1 \Rightarrow 235 \equiv 1 \pmod 9$ ✓; $235 = 47 \cdot 5 + 0$. Wait! $235 \bmod 5 = 0$, not $5$. **ERROR!** 

⚠️ Let me recheck: $a_3 = 5$ but we need $a_3 < n_3$. Since $5 \bmod 5 = 0$, the residue $a_3$ should be **reduced first** to $0$. The problem as stated has $a_3 = 5$ which is equivalent to $a_3 \equiv 0 \pmod 5$. So the correct solution is for $x \equiv 0 \pmod 5$:

Recompute with $a_3 = 0$: $x \equiv 675 + 280 + 0 = 955 \pmod{360}$. $955 = 2 \cdot 360 + 235$. So $x = 235$. Then $235 \equiv 0 \pmod 5$ ✓. ✓

**Answer:** $\boxed{x = 235}$

---

#### (b) Explain the cryptographic application of CRT in **RSA decryption speedup** (7 marks):

**Model Answer:**

In RSA, the modulus $n = p \cdot q$ is a product of two large primes. To decrypt a ciphertext $c$, one computes $m = c^d \bmod n$. Direct computation requires a full modular exponentiation modulo $n$ (a ~1024 to 4096-bit number). *(1 mark)*

Using CRT, Bob (who knows $p$ and $q$) splits the computation:

1. Compute $m_p = c^{d \bmod (p-1)} \bmod p$ *(1 mark)*
2. Compute $m_q = c^{d \bmod (q-1)} \bmod q$ *(1 mark)*

Each exponentiation is over a modulus roughly **half the bit-length** of $n$. Modular exponentiation cost is $O(k^3)$ where $k$ is the bit-length, so reducing the modulus by half reduces each operation to $\approx 1/8$ of the original cost. Total: $2 \cdot (1/8) = 1/4$ of original cost — a **4× speedup**. *(2 marks)*

The original message is then reconstructed via CRT:

$$m \equiv m_p \pmod{p}, \quad m \equiv m_q \pmod{q}$$

Applying the CRT formula:

$$m = m_q + q \cdot \left((m_p - m_q) \cdot (q^{-1} \bmod p)\right) \bmod n$$

This recovers $m$ uniquely. *(2 marks)*

This technique is standard in **OpenSSL, GnuPG, and most production RSA implementations** and is one of the most practical applications of CRT in modern cryptography.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CRT statement:** Pairwise coprime moduli $\Rightarrow$ unique solution mod $N = \prod n_i$.
- **Master formula:** $x = \sum a_i N_i y_i \bmod N$ where $N_i = N/n_i$ and $N_i y_i \equiv 1 \pmod{n_i}$.
- **Pairwise coprimality is mandatory** — always verify $\gcd(n_i, n_j) = 1$ first.
- **Solution range:** Always report $x \in [0, N)$; otherwise partial credit may be lost.
- **Reduction step:** Reduce each $N_i$ mod $n_i$ before computing its inverse to simplify.
- **Verification is essential:** Plug the final answer back into all $k$ congruences.
- **Garner's algorithm** offers a recursive alternative requiring only one inversion per step — preferred in hardware.
- **Cryptographic relevance:** RSA decryption acceleration ($\approx 4\times$), Pohlig–Hellman DLP, Asmuth–Bloom secret sharing.
- **Uniqueness theorem:** No two distinct solutions in $[0, N)$ exist.
- **Existence theorem:** If pairwise coprime, at least one solution always exists.
- **Generalization:** When moduli are *not* coprime, use the *generalized CRT* (which requires pairwise solvability of linear combinations).
- **Computational cost:** Dominated by $k$ modular inverses via Extended Euclidean Algorithm ($O(k \log^2 N)$ overall).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Security Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **PECST637 – Fundamentals of Cryptography**, a **Security Attack** is formally defined (per ITU-T X.800 / ISO 7498-2 standard adopted by KTU) as:

> *"Any action that compromises the security of information owned by an organization. A security attack is an intelligent threat that is designed to deliberately defeat or bypass the security services of an information system, violating one or more of the fundamental security goals — **Confidentiality**, **Integrity**, **Availability**, **Authentication**, **Non-repudiation**, and **Access Control**."*

In cryptography-specific terms (Stallings' canonical taxonomy referenced by KTU), a security attack is a deliberate mathematical, computational, or protocol-level manoeuvre intended to break a cryptosystem and recover the **plaintext ($P$)**, the **key ($K$)**, or both, from intercepted **ciphertext ($C$)**.

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus maps Security Attacks under **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Apply classical and modern cryptographic techniques to ensure confidentiality, integrity and authentication."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT] Intuitive Analogy — "The Postal Locker System"
> Imagine you and your friend exchange sealed steel lockers through a courier. The locker has a 4-digit numeric combination (the **key**).
>
> - **Passive attack** is like a spy secretly *photographing* the locker dimensions, counting how often couriers arrive, and noting the locker's weight — without ever opening it. He learns *patterns* but not the secret.
> - **Active attack** is like the spy *smashing* the locker with a crowbar, *swapping* your sealed locker with a fake one, or *flooding* the courier office so your locker never arrives.
>
> Cryptography's job is to design the locker (cipher) and combination (key) so that even if the spy is powerful, the contents remain safe. **Security Attack** = everything the spy tries to do to defeat that lock.

> [!NOTE] KTU Board Definition to Memorize
> **Threat** × **Vulnerability** = **Attack**
> A threat becomes an attack only when a vulnerability exists. The KTU examiner expects students to clearly distinguish *threat*, *vulnerability*, and *attack*.

### 1.3 Fundamental Security Goals Violated by Attacks

| Security Goal | Symbol | Attacked By |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality | $C$ | Release of message contents, Traffic analysis |
| Integrity | $I$ | Modification, Replay |
| Availability | $A$ | Denial of Service |
| Authentication | $Auth$ | Masquerade |
| Non-Repudiation | $NR$ | Forged digital signatures |


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The OSI Security Attack Taxonomy (X.800)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly follows the **ITU-T X.800** two-tier classification:

1. **Passive Attacks** — Observation only, do not alter system resources. *Hard to detect, but preventable.*
2. **Active Attacks** — Modification or fabrication of data/services. *Easy to detect, but hard to prevent.*

### 2.2 Passive Attacks — Step-by-Step Logic

Passive attacks are the "eavesdropping" class. They violate **Confidentiality**.

**Step 1: Release of Message Contents**
- The attacker intercepts the ciphertext $C$ on the channel and, through cryptanalysis, recovers the plaintext $P$.
- Confidentiality is *directly* broken.

**Step 2: Traffic Analysis**
- Even when the ciphertext cannot be decrypted (e.g., a strong block cipher), the attacker observes:
  - Frequency of messages
  - Source $\rightarrow$ Destination patterns
  - Message length, timing
- Useful in military/strategic intelligence: *"The embassy sent 47 encrypted bursts between 02:00–04:00 hrs → an attack was being coordinated."*

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Mnemonic — Passive Attacks
> **"RT = Read Traffic"**
> - **R**elease of contents
> - **T**raffic analysis

### 2.3 Active Attacks — Step-by-Step Logic

Active attacks involve *deliberate data alteration, fabrication, or disruption*.

**Step 1: Masquerade**
- An entity pretends to be a different entity. Example: Spoofing the IP address of a legitimate bank server.

**Step 2: Replay**
- The attacker captures a valid transmitted message $M_i$ and retransmits it later (or repeatedly). The receiver, thinking it is fresh, accepts it.

**Step 3: Modification of Messages**
- Some portion of a legitimate message is altered, or messages are delayed/reordered. Violates **Integrity**.

**Step 4: Denial of Service (DoS)**
- The attacker floods the server with bogus requests so legitimate users cannot access resources. Violates **Availability**.
- Distributed variant: **DDoS** (e.g., Mirai botnet, 2016).

> [!NOTE] KTU Mnemonic — Active Attacks
> **"M²RD"** (read as "M-squared-R-D")
> - **M**asquerade
> - **M**odification (of messages)
> - **R**eplay
> - **D**enial of Service

### 2.4 Cryptanalytic Attack Models (Mathematical Perspective)

These are the **four classical attack models** KTU expects students to differentiate for Module 2:

| Attack Model | Attacker Knows | Cryptanalyst's Goal | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA) | Only ciphertext $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_n$ | Find $P_i$ or $K$ | Sniffing Wi-Fi traffic |
| Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA) | Several $(P_i, C_i)$ pairs | Find $K$ | WWII Enigma |
| Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA) | Ciphertexts for plaintexts he *chooses* | Find $K$ | WWII Bombe vs Enigma |
| Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA) | Plaintexts for ciphertexts he *chooses* | Find $K$ | Bleichenbacher on RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5 |

### 2.5 Brute Force & Work Factor

The **Work Factor** ($W$) is the *expected effort* (in time or operations) required to defeat a cryptosystem by exhaustive search.

$$
W \;=\; 2^{k}
$$

where $k$ = effective key length in **bits**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] All formulas below are examinable in ESE & Internal Assessments.

| Formula / Notation | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $W = 2^{k}$ | Work factor for brute-force key search | operations |
| $T_{crack} = \dfrac{2^{k}}{R}$ | Time to break key at rate $R$ | seconds |
| $P(\text{guess}) = \dfrac{1}{2^{k}}$ | Probability of random correct key guess | dimensionless |
| $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_{2} p_i$ | Shannon Entropy of source $X$ (information leaked by traffic analysis) | bits/symbol |
| $R_{sec} = 1 - P_{compromise}$ | Residual security after attack | dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| $\mathbb{E}[C_{break}] = \dfrac{2^{k-1}}{R}$ | Expected cost (average brute-force tries) | operations |
| $\vert K \vert = 2^{k}$ | Key space size | count |
| $C = E_{K}(P)$ | Encryption function (passive attack target) | ciphertext |
| $P = D_{K}(C)$ | Decryption without key = attack success | plaintext |

**Engineering Utility:**
- $W = 2^{k}$ is used by **NIST, NSA, and ISO/IEC 19790** to certify cryptographic modules (FIPS 140-3).
- A key is considered **computationally secure** if $W \geq 2^{80}$ (≈ **1.2 × 10²⁴** operations), requiring more than the age of the universe on classical hardware.
- Shannon's entropy $H(X)$ is the theoretical foundation for **quantifying information leakage** in traffic-analysis attacks, used in modern side-channel research.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Work Factor and Effective Key Length

**Given:** A symmetric cipher uses a key of $k = 128$ bits (e.g., AES-128). An attacker can test $R = 10^{9}$ keys per second (≈ 1 GHz processor).

**To Find:** (a) Total work factor $W$, (b) Expected time to break.

#### Step (a): Compute the Work Factor

By definition, a brute-force attacker must, in the worst case, try all $2^{k}$ keys.

$$
W \;=\; 2^{k}
$$

Substitute $k = 128$:

$$
W \;=\; 2^{128}
$$

Evaluate:

$$
2^{128} \;=\; 3.4028 \times 10^{38}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the total number of candidate keys in the search space. Any cryptanalytic shortcut below this number represents a *successful attack* of severity $(2^{128} / W_{shortcut}) \times$ faster than brute force.

#### Step (b): Expected Time to Break

Average guesses needed = $W / 2 = 2^{127}$ (since on average the correct key is at the midpoint of the search).

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{break}] \;=\; \frac{2^{k-1}}{R}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{break}] \;=\; \frac{2^{127}}{10^{9}} \;=\; \frac{1.7014 \times 10^{38}}{10^{9}} \;=\; 1.7014 \times 10^{29} \text{ seconds}
$$

Convert to years:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{break}] \;=\; \frac{1.7014 \times 10^{29}}{3.154 \times 10^{7}} \;=\; 5.39 \times 10^{21} \text{ years}
$$

> *Logic:* 5.4 sextillion years vastly exceeds the age of the universe (≈ $1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years). Hence AES-128 is **computationally secure** against classical brute force.

### 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Entropy and Traffic-Analysis Information Leak

**Scenario:** A network carries 4 message types with probabilities $p = \{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$ for types A, B, C, D.

**Find:** Information revealed per message (entropy).

$$
H(X) \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_{2} p_i
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\bigl[ 0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= -\bigl[ 0.5(-1) + 0.25(-2) + 0.125(-3) + 0.125(-3) \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= -\bigl[ -0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375 \bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1.75 \text{ bits/message}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Even if the *content* of each message is perfectly encrypted, an attacker performing traffic analysis learns on average **1.75 bits** per message about which type of traffic is flowing. This is the theoretical justification for **traffic-flow confidentiality (TFC)** mechanisms in military networks (e.g., constant-bit-rate padding).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attack Severity vs. Cryptosystem

| Cryptosystem | Key Size $k$ | $W = 2^{k}$ | Brute-Force @ $10^{9}$ keys/s | Vulnerable To (Modern) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DES | 56 bits | $7.2 \times 10^{16}$ | ≈ 1.1 years | COA, exhaustive search |
| 3DES (2-key) | 112 bits | $5.2 \times 10^{33}$ | $1.6 \times 10^{17}$ years | Meet-in-the-middle |
| AES-128 | 128 bits | $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | $1.08 \times 10^{22}$ years | Side-channel (cache-timing) |
| AES-256 | 256 bits | $1.16 \times 10^{77}$ | $3.7 \times 10^{60}$ years | Theoretical quantum (Grover: $2^{128}$) |
| RSA-2048 | 2048 bits (asymmetric) | — | $2^{112}$ (best sub-exponential) | Shor's algorithm on quantum |

> [!NOTE] Interpretation
> Grover's quantum algorithm reduces brute-force work to $2^{k/2}$. Hence AES-256 is the post-quantum safe choice, while AES-128 is only marginally safe.


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    C0 --> C1["Masquerade"]

    C1 -.->|"Violates"| V2["Authentication"]

    ATT -->|"Tampered C"| DEC["Decryption Algorithm D at Receiver"]:::crypto
