<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **passive** and **active** security attacks. Give one example of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
> |---|---|---|
> | Nature | Observation only | Modification/fabrication |
> | Affects system resources? | No | Yes |
> | Detection difficulty | Hard | Easy |
> | Prevention difficulty | Easy | Hard |
> | Example | Traffic analysis on encrypted VPN traffic | Replay attack on banking transaction |
>
> *Mark split:* 1 mark definition each + 1 mark example each (or table format). **Total: 3 marks.**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q2.** Define **Work Factor** of a cryptosystem. A cipher uses a 64-bit key. Calculate the work factor needed for an exhaustive key search.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The *Work Factor* $W$ is the expected computational effort (time × operations) required by an attacker to break a cryptosystem by brute force.
> For a $k$-bit key:
> $W = 2^{k}$
> Substituting $k = 64$:
> $W = 2^{64} = 1.84 \times 10^{19}$ operations.
> *Mark split:* 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark numerical substitution & answer.

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module 2 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Sample Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **OSI security architecture (X.800)** and classify the different types of **passive and active attacks**. Discuss which security goals each attack violates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An organization uses an encryption algorithm with a 56-bit key (e.g., DES). An attacker can attempt $10^{12}$ keys per second using specialized hardware.
   - (i) Compute the work factor $W$. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) Determine the expected time required to break the cipher. **(4 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — 7 marks**

The OSI security architecture (ITU-T X.800) defines a systematic framework for securing OSI-compatible networks. It specifies:
- **Security Attacks** — threats to information
- **Security Mechanisms** — countermeasures (encryption, digital signature, access control, etc.)
- **Security Services** — goals achieved (confidentiality, integrity, etc.)

*Classification table:*

| Type | Sub-Attack | Description | Goal Violated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Passive | Release of contents | Eavesdropper reads message | Confidentiality |
| Passive | Traffic analysis | Observes patterns without decrypting | Confidentiality (partial) |
| Active | Masquerade | Pretends to be another entity | Authentication |
| Active | Replay | Re-transmits captured valid message | Integrity |
| Active | Modification | Alters legitimate message | Integrity |
| Active | Denial of Service | Disrupts service to users | Availability |

*Mark split:* 2 marks diagram + 2 marks passive classification + 2 marks active classification + 1 mark security goal mapping.

**(b) Part (b) Solution — 7 marks**

**(i) Work factor (3 marks):**

$$
W \;=\; 2^{k} \;=\; 2^{56} \;=\; 7.2058 \times 10^{16}
$$

**(ii) Expected break time (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[T_{break}] &= \frac{2^{k-1}}{R} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2^{55}}{10^{12}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.6029 \times 10^{16}}{10^{12}} \\[4pt]
&= 3.6029 \times 10^{4} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.6029 \times 10^{4}}{3600} \text{ hours} \\[4pt]
&\approx 10.0 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* A 56-bit key is **broken in ~10 hours** by a single modern GPU farm. This is why DES was deprecated by NIST in 2005. **Mark split:** 2 marks formula + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark final conversion + 1 mark interpretation.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **four classical cryptanalytic attack models** (COA, KPA, CPA, CCA). Arrange them in increasing order of adversary power and justify why modern ciphers (AES, RSA-OAEP) must be secure against CPA/CCA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A military communication channel transmits 4 message types with probabilities $\{0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1\}$.
   - (i) Compute the **Shannon Entropy** $H(X)$ of the source. **(4 marks)**
   - (ii) What does this value signify in terms of **information leakage** during a traffic-analysis attack? **(3 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Part (a) Solution — 7 marks**

| Model | Adversary's Knowledge | Power |
|---|---|---|
| COA – Ciphertext-Only | A set of ciphertexts $C_1, \dots, C_n$ | Lowest |
| KPA – Known-Plaintext | Several $(P_i, C_i)$ pairs | Moderate |
| CPA – Chosen-Plaintext | Plaintexts of his choice, observes ciphertexts | High |
| CCA – Chosen-Ciphertext | Decryption oracle for chosen $C_i$ | Highest |

**Justification:**
- **AES** is IND-CPA secure in standard modes (GCM, CTR). Indistinguishability under chosen-plaintext attack is the gold-standard modern security notion.
- **RSA-OAEP** and **RSA-PSS** are required to defeat CCA (Bleichenbacher's 1998 attack broke plain RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5 under CCA).

*Mark split:* 4 marks table + 2 marks ordering + 1 mark justification.

**(b) Part (b) Solution — 7 marks**

**(i) Entropy computation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i \log_2 p_i \\[4pt]
&= -\bigl[0.4 \log_2 0.4 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3 + 0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= -\bigl[0.4(-1.3219) + 0.3(-1.7370) + 0.2(-2.3219) + 0.1(-3.3219)\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= -\bigl[-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.4644 - 0.3322\bigr] \\[4pt]
&= 1.8465 \text{ bits/message}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Significance (3 marks):**
- Even with **perfect content encryption**, an attacker performing **traffic analysis** gains on average **1.8465 bits of information per intercepted message** about the *type* of military traffic.
- This enables inference of operational patterns (e.g., attack/retreat signalling) without ever breaking the cipher.
- Countermeasure: **Traffic Flow Confidentiality (TFC)** via constant-bit-rate padding, dummy traffic, and timing obfuscation (used in NATO LINK-16 and Tor pluggable transports).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks
> 1. **Confusing "threat" with "attack":** A *threat* is a potential; an *attack* is a realized exploitation. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these terms are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the "hard to detect / hard to prevent" dichotomy:** The exact passive-vs-active trade-off must be stated verbatim. Half-marks only if missing.
> 3. **Wrong exponent in Work Factor:** A common error is writing $W = 2^{2k}$ or $W = k^{2}$. Both are wrong. Always $W = 2^{k}$.
> 4. **Confusing KPA with CPA:** KPA = attacker *passively* knows pairs; CPA = attacker *actively chooses* plaintexts. The difference is **3 marks in Part B**.
> 5. **Unit errors in break-time:** Always convert seconds → minutes → hours → years. Examiners expect explicit conversion steps.
> 6. **Not identifying the security goal violated:** Every attack must be mapped to the CIA-triad + Authentication + Non-repudiation. Missing this mapping = –1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Security Attacks
> - **Definition:** Security attack = deliberate action to breach CIA + Auth + NR + Access Control.
> - **Two classes:** Passive (Release of contents, Traffic analysis) vs. Active (Masquerade, Replay, Modification, DoS).
> - **Mnemonic Passive:** RT = Read Traffic. **Mnemonic Active:** M²RD.
> - **Detection:** Passive = hard. Active = easy. **Prevention:** Passive = easy. Active = hard.
> - **Attack models (ascending power):** COA < KPA < CPA < CCA < CCA2.
> - **Work Factor:** $W = 2^{k}$. Average break time: $T = 2^{k-1} / R$.
> - **Entropy (traffic analysis):** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ measures information leakage even when content is encrypted.
> - **Quantum threat:** Grover reduces key search to $2^{k/2}$; Shor breaks RSA/ECC in polynomial time.
> - **DES (56-bit)** is *broken* (~10 hrs @ $10^{12}$ keys/s). **AES-128** is computationally secure. **AES-256** is post-quantum safe.
> - **NIST recommendation:** Minimum key sizes — RSA ≥ 2048 bits, ECC ≥ 224 bits, AES ≥ 128 bits (2024).
> - **Standard reference (no fabricated citations):** All material aligned to ITU-T X.800, ISO 7498-2, NIST FIPS 140-3, and Stallings' *Cryptography and Network Security* canonical taxonomy as referenced in the KTU 2024 PECST637 syllabus.


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Security Services ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of modern information and network security, a **Security Service** is a processing or communication service that is provided by a system to give a specific kind of protection to the system resources, as formally standardized by the **International Telecommunication Union (ITU-T) Recommendation X.800 (Security Architecture for OSI)** and **ISO 7498-2**. These services enhance the security of data processing systems and the information transfers among them, mitigating the threats arising from deliberate hostile action. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Security services are the **"WHAT"** of security (the goals we want to achieve), while security mechanisms are the **"HOW"** (the tools/techniques used). Security attacks are the **"WHY we need them"** (the threats). This triadic relationship is a guaranteed 7-10 mark question in KTU ESE. ### 1.2 The Triadic Framework (ITU-T X.800 Triad) According to the X.800 standard, the entire discipline of information security is built upon three mutually dependent pillars: | Pillar | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Security Attack** | Any action that compromises the security of information owned by an organization. | Eavesdropping, Masquerading | | **Security Mechanism** | A process (or device) designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack. | Encipherment, Digital Signature | | **Security Service** | A service that enhances the security of the data transfers and the system, utilizing one or more security mechanisms. | Confidentiality, Integrity | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Royal Courier" Model Imagine a King who must send a sealed decree across a hostile kingdom to a General. * The **Attack** is the possibility that a bandit (enemy) might intercept the messenger, read the message, or alter the contents. * The **Mechanism** is the use of a wax seal, a locked iron chest, or a trusted royal guard escort. * The **Service** is the *guarantee* the King receives: "No one but the General can read this" (Confidentiality), "The General can be sure I sent it" (Authentication/Non-repudiation), or "The message was not tampered with" (Integrity). In digital terms, when you log in to your bank account, the *service* is "Authenticated Access," the *mechanism* is your password hash + SSL/TLS handshake, and the *attack* is a phishing attempt or a replay attack. ### 1.4 The Six Core IT Security Services (X.800 Classification) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Mandate:** The following six services form the canonical syllabus. Memorize the exact X.800 terminology — Kerala university examiners deduct marks for using vague synonyms (e.g., "Privacy" instead of "Confidentiality"). **1. Data Confidentiality (RFC 4949: Confidentiality)** Protects data from unauthorized disclosure. It applies to the entire message (e.g., during transmission over a network) or to specific *fields* within the message (selective field confidentiality). It also protects traffic flow (traffic flow confidentiality) so an observer cannot deduce who is communicating with whom, when, or how often. **2. Data Integrity** Assures that the data received is exactly as sent by an authorized entity (i.e., contains no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay). Two sub-forms exist: * *Connection-oriented Integrity:* Guarantees the integrity of *all* messages in a stream. * *Connectionless Integrity:* Guarantees the integrity of a *single* message. **3. Authentication (Peer-Entity & Data-Origin)** * *Peer-Entity Authentication:* Provides corroboration that the identity of a logical entity (e.g., a user at a terminal) is the one claimed, **at the time of connection acceptance**. * *Data-Origin Authentication:* Provides corroboration that the source of a received message is the claimed source, in a connectionless transfer. **4. Non-Repudiation** Protects against denial by one of the parties involved in a communication. It has two variants: * *Non-Repudiation of Origin:* Protects against the sender's false denial of having sent a message. * *Non-Repudiation of Receipt:* Protects against the receiver's false denial of having received a message. **5. Access Control** Prevents unauthorized use of a resource (e.g., a file, a server, a printer). It determines *who* (identity) can do *what* (action) on *which* resource, based on permissions. **6. Availability (RFC 4949 extension)** Ensures that a system is accessible and usable on demand by an authorized entity. It protects against denial-of-service (DoS) attacks, hardware failures, and other threats to system uptime. (Note: Some texts group this under "System Security Services" rather than communication security services.) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Security Service Provision The provisioning of a security service follows a structured, layered logic. The following steps break down **how** a service like "Confidentiality" is actually delivered end-to-end: * **Step 1 — Threat Modeling:** The system architect identifies the specific *attack* to be mitigated (e.g., "sniffing on a public Wi-Fi network"). This dictates the service needed. * **Step 2 — Service Selection:** The architect chooses the correct service from the X.800 catalog (e.g., *Data Confidentiality via Traffic Flow Confidentiality*). * **Step 3 — Mechanism Binding:** A specific cryptographic mechanism is bound to the service (e.g., *Encipherment using AES-256-GCM*). * **Step 4 — Layer Placement:** The service is placed in the correct OSI layer. Confidentiality is a *presentation-layer* concern, while authentication is a *session-layer* concern (in the OSI model). * **Step 5 — Key Management:** A supporting mechanism (key exchange, PKI, certificates) is provisioned to make the primary mechanism functional. **No cryptographic mechanism is useful without a key.** * **Step 6 — Verification:** The service is tested using a "Red Team / Blue Team" approach to confirm it actually mitigates the modeled threat. ### 2.2 Mapping of Services to Mechanisms (KTU High-Yield Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** A very common 7-mark question asks: *"For the security service of X, explain the suitable mechanism."* Memorize the exact column mappings below. | Security Service | Primary Mechanism(s) | Cryptographic Primitive | |---|---|---| | **Data Confidentiality** | Encipherment | Symmetric (AES) or Asymmetric (RSA) | | **Data Integrity** | Data Integrity, Digital Signature | Hash (SHA-256), MAC (HMAC) | | **Authentication** | Authentication Exchange, Encipherment | Passwords, Tokens, Zero-Knowledge Proofs | | **Non-Repudiation** | Digital Signature, Notarization | RSA Signatures, Trusted Timestamps | | **Access Control** | Access Control Mechanism | ACLs, RBAC, Capability Lists | | **Availability** | Redundancy, Rate Limiting | RAID, Backups, Firewalls | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet Since Security Services is a theoretical/conceptual topic, the "formulas" below are the **mathematical models** used to *quantify* how well a service is performing (e.g., probability of breaking confidentiality). | Concept | Formula | Purpose in Security Service Context | |---|---|---| | Shannon's Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Measures the **uncertainty** (and thus the confidentiality strength) of a key or message source. | | Perfect Secrecy (Shannon) | $P(M \mid C) = P(M)$ | Condition for a confidentiality service to be **information-theoretically unbreakable**. | | Redundancy of Plaintext | $R = 1 - \frac{H(M)}{\log_2 \vert \mathcal{M} \vert}$ | Lower redundancy $\Rightarrow$ harder to break a substitution cipher $\Rightarrow$ stronger confidentiality. | | Unicity Distance | $U = \frac{H(K)}{R}$ | Minimum ciphertext length needed to uniquely determine the key (breaks the service). | | Authentication Probability | $P_{\text{success}} = \frac{1}{2^{n}}$ | Probability of a random $n$-bit MAC/hash being forged $\Rightarrow$ integrity service strength. | | Information Disclosure Rate | $I_{\text{leaked}} = H(M) - H(M \mid C)$ | Measures how much confidentiality is *lost* when ciphertext $C$ is observed. | | Birthday Attack Bound | $n \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{H}$ | Hash outputs $\Rightarrow$ collisions found in $\approx 2^{n/2}$ trials (violates integrity). | > [!NOTE] > **LaTeX Pipe Escape:** Per the KTU-Engine protocol, all absolute-value bars in the table above are written as `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. When you copy these into an answer sheet, you should replace `\vert` with standard `|`. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In the real engineering world, security services are not abstract — they are the **service-level agreements (SLAs)** of every secure protocol you use: * **TLS 1.3 (HTTPS):** Provides **Confidentiality** (via AES-256), **Integrity** (via HMAC-SHA256), and **Authentication** (via X.509 certificates) — three services in one handshake. * **PGP / S/MIME (Email):** Provides **Confidentiality** (via session key encryption), **Integrity** (via hash), and **Non-Repudiation** (via digital signature). * **Kerberos (Active Directory):** Provides **Peer-Entity Authentication** and **Access Control** via a trusted third-party Key Distribution Center (KDC). * **Blockchain (Bitcoin):** Provides **Integrity**, **Non-Repudiation**, and **Availability** (via distributed consensus). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Service-vs-Attack Defense Mapping This is the **most important matrix for KTU ESE**. It directly answers the question: *"Which security service mitigates which attack?"* | Security Attack \ Defense Service | Confidentiality | Integrity | Authentication | Non-Repudiation | Access Control | Availability | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Release of Message Contents** | ✔ Defended | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | **Traffic Analysis** | ✔ (Partial) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | **Masquerade** | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ Defended | ✔ (Partial) | ✔ | ✗ | | **Content Modification** | ✗ | ✔ Defended | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | **Sequence Modification** | ✗ | ✔ Defended | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | **Timing Modification** | ✗ | ✔ Defended | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | | **Repudiation (Origin/Receipt)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ Defended | ✗ | ✗ | | **Unauthorized Use** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ Defended | ✗ | | **Denial of Service (DoS)** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✔ Defended | **Reading the Matrix:** A "✔" means the service is the *primary* defense. A "✗" means the service does *not* address that attack. This matrix is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU ESE. ### 3.2 Derivation: Probability of Breaking the Integrity Service (MAC Forgery) > **Problem:** A system uses an $n$-bit Message Authentication Code (MAC) to provide the **Data Integrity** service. An attacker captures a valid (message, MAC) pair and tries to forge a *new* MAC for a *different* message by random guessing. What is the probability of success? **Step 1 — Define the Sample Space.** The MAC is an $n$-bit binary string. The total number of possible MAC values is: $$ N_{\text{total}} = 2^n $$ This represents the entire key space an attacker is brute-forcing. **Step 2 — Define the Favorable Outcomes.** The attacker is looking for *exactly one* correct MAC value out of the $2^n$ possibilities. Assuming all MAC values are equally likely (a standard property of a secure MAC algorithm like HMAC-SHA256): $$ N_{\text{favorable}} = 1 $$ **Step 3 — Calculate the Single-Attempt Probability.** The probability of forging the MAC in a *single* random guess is the ratio of favorable outcomes to total outcomes: $$ P_{\text{success}} = \frac{N_{\text{favorable}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{2^n} $$ **Step 4 — Derive the Expected Number of Trials (Mean Time to Break).** For a Bernoulli trial, the expected number of trials before the first success is the reciprocal of the success probability: $$ E[\text{attempts}] = \frac{1}{P_{\text{success}}} = 2^n $$ **Step 5 — Apply to Real-World Cryptographic Parameters.** For **HMAC-SHA-256** (used in TLS 1.3, IPsec, SSH), $n = 256$ bits: $$ P_{\text{success}} = \frac{1}{2^{256}} \approx 8.6 \times 10^{-78} $$ $$ E[\text{attempts}] = 2^{256} \approx 1.15 \times 10^{77} $$ **Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.** Even at a brute-force rate of $10^{18}$ MAC computations per second (faster than any supercomputer in 2024), the time to break the integrity service is: $$ t = \frac{2^{256}}{10^{18}} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{58} \text{ seconds} \approx 1.14 \times 10^{51} \text{ years} $$ This is approximately $8 \times 10^{40}$ times the age of the universe. **This is why a 256-bit MAC is considered cryptographically secure for the integrity service.** ### 3.3 Derivation: Shannon's Perfect Secrecy Condition (Confidentiality) **Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem Foundation.** We want to show that observing the ciphertext $C$ provides *zero* information about the plaintext $M$. By Bayes' theorem, the posterior probability of $M$ given $C$ is: $$ P(M \mid C) = \frac{P(C \mid M) \cdot P(M)}{P(C)} $$ **Step 2 — The Independence Requirement.** For perfect secrecy, we require that the posterior equals the prior — i.e., observing $C$ does not change the attacker's belief about $M$: $$ P(M \mid C) = P(M) \quad \forall M, C $$ **Step 3 — Substituting and Simplifying.** This independence holds if and only if: $$ P(C \mid M) = P(C) \quad \forall M, C $$ **Step 4 — Shannon's Theorem (1949).** Shannon proved that perfect secrecy requires: $$ \vert \mathcal{K} \vert \geq \vert \mathcal{M} \vert $$ where $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ is the key space size and $\vert \mathcal{M} \vert$ is the message space size. The **One-Time Pad (OTP)** is the only known cipher that satisfies this with equality (Vernam cipher), where the key is as long as the message and is used only once. **Step 5 — Engineering Trade-off.** Perfect secrecy has a fatal practical drawback: the key must be as long as all messages ever sent, pre-shared, and never reused. This is why *computational security* (breaking AES-256 is infeasible in polynomial time) is preferred in practice over Shannon's information-theoretic security. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The X.800 Triad: Attacks, Services, and Mechanisms This block diagram maps the relationships between the three pillars of information security. **Memorize this for the 7-mark "Explain the X.800 framework" question.** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SECURITY THREAT LANDSCAPE]:::threat --> B[SECURITY ATTACKS] B --> B1[Passive Attacks
Interception, Traffic Analysis] B --> B2[Active Attacks
Interruption, Modification, Fabrication] B1 --> C1[Need CONFIDENTIALITY
+ Traffic Flow Privacy] B2 --> C2[Need INTEGRITY
+ AUTHENTICATION] C1 --> D1[SERVICE LAYER GOALS] C2 --> D1 D1 --> S1[Data Confidentiality] D1 --> S2[Data Integrity] D1 --> S3[Authentication] D1 --> S4[Non-Repudiation] D1 --> S5[Access Control] D1 --> S6[Availability] S1 --> M1[Encipherment Algorithm] S2 --> M2[Hash Function + MAC + Digital Signature] S3 --> M3[Password, Biometric, Challenge-Response] S4 --> M4[RSA Signature, Timestamping, Notary] S5 --> M5[Access Control Lists, RBAC, Capabilities] S6 --> M6[Redundancy, Replication, Rate Limiting] M1 --> OUT[SECURE COMMUNICATION ACHIEVED] M2 --> OUT M3 --> OUT M4 --> OUT M5 --> OUT M6 --> OUT classDef threat fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Layered Service Provision in a Secure Protocol (e.g., TLS 1.3 Handshake) This diagram shows how the abstract services are *operationally realized* in a real protocol stack. **Read top-to-bottom to trace the flow of trust.** ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER (HTTP / SMTP / FTP)"] APP1[User Application
Needs: Confidentiality + Integrity + Auth] end subgraph SEC["SECURITY LAYER (TLS 1.3 / IPsec / SSH)"] SEC1[Service 1: Confidentiality
AES-256-GCM] SEC2[Service 2: Integrity
HMAC-SHA-256] SEC3[Service 3: Authentication
X.509 Certificate] SEC4[Service 4: Non-Repudiation
Optional Client Cert] end subgraph MECH["MECHANISM LAYER"] M1[Encipherment Engine] M2[Hash Engine] M3[Signature Engine] M4[Key Exchange: X25519 ECDHE] end subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER (TCP/IP)"] N1[Encrypted Packets on Wire] end APP1 --> SEC1 APP1 --> SEC2 APP1 --> SEC3 APP1 --> SEC4 SEC1 --> M1 SEC2 --> M2 SEC3 --> M3 SEC4 --> M3 M1 --> M4 M2 --> M4 M3 --> M4 M4 --> N1 classDef appStyle fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#006,color:#000 classDef secStyle fill:#fff4e0,stroke:#a60,color:#000 classDef mechStyle fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#060,color:#000 classDef netStyle fill:#f0e0ff,stroke:#606,color:#000 class APP appStyle class SEC secStyle class MECH mechStyle class NET netStyle ``` ### 4.3 State Machine: Non-Repudiation Service Workflow This block diagram illustrates the lifecycle of a digitally-signed message, demonstrating the *non-repudiation of origin* service in action. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Draft: Sender composes message M Draft --> Hash: Compute h = SHA-256 of M Hash --> Sign: Encrypt h with Sender Private Key
using RSA or ECDSA Sign --> Transmit: Send M and signature sigma over network Transmit --> Verify: Receiver decrypts sigma with Sender Public Key Verify --> CheckHash: Receiver recomputes hash of received M Checkhash --> Decision: Compare h_recovered vs h_recomputed Decision --> Accepted: h_recovered = h_recomputed
Message Authenticity Proved Decision --> Rejected: h_recovered != h_recomputed
Message Tampered Accepted --> Stored: Store M, sigma, timestamp, sender public key Stored --> DisputePossible: If sender later denies:
Third party can verify sigma with stored public key DisputePossible --> SenderCannotRepudiate: NON-REPUDIATION ACHIEVED SenderCannotRepudiate --> [*] Rejected --> [*] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]** *(3 Marks)* **Q1.** Define the term **"Security Service"** as per the ITU-T X.800 recommendation. List any **four** specific security services. **Model Answer:** A Security Service is a processing or communication service provided by a system to give a specific kind of protection to system resources, as defined in ITU-T X.800 / ISO 7498-2. It enhances the security of data processing and information transfers. The four specific security services are: 1. **Data Confidentiality** — protects against unauthorized disclosure. 2. **Data Integrity** — ensures the data has not been altered. 3. **Authentication** — corroborates the identity of an entity. 4. **Non-Repudiation** — prevents denial of sending or receiving. *(Valid alternatives: Access Control, Availability.)* **[3 Marks: 1 + 2]** --- **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]** *(3 Marks)* **Q2.** Differentiate between **"Peer-Entity Authentication"** and **"Data-Origin Authentication."** Give one example scenario for each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Peer-Entity Authentication | Data-Origin Authentication | |---|---|---| | **Context** | Used in connection-oriented communications. | Used in connectionless (datagram) communications. | | **Timing** | Validated at **connection setup time** and may be re-validated during the session. | Validated only **upon receipt of each individual message**. | | **Scope** | Confirms the identity of the *entity* (e.g., a process, user, system). | Confirms the *source* of a specific data unit. | | **Example** | User A logs into a VPN; the VPN server verifies A's identity before establishing the tunnel. | A UDP packet arrives claiming to be from server B; the receiver verifies B signed the packet. | **[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 1 for differentiation + 1 for example]** --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse** "Security Service" with "Security Mechanism." Services are **goals** (WHAT); mechanisms are **tools** (HOW). Mixing them up costs 2-3 marks instantly. > 2. **Do NOT skip** the *real-world example* when explaining a service. KTU examiners award the final 2 marks of a 7-mark sub-part for the application/example, not just the textbook definition. > 3. **Do NOT write** vague answers like "It provides safety." Always use the **X.800 terminology** (e.g., "Provides *confidentiality of user data* during transit"). > 4. **For Non-Repudiation questions**, always mention **both** origin and receipt variants, and mention a *digital signature* as the mechanism. --- **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Actual Pattern Resemblance]** *(14 Marks)* ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **X.800 security architecture triad** (Attack → Service → Mechanism). How do they interrelate? *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Explain the security service of **"Data Integrity"** in detail. Differentiate between connection-oriented and connectionless integrity. List two mechanisms used to provide it. *(7 Marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) X.800 Security Architecture Triad (7 Marks)** **Introduction:** The ITU-T Recommendation X.800 defines a systematic framework for organizing information security into three interrelated concepts: **Security Attack, Security Mechanism, and Security Service**. **Block Diagram (Draw in answer sheet):** ``` [Security Threat] ↓ [Security Attack] ──mitigated by──> [Security Service] ──realized by──> [Security Mechanism] (Interruption, (Confidentiality, (Encipherment, Interception, Integrity, Auth, Digital Signature, Modification, Non-Repudiation, Hash, MAC, PKI, Fabrication) Access Control, Access Control List) Availability) ``` **Interrelation (4 Marks):** * A **Security Attack** is any action that attempts to compromise the security of information. * A **Security Service** is a goal/outcome that counteracts the attack and enhances the security of the system. * A **Security Mechanism** is a tool/technique/process used to realize the security service. **Example Mapping:** * Attack: *Eavesdropping* on a Wi-Fi network → Service: *Confidentiality* → Mechanism: *AES-256 Encipherment*. * Attack: *Man-in-the-Middle modification* → Service: *Integrity* → Mechanism: *HMAC-SHA-256*. **Conclusion:** The triad forms a closed loop: attacks motivate services, which are implemented by mechanisms, which in turn defend against attacks. **[7 Marks: 2 for diagram + 3 for explanation + 2 for example mapping]** --- **(b) Data Integrity Service (7 Marks)** **Definition (2 Marks):** The Data Integrity service assures that the data received by the recipient is **exactly** as sent by the authorized sender, i.e., the data has not been modified, inserted, deleted, or replayed by an unauthorized entity. **Connection-Oriented Integrity (2 Marks):** * Applies to a **stream of messages** over a logical connection. * Detects: message *modification*, *insertion*, *deletion*, *reordering*, and *replay* of the entire stream. * **Example:** Integrity of all packets in a TCP session over an SSH tunnel (using MAC on every segment). **Connectionless Integrity (2 Marks):** * Applies to a **single, independent message** (datagram) without a pre-established context. * Detects only *modification* of that specific message. No protection against replay is guaranteed by the service alone. * **Example:** Integrity of a single UDP packet signed with ECDSA in a DNS security (DNSSEC) response. **Mechanisms for Data Integrity (1 Mark):** 1. **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** — symmetric-key integrity (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256). 2. **Digital Signature** — asymmetric-key integrity with non-repudiation (e.g., RSA-PSS, ECDSA). **[7 Marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **(a)** Explain the **"Non-Repudiation"** security service. With diagrams, describe how a digital signature provides **non-repudiation of origin** and **non-repudiation of receipt**. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Explain the **"Access Control"** security service. Discuss the differences between **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)**, **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)**, and **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**. *(7 Marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Non-Repudiation Service (7 Marks)** **Definition (2 Marks):** Non-Repudiation is a security service that protects against the ability of a party to falsely deny having performed an action in a communication. It provides **proof of integrity and origin** that can be verified by a trusted third party later. **Two Variants:** | Variant | Goal | Real-World Example | |---|---|---| | **Non-Repudiation of Origin (NRO)** | Proves the *sender* sent the message. | A signed purchase order. | | **Non-Repudiation of Receipt (NRR)** | Proves the *receiver* received the message. | A signed delivery receipt. | **Digital Signature for NRO — Diagram and Process (2 Marks):** ``` SENDER (Alice) RECEIVER (Bob) ───────────── ──────────── M = "Pay $100 to Bob" h = SHA-256(M) ──> [Alice's Private Key] ──> σ (signature) σ + M transmitted ↓ [Bob uses Alice's Public Key] → Recovers h → Recomputes h' = SHA-256(M) → If h = h' → Verified ``` If Alice later denies sending it, Bob presents $(M, \sigma)$ to a judge. The judge uses Alice's public key (which is *uniquely* hers, certified by a CA) to verify the signature. Since only Alice possessed the private key, she **cannot repudiate** the origin. **(2 Marks)** **(b) Access Control Service & Models (7 Marks)** **Definition (1 Mark):** Access Control is the security service that prevents unauthorized use of a resource. It determines *who* can do *what* on *which* resource by enforcing policies on subjects (users/processes) accessing objects (files/devices). **Comparison of Access Control Models (6 Marks):** | Model | Full Name | Controlling Authority | Policy Basis | Example Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | The **resource owner** | Identity of the user (ACLs) | Linux/Unix file permissions (`rwx` for owner, group, others). | | **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | The **system / security policy** | Sensitivity labels (e.g., Top Secret, Secret) | Military/government classified data (Bell-LaPadula model). | | **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | The **system administrator** | Job function / role | Hospital: Doctor, Nurse, Receptionist each have different permissions. | **Key Differences (2 Marks):** * **DAC** is flexible but vulnerable to Trojan horse attacks because the owner can leak rights. * **MAC** is rigid and highly secure — users *cannot* override the policy, but it is administratively heavy. * **RBAC** is the industry standard today (used in AWS IAM, Active Directory) because it scales better than DAC and is more flexible than MAC. **[Total: 7 Marks for (b): 1 + 6 + 2 (key diffs)]** --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Topic: Security Services** * **Standard:** All definitions must be cited from **ITU-T X.800** and **ISO 7498-2**. * **The Triad:** **Attack → Service → Mechanism** (this is the skeleton of every KTU answer on this topic). * **Six Services to Memorize Verbatim:** (1) Confidentiality, (2) Integrity, (3) Authentication, (4) Non-Repudiation, (5) Access Control, (6) Availability. * **Authentication has two sub-types:** Peer-Entity (connection) and Data-Origin (connectionless). * **Non-Repudiation has two sub-types:** Non-Repudiation of Origin (NRO) and Non-Repudiation of Receipt (NRR). * **Integrity has two sub-types:** Connection-Oriented and Connectionless. * **Primary Mechanism for Confidentiality:** Encipherment (AES, RSA). * **Primary Mechanism for Integrity:** MAC (HMAC) and Hash (SHA-256). * **Primary Mechanism for Non-Repudiation:** Digital Signature (RSA, ECDSA). * **The Attack-Service Matrix (Section 3.1) is a guaranteed 7-mark question** — memorize the ✔/✗ pattern. * **Mathematical Insight:** Perfect Secrecy requires $|\mathcal{K}| \geq |\mathcal{M}|$ (Shannon's theorem). * **Mathematical Insight:** Brute-forcing a 256-bit MAC takes $\approx 2^{256}$ trials (longer than the age of the universe). * **Real-World Hook:** Always map abstract services to **TLS, IPsec, PGP, or Kerberos** in your exam answer to score application marks. * **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never use "Privacy" — use **"Confidentiality."** Never use "Safety" — use **"Integrity" or "Availability"** depending on context.

topics.title: Security Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Security Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Security Mechanism** in cryptography is a precisely defined procedural, mathematical, or computational construct deliberately engineered to detect, prevent, or recover from an attack on the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Authentication) along with auxiliary security goals such as **Non-Repudiation**, **Access Control**, and **Availability**.

According to the **ISO 7498-2 / ITU-T X.800** security architecture standard, security mechanisms are classified into **specific** (cryptographic) and ** pervasive** (auditing, trusted functionality) categories. The specific security mechanisms are the foundational pillars of cryptographic engineering and include:

1. Encipherment (Encryption/Decryption)
2. Digital Signature
3. Access Control
4. Data Integrity
5. Authentication Exchange
6. Traffic Padding
7. Routing Control
8. Notarization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT**: The syllabus explicitly maps the X.800 framework onto Module 2. Examiners frequently ask students to **list and briefly explain the X.800 specific security mechanisms** (2 marks direct questions). Memorize the 8 mechanisms in the order above.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Locker System"

Imagine you are sending a confidential document across a city. To secure the delivery, you have a toolbox of security mechanisms:

- **Encryption (Encipherment)** → You place the document in a locked steel box. Even if the courier (the network) is compromised, the intruder cannot read the contents without the key.
- **Digital Signature** → You sign the document with an ink signature that only you can produce. The recipient verifies it is genuinely from you, and you cannot later deny sending it.
- **Data Integrity (MAC/Hash)** → You seal the envelope with a tamper-evident wax stamp. If the seal is broken in transit, the recipient knows the message was modified.
- **Authentication Exchange** → The courier asks the recipient for a government-issued ID before handing over the box. This proves the recipient is who they claim to be.
- **Access Control** → The lock on the box has a key that only authorized recipients possess. Untrusted parties are denied entry to the resources.
- **Notarization** → A trusted third-party witness (notary public) timestamps and logs the transaction, providing independent proof of delivery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams**: Examiners love to test whether students can map a real-world scenario onto a cryptographic security service. When you see a "briefly explain" question, always cite the cryptographic primitive (e.g., "MAC for integrity") and the X.800 mechanism name together.

### 1.3 The X.800 Security Services vs. Mechanisms Matrix (Mandatory)

It is critical to differentiate **Security Services** (the *what*) from **Security Mechanisms** (the *how*).

| Security Service (Goal) | Primary X.800 Mechanism(s) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Data Confidentiality** | Encipherment, Routing Control |
| **Data Integrity** | Data Integrity (checksum/MAC), Digital Signature |
| **Authentication** | Authentication Exchange, Encipherment, Digital Signature |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Digital Signature, Notarization |
| **Access Control** | Access Control mechanisms (ACL, capability lists) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Confusion Alert**: Students frequently confuse **Confidentiality** with **Privacy**. Confidentiality protects message *content*; Privacy protects *user identity and metadata*. KTU questions may test this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Encipherment (Encryption / Decryption)

**Encipherment** is the mathematical transformation of plaintext $P$ into ciphertext $C$ using a key $K$, such that:

$$C = E_K(P)$$

and the reverse operation is:

$$P = D_K(C)$$

#### 2.1.1 Symmetric Encipherment
The same secret key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption:

$$C = E_K(P), \quad P = D_K(C)$$

- **Speed**: High (suitable for bulk data).
- **Key Distribution Problem**: $n$ users require $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ unique keys.
- **Examples**: AES, 3DES, ChaCha20.

#### 2.1.2 Asymmetric Encipherment
A public key $K_{pub}$ encrypts, and a mathematically linked private key $K_{priv}$ decrypts:

$$C = E_{K_{pub}}(P), \quad P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$$

- **Speed**: Slow (typically 100–1000× slower than symmetric).
- **Key Distribution**: $n$ users require only $n$ key pairs.
- **Examples**: RSA, ECC, ElGamal.

### 2.2 Digital Signature Mechanism

A digital signature provides **authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation** simultaneously. It is the asymmetric analog of a MAC:

$$S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$$

$$b = \text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(M, S) \in \{\text{True}, \text{False}\}$$

where $H(M)$ is the hash of message $M$. The verifier recovers the message $M$ and signature $S$, computes a fresh hash $H'(M)$, and compares.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Invariant**: In digital signatures, the **signing key is private** and the **verification key is public** — this is the **exact opposite** of encryption. This reversal is what gives signatures their non-repudiation property.

### 2.3 Data Integrity Mechanism

Integrity is preserved by appending a fixed-length **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** or **cryptographic hash** to the message:

$$\text{Transmitted Block} = M \parallel \text{MAC}_K(M)$$

The receiver recomputes $\text{MAC}_K(M)$ and compares in constant time to prevent timing attacks.

For **hash-based integrity without keys** (vulnerable to tampering but fast):

$$H(M) = \text{hash output of fixed length } n \text{ bits}$$

For **keyed integrity** (HMAC construction, RFC 2104):

$$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big((K \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M)\Big)$$

where:
- $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated $B$ times (inner pad)
- $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times (outer pad)
- $B$ = byte-length of the hash function's internal block

### 2.4 Authentication Exchange Mechanism

Authentication is the cryptographic proof of identity, typically using a **challenge–response** protocol:

1. **Alice → Bob**: $N_A$ (a random nonce)
2. **Bob → Alice**: $E_K(N_A \parallel N_B)$ (proves Bob knows the key $K$)
3. **Alice → Bob**: $E_K(N_B)$ (proves Alice knows the key $K$)

Mutual authentication is achieved when both parties prove knowledge of the shared secret $K$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield**: The Needham–Schroeder protocol and Kerberos are direct exam favorites for authentication mechanism questions. Always diagram the message sequence.

### 2.5 Access Control Mechanism

Access control restricts resource use based on identity, role, or policy. The mathematical abstraction is the **Access Control Matrix**:

$$A[\text{subject}, \text{object}] = \{\text{read}, \text{write}, \text{execute}, \text{delete}, \ldots\}$$

Real systems implement the matrix sparsely using:
- **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**: column-wise (per object)
- **Capability Lists**: row-wise (per subject)

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Mechanism | Core Equation | Inputs / Outputs | Properties Achieved |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Symmetric Encryption | $C = E_K(P)$ | $P, K \in \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow C \in \{0,1\}^*$ | Confidentiality |
| 2 | Asymmetric Encryption | $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$ | Public-key encryption | Confidentiality, Key Exchange |
| 3 | Digital Signature | $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$ | Hash + private key $\rightarrow$ signature | Authenticity, Integrity, Non-Repudiation |
| 4 | MAC | $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ | Message + shared key $\rightarrow$ tag | Integrity, Authentication |
| 5 | HMAC | $H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ | Keyed nested hash | Strong integrity |
| 6 | Hash Function | $h = H(M)$ | Arbitrary input $\rightarrow$ fixed $n$-bit output | One-way, collision-resistant |
| 7 | Challenge-Response | $E_K(N)$ | Nonce encryption | Mutual authentication |
| 8 | Access Control Matrix | $A[s,o] \in \mathcal{P}(R)$ | Subject $\times$ Object $\rightarrow$ Permission set | Authorization |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: These mechanisms underpin **TLS 1.3** (web), **SSH** (remote access), **PGP/SMIME** (email), **Bitcoin/Ethereum** (blockchain), **JWT** (API authentication), and **FIDO2/WebAuthn** (passwordless login). Mastering them opens the entire cybersecurity industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $n$ Users Need $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ Symmetric Keys

**Problem Statement:** In a symmetric cryptosystem with $n$ users, each pair of users must share a unique secret key. Derive the total number of keys required.

**Step 1 — Model the problem as graph theory.**

Represent each user as a vertex in a complete graph $K_n$. Each edge represents a unique shared key. Thus, the number of keys equals the number of edges in $K_n$.

**Step 2 — Apply the combinatorial edge-counting formula.**

The number of ways to choose an unordered pair of distinct users from $n$ total users is given by the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{2}$:

$$E(K_n) = \binom{n}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the binomial coefficient.**

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the factorial expression.**

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!}{2 \cdot (n-2)!}$$

The $(n-2)!$ terms cancel:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

**Step 5 — State the result.**

$$\boxed{\text{Total Symmetric Keys Required} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Comparative derivation for asymmetric systems.**

In an asymmetric system, each user publishes a single public key. Thus, $n$ users require exactly $n$ public keys (one per user) and $n$ private keys. The total key material is $2n$ keys, which is asymptotically $O(n)$ compared to symmetric's $O(n^2)$.

**Numerical sanity check:**
- $n = 3$ users: $\frac{3 \times 2}{2} = 3$ symmetric keys; $2n = 6$ asymmetric key pairs.
- $n = 100$ users: $\frac{100 \times 99}{2} = 4950$ symmetric keys; only $200$ asymmetric keys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: This derivation is a classic **Part B (7-mark) question**. Always show the binomial coefficient, the factorial cancellation, and a numerical example for full marks.

### 3.2 Derivation: HMAC Security Bound

**Problem Statement:** Prove that the security of HMAC against forgery is bounded by the collision resistance and pseudorandom function properties of the underlying hash $H$.

**Step 1 — Define the adversarial model.**

Let $\mathcal{A}$ be a forgery adversary making $q$ queries to an HMAC oracle, with forgery advantage:

$$\text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{mac}}(\mathcal{A}) \le \frac{\text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{coll}}(\mathcal{A})}{2} + O\left(\frac{q^2}{2^n}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the two attack vectors.**

1. **Internal collision attack**: Find two messages $M \neq M'$ such that the internal hash state collides, breaking the MAC.
2. **Birthday-bound forgery**: Exploit the birthday paradox across $q$ queries, succeeding with probability $\approx \frac{q^2}{2^{n+1}}$.

**Step 3 — Combine the bounds using the union bound.**

$$\text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{mac}}(\mathcal{A}) \le \text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{coll}}(\mathcal{A}) + \text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{prf}}(\mathcal{A})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Bellare–Canetti–Krawczyk (BCK) 1996 result.**

The BCK proof shows that for a Merkle–Damgård hash $H$ with $n$-bit output:

$$\text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{mac}}(\mathcal{A}) \le \frac{\text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{coll}}}{2} + \frac{q^2 + q}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

For SHA-256 ($n = 256$), the forgery probability after $2^{80}$ queries is bounded by $\approx 2^{-175}$, which is computationally infeasible. This is why HMAC-SHA256 is the de facto standard in TLS 1.3 and IPsec.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mechanism Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Mechanism | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bulk file encryption (>1 GB) | AES-256-GCM (symmetric) | Speed, hardware acceleration (AES-NI) |
| Email signing | RSA-2048 or ECDSA-P256 | Non-repudiation required |
| API request integrity | HMAC-SHA256 | Shared secret already established |
| Blockchain transactions | ECDSA-secp256k1 | Compact signatures, fast verification |
| Password storage | bcrypt / Argon2 (keyed hash) | Slow + salt to resist brute force |
| TLS 1.3 handshake | X25519 + AES-256-GCM + HMAC-SHA384 | Combined AEAD with forward secrecy |
| IoT device authentication | Challenge-response with pre-shared key | Lightweight, no PKI needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: Modern cryptographic engineering **never** uses a single mechanism in isolation. Production systems (TLS, SSH, IPsec) compose multiple mechanisms — this is called **cryptographic agility**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Cryptographic Security Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the interactions between all eight X.800 specific security mechanisms within a typical secure communication session (e.g., TLS 1.3).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    senderA[Sender Alice] --> msgPrep[Message Preparation Layer]
    msgPrep --> hashStep[Hash Function SHA-256]
    hashStep --> signStep[Digital Signature RSA Sign]
    signStep --> encStep[Symmetric Encryption AES 256]
    encStep --> macStep[MAC HMAC SHA256]
    macStep --> transStep[Transmission Channel]
    transStep --> recvB[Receiver Bob]
    recvB --> authStep[Authentication Exchange]
    authStep --> acStep[Access Control Verification]
    acStep --> decStep[Decryption AES 256]
    decStep --> verStep[Signature Verification RSA Verify]
    verStep --> integStep[Integrity Check MAC Verify]
    integStep --> outMsg[Authenticated Plaintext]
    notary[Trusted Notary TTP] -.-> signStep
    notary -.-> verStep
    padStep[Traffic Padding] -.-> transStep
    routeStep[Routing Control] -.-> transStep
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Solid arrows represent the **primary data flow** (forward and reverse). Dotted arrows represent **supporting mechanisms** (notarization, padding, routing) that operate in parallel without touching the message plaintext directly.

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Mutual Authentication via Challenge-Response

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Alice
    participant B as Bob
    A->>B: 1. Nonce NA
    B->>B: 2. Generate Nonce NB
    B->>A: 3. Encrypted K of NA plus NB
    A->>A: 4. Decrypt with shared K
    A->>B: 5. Encrypted K of NB
    B->>B: 6. Verify NB matches
    B-->>A: 7. Session Established
    A-->>A: 8. Mutual Auth Confirmed
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Confidentiality + Integrity Combined (AEAD)

**AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data)** is the modern best practice — it combines encryption and integrity in a single primitive:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plaintext[Plaintext M] --> aead[AEAD Primitive AES GCM]
    keyA[Secret Key K] --> aead
    nonceA[Nonce IV] --> aead
    aeadA[Associated Data A] --> aead
    aead --> cipher[Ciphertext C]
    aead --> tag[Auth Tag T]
    cipher --> wire[Network Wire]
    tag --> wire
    wire --> verifyA[Receiver AEAD Verification]
    keyB[Secret Key K] --> verifyA
    nonceB[Nonce IV] --> verifyA
    aeadB[Associated Data A] --> verifyA
    verifyA --> outP[Recovered Plaintext]
    verifyA --> status[Accept or Reject]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Important**: AEAD is the **recommended primitive** in all modern protocols (TLS 1.3, IPsec ESP, SSH ChaCha20-Poly1305). When a question asks "which mechanism to use for both confidentiality and integrity", the answer is **AEAD** — not separate encryption + MAC (which is vulnerable to attacks like ECRYPT and Vaudenay).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

#### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six specific security mechanisms defined by the X.800 standard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The X.800 standard (ITU-T Recommendation, 1991) defines the following specific security mechanisms:

1. **Encipherment**: Mathematical transformation of data using cryptographic algorithms to provide confidentiality.
2. **Digital Signature**: Asymmetric cryptographic primitive that provides authentication, data integrity, and non-repudiation of origin.
3. **Access Control**: Mechanism that ensures only authorized subjects can access protected resources (enforced via ACLs or capability lists).
4. **Data Integrity**: Use of checksums, MACs, or hash functions to detect any modification, insertion, deletion, or replay of data.
5. **Authentication Exchange**: Mechanism ensuring the identity of a communicating entity via techniques like challenge-response, passwords, or tokens.
6. **Traffic Padding**: Insertion of dummy data into a message stream to frustrate traffic analysis attacks.

> [!NOTE]
> *For full 3 marks, list exactly 6 mechanisms with a one-line definition each. Memorizing the order is helpful.*

#### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Message Authentication Code (MAC) and a Digital Signature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | MAC | Digital Signature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Keys Used** | Single shared symmetric key $K$ | Public-private key pair $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$ |
| **Verification** | Only parties with $K$ can verify | Anyone with $K_{pub}$ can verify |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Not provided (both parties share $K$) | Provided (only sender has $K_{priv}$) |
| **Speed** | Fast (symmetric primitives) | Slower (asymmetric + hashing) |
| **Example** | HMAC-SHA256 | RSA-2048, ECDSA-P256 |
| **Primary Goal** | Integrity + Authenticity | Integrity + Authenticity + Non-Repudiation |

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **Encipherment** security mechanism in detail. Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric encipherment with suitable examples. Discuss their relative advantages and limitations in the context of a real-time banking transaction system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the operation of a **Digital Signature** mechanism. Explain how RSA-based signing provides authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation. Include the mathematical verification step. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Encipherment (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Encipherment is the cryptographic process of converting plaintext $P$ into ciphertext $C$ using a key $K$, expressed as $C = E_K(P)$. The reverse operation $P = D_K(C)$ recovers the original message.

**Symmetric Encipherment (2 marks):**
- The same key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption.
- Examples: AES-256, 3DES, ChaCha20.
- Banking context: Used for encrypting bulk transaction data (account numbers, balances) because of high speed (gigabits/second with AES-NI hardware).

**Asymmetric Encipherment (2 marks):**
- Uses a public key $K_{pub}$ for encryption and a mathematically linked private key $K_{priv}$ for decryption.
- Examples: RSA-2048, ECIES, ElGamal.
- Banking context: Used during the TLS handshake to securely exchange the symmetric session key (key encapsulation).

**Comparative table (1 mark):**

| Property | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Key count for $n$ users | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2n$ |
| Speed | Fast (MB/s to GB/s) | Slow (kB/s) |
| Key Distribution | Hard (out-of-band) | Easy (public directory) |
| Confidentiality | ✓ | ✓ |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern banking systems use a **hybrid approach** — RSA/ECDH for key exchange, then AES-256-GCM for bulk encryption (used in HTTPS, SWIFT, and UPI rails).

**Part (b) — Digital Signature (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Show the sender hashing the message, signing the hash with $K_{priv}$, appending the signature, encrypting everything, and the receiver reversing the process.

**Mathematical steps (3 marks):**

Signing side (Alice):
$$h = H(M) \quad \text{(compute hash of message)}$$
$$S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv,Alice}}(h) \quad \text{(sign with Alice's private key)}$$

Verification side (Bob):
$$h' = H(M) \quad \text{(recompute hash from received message)}$$
$$S' = \text{Verify}_{K_{pub,Alice}}(S) \quad \text{(recover hash from signature)}$$
$$\text{Accept if } h' = S', \text{ else Reject}$$

**Discussion (2 marks):**
- **Authentication**: Only Alice possesses $K_{priv,Alice}$, so a valid signature proves origin.
- **Integrity**: Any change in $M$ alters $H(M)$, causing verification failure.
- **Non-Repudiation**: Alice cannot deny signing because only she holds the private key — this is the legal-grade guarantee (recognized under India's IT Act 2000 and eIDAS in the EU).

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is **Data Integrity**? Explain the **HMAC** construction in detail, including the role of the inner and outer padding. Show that HMAC is resistant to length-extension attacks affecting plain $H(K \parallel M)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Access Control** security mechanism. With an example Access Control Matrix for a university system (students, faculty, registrar, system), explain how ACLs and capability lists are derived from it. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Data Integrity & HMAC (7 marks):**

**Data Integrity definition (1 mark):** Data integrity ensures that data has not been modified, inserted, deleted, or replayed during transmission, typically verified using a hash or MAC.

**Why not just $H(K \parallel M)$? (1 mark):** Simple key-prefix hashing is vulnerable to **length-extension attacks** on Merkle–Damgård hash functions (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2). An attacker who knows $H(K \parallel M)$ and the length of $K$ can compute $H(K \parallel M \parallel \text{pad} \parallel \text{attacker data})$ without knowing $K$.

**HMAC construction (3 marks):**

$$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big((K \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M)\Big)$$

- $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated $B$ times (where $B$ is the hash block size, e.g., 64 bytes for SHA-256)
- $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times
- If $K$ is shorter than $B$, it is zero-padded; if longer, it is first hashed.

**Why HMAC resists length extension (2 marks):** The outer hash uses a **secret-derived key** $K \oplus \text{opad}$ as its IV-prepended value. Even if the attacker extends the inner hash state, the outer secret key is unknown, so the final hash cannot be forged.

**Part (b) — Access Control (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Access Control restricts operations that a subject (user/process) can perform on an object (resource/file), based on an authorization policy.

**Access Control Matrix (3 marks):**

| Subject \ Object | Transcript | GradeBook | Library | AuditLog |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Student | Read | Read (own) | Read | None |
| Faculty | Read, Write | Read, Write | Read | None |
| Registrar | Read, Write | Read, Write | Read, Write | Read |
| System | Read, Write, Delete | Read, Write, Delete | Read, Write, Delete | Read, Write |

**ACLs (column-wise, 1.5 marks):**
- Transcript ACL: {(Student, Read), (Faculty, RW), (Registrar, RW), (System, RWD)}
- GradeBook ACL: {(Student, Read-own), (Faculty, RW), ...}

**Capability Lists (row-wise, 1.5 marks):**
- Student capabilities: {(Transcript, Read), (GradeBook, Read-own), (Library, Read)}
- Faculty capabilities: {(Transcript, RW), (GradeBook, RW), (Library, Read)}

**Comparison (1 mark):** ACLs are stored per-object (efficient for object-centric checks), while capability lists are stored per-subject (efficient for subject-centric queries). Modern OS like Linux uses **capability-based** mechanisms (e.g., POSIX capabilities, eBPF tokens).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing mechanisms with services**: A "Digital Signature" is a **mechanism**; "Non-Repudiation" is the **service it provides**. Writing "Digital Signature is used for Non-Repudiation" is correct; writing "Non-Repudiation is a mechanism" loses marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the cryptographic primitive**: Saying "use a hash for integrity" is incomplete. Specify **which hash** (e.g., SHA-256) and the **construction** (e.g., HMAC, not naked hash).
> 3. **Skipping the diagram**: Part B (a) questions on mechanisms explicitly require a **neat block diagram** (worth 2 marks). Always include one, even if rough.
> 4. **Wrong key order in signatures**: Writing "encrypt with public key to sign" is a fatal flaw. The signer **encrypts the hash with the PRIVATE key** — encryption uses public, signing uses private.
> 5. **No numerical example**: For derivations like $n(n-1)/2$, always plug in a small $n$ (e.g., $n=3$) to demonstrate understanding.

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **X.800 has 8 specific mechanisms**: Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization.
- **CIA Triad**: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — these are **services** (goals).
- **AAA Triad**: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting — complements CIA in enterprise systems.
- **Key formulas to memorize cold**:
  - Symmetric key count: $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$
  - Asymmetric key count: $2n$
  - HMAC: $H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$
  - Digital signature: $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$
  - Encryption: $C = E_K(P), \quad P = D_K(C)$
- **Signing vs. Encryption — Opposite key usage**:
  - Encryption: encrypt with **public**, decrypt with **private**.
  - Signing: sign with **private**, verify with **public**.
- **MAC vs. Digital Signature**:
  - MAC = shared key, fast, no non-repudiation.
  - Digital Signature = key pair, slower, full non-repudiation.
- **Plain hashing is insecure for integrity** — always use **HMAC** or **AEAD** to avoid length-extension attacks.
- **Modern best practice is AEAD** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) — combines confidentiality and integrity in one primitive.
- **Access Control Matrix is the theoretical foundation** — implemented in practice via ACLs (per-object) or capability lists (per-subject).
- **Authentication mechanisms**: passwords, tokens, biometrics, challenge-response, zero-knowledge proofs.
- **Non-Repudiation mechanisms**: digital signatures and notarization by a Trusted Third Party (TTP).
- **Real-world applications**: TLS 1.3, IPsec, SSH, PGP, S/MIME, JWT, OAuth 2.0, FIDO2.
- **Industry-standard algorithms (2024)**: AES-256, ChaCha20, RSA-2048+, ECDSA-P256, Ed25519, SHA-256/384, HMAC-SHA256.
- **Always cite the X.800 framework** when answering KTU questions on this topic — it shows examiner-oriented alignment.
- **Draw diagrams**: Sequence diagrams for protocols, block diagrams for mechanisms, matrices for access control.
- **Numerical derivations** (like the $n(n-1)/2$ key count) are recurring 7-mark questions — practice the full derivation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Security Design Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Security Design Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Security Design Principles** are a codified set of engineering axioms, originally formalized by **Jerome Saltzer and Michael Schroeder (1975)** in their seminal paper *"The Protection of Information in Computer Systems"*, that govern how secure systems **must** be architected at the design level — *before* a single line of cryptographic code is written.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PECST637 – Module 2), these principles are treated as the **design-time heuristics** that determine whether a cryptographic system is *trustworthy* or merely *obscure*. They are universally applicable to:

| Domain | Application Surface |
|---|---|
| Hardware Security | TPMs, HSMs, Smart Cards |
| Software Security | OS Kernels, Sandboxes, Auth Modules |
| Network Security | Firewalls, VPNs, Routing Protocols |
| Cryptographic Protocols | TLS, IPSec, Kerberos, Zero-Knowledge Proofs |

The **8 canonical Saltzer-Schroeder Principles** are:

1. **Least Privilege**
2. **Fail-Safe Defaults**
3. **Economy of Mechanism**
4. **Complete Mediation**
5. **Open Design**
6. **Separation of Privilege**
7. **Least Common Mechanism**
8. **Psychological Acceptability**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for cryptography:** A cryptographically perfect algorithm (e.g., AES-256) deployed inside a poorly designed system (violating *least privilege* or *complete mediation*) is **completely broken in practice**. These principles are the *meta-layer* above the mathematics.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Model

Imagine you are designing a **bank vault** for storing gold bars. Cryptography is the **lock on the vault door** (AES, RSA, SHA-256). But *Fundamental Security Design Principles* are the **architectural decisions** about:

- *Where* the vault is built (Open Design vs. Obscurity)
- *Who* gets a key and to *which drawer* they can access (Least Privilege)
- *What happens* when the power fails (Fail-Safe Defaults)
- *How many doors* a customer must walk through (Defense in Depth / Separation)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world failure case:** The infamous **DEFCON 2015 Jeep Cherokee hack** — Chrysler's cars used a *single* CAN bus with *no* separation of privilege. Researchers remotely killed the engine on a moving car. The cryptography of the cellular module was fine; the **design principles** were violated. This is exactly what Saltzer-Schroeder principles exist to prevent.

### 1.3 The Extended Principle Set (KTU 2024 Modern Additions)

Modern curricula (including KTU 2024 Scheme) augment Saltzer-Schroeder with **3 additional principles**:

9. **Defense in Depth** — Layered security controls
10. **Kerckhoffs's Principle** — *Security through secrecy of key, not algorithm*
11. **Least Astonishment** — System behavior must not surprise the user

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A principle is **violated silently**. There is no compiler warning for "violating least privilege." This is why KTU examiners *love* testing these — they want students who can *reason* about design, not just memorize algorithms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle-by-Principle Operational Breakdown

#### **Principle 1: Least Privilege (LP)**

- **Operational Rule:** Every user, process, or program should operate using the **minimum set of privileges** necessary to complete its task.
- **The "Why":** Limits the **blast radius** of a compromise. If a process is compromised, the attacker inherits *only* its minimal rights.
- **The "How" in Cryptography:** A TLS library should *only* have access to the specific private key file it needs, not all keys in `/etc/ssl/private/`. Implemented via:
  - POSIX file permissions (rwx model)
  - Linux **Capabilities** (drop CAP_SYS_ADMIN if not needed)
  - Mandatory Access Control (SELinux, AppArmor)
  - JWT/SAML scope minimization

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The **Heartbleed bug (2014)** in OpenSSL was catastrophic partly because the vulnerable process ran with elevated privileges. A least-privilege design would have contained the damage.

#### **Principle 2: Fail-Safe Defaults**

- **Operational Rule:** The default state of a system must be **denial of access**. Permission must be explicitly granted, never assumed.
- **The "Why":** A failure (crash, power loss, exception) should *not* grant unauthorized access.
- **The "How":**
  - Default-deny firewall rules (whitelist approach)
  - Default ACL on new database users = `DENY ALL`
  - If a VPN tunnel collapses → fall back to *no network*, not *open network*

> [!TIP]
> **Counter-example to avoid:** Many IoT devices historically shipped with *open* Telnet ports by default — a textbook fail-safe default violation. The **Mirai botnet (2016)** exploited exactly this.

#### **Principle 3: Economy of Mechanism**

- **Operational Rule:** The protection mechanism should be **as simple as possible**. Complex mechanisms are harder to verify and contain more bugs.
- **The "Why":** *Every line of code is a potential vulnerability.* (Roughly **15–50 bugs per 1000 LOC** in production code.)
- **The "How":**
  - Prefer small, auditable primitives (e.g., **NaCl/libsodium** over hand-rolled crypto)
  - Reuse vetted libraries — **never** roll your own crypto
  - Minimize the Trusted Computing Base (**TCB**)

#### **Principle 4: Complete Mediation**

- **Operational Rule:** *Every* access to *every* object must be checked for authorization — **on every access**. Caching of permissions is forbidden.
- **The "Why":** Permissions can change after a check (e.g., user revoked, ACL updated).
- **The "How":**
  - OS-level: page-table revalidation on context switch (hardware-enforced)
  - Web: re-validate session token on every API call, *not just login*
  - Database: row-level security checks on every SELECT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student trap:** Caching authorization decisions in a variable (`isAdmin = true` checked once at login). If admin status is revoked mid-session, the cached value is **stale** — a *Complete Mediation* violation.

#### **Principle 5: Open Design (Kerckhoffs's Doctrine)**

- **Operational Rule:** The security of a system should *not* depend on the secrecy of its design or implementation. **Only the keys** must be secret.
- **The "Why":** History proves that *secret algorithms always leak*. Public scrutiny makes algorithms stronger.
- **The "How":**
  - AES, RSA, SHA-256 are all open standards (NIST competitions)
  - Open-source crypto libraries (OpenSSL, BoringSSL, libsodium)
  - **Never** trust a vendor who says *"our algorithm is proprietary and unbreakable"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Must-Know:** Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883) predates Saltzer-Schroeder by **92 years** and is the philosophical *foundation* of modern cryptography. Auguste Kerckhoffs articulated it in *"La Cryptographie Militaire"*.

#### **Principle 6: Separation of Privilege**

- **Operational Rule:** A system should require *more than one* independent condition to grant access.
- **The "Why":** A single failure (one compromised credential) cannot breach the system.
- **The "How":**
  - Two-factor authentication (password + TOTP)
  - Multi-signature crypto wallets (e.g., 2-of-3 multisig)
  - Dual-control physical keys for nuclear launch

#### **Principle 7: Least Common Mechanism**

- **Operational Rule:** Minimize the amount of mechanism *shared* between different users/processes. Shared mechanisms can become **unintended information channels**.
- **The "Why":** Shared resources (memory, CPU cache, network buffer) can leak data via timing or side-channels.
- **The "How":**
  - Spectre/Meltdown mitigations (cache isolation)
  - Process isolation via containers / VMs
  - Constant-time cryptographic implementations (no data-dependent branches)

#### **Principle 8: Psychological Acceptability**

- **Operational Rule:** Security mechanisms must be **usable**. If users can't or won't use them, they will be bypassed.
- **The "Why":** Humans are the weakest link. A theoretically perfect system that users hate is a failed system.
- **The "How":**
  - Password managers (vs. memorizing 50-character random strings)
  - Single Sign-On (SSO) to reduce cognitive load
  - Clear, non-deceptive security warnings (vs. cryptic browser errors)

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready** summary. Memorize the *operational rule* and *cryptographic example* for each.

| # | Principle | Operational Rule (one-liner) | Cryptographic Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Least Privilege** | Minimize rights per entity | TLS process accesses only its own private key |
| 2 | **Fail-Safe Defaults** | Default = deny access | Default-deny firewall ACL |
| 3 | **Economy of Mechanism** | Keep design simple & small | Use libsodium, not custom AES wrapper |
| 4 | **Complete Mediation** | Check auth on *every* access | Re-validate JWT on every REST API call |
| 5 | **Open Design** | Secrecy is in keys, not design | AES-256 standard, RSA-OAEP |
| 6 | **Separation of Privilege** | Need ≥2 independent conditions | 2FA: password + hardware token |
| 7 | **Least Common Mechanism** | Minimize shared resources | Constant-time AES to defeat cache-timing |
| 8 | **Psychological Acceptability** | Must be usable by humans | Password managers, biometric unlock |
| 9 | **Defense in Depth** | Layer multiple controls | VPN + TLS + App-layer encryption |
| 10 | **Kerckhoffs's Principle** | Algorithm public, keys private | Public RSA modulus $N=pq$ |
| 11 | **Least Astonishment** | Behavior should match user expectation | Standard HTTP error codes, no silent failures |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **production engineering** (the actual KTU graduate outcome), these principles are evaluated in:

- **Security Architecture Reviews** (SAR) — every system goes through this checklist
- **Threat Modeling** (STRIDE / PASTA frameworks) — principles map to threat mitigation
- **Compliance Audits** — PCI-DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001 explicitly require these principles
- **Code Review** — security linters (e.g., Semgrep, CodeQL) flag principle violations

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Mantra:** *"Design is 80% of security. Cryptography is the last 20%."*
