(2 marks) - **Phase 2 — PRGA:** Increment $i$, update $j = (j + S[i]) \bmod 256$, swap $S[i], S[j]$, output $K_{byte} = S[(S[i]+S[j]) \bmod 256]$, XOR with plaintext byte. (2 marks) **Model Solution (b):** - $K = \{0x41, 0x42\}$, $L = 2$, $P = \{0x41, 0x43, 0x54\}$. (1 mark) - **KSA** loop shown for $i = 0, 1, \dots, 255$ — only first few swaps need to be shown for marks. (2 marks) - **PRGA** steps generating 3 keystream bytes $K_1, K_2, K_3$. (2 marks) - Final ciphertext: $C_n = P_n \oplus K_n$. (2 marks) --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Stream cipher = plaintext XOR keystream; key is the **seed**, not the keystream itself. - **Synchronous** stream ciphers depend only on key and clock; **self-synchronising** depend on previous ciphertext. - The keystream must be **pseudo-random** with long period, balance, and unpredictability. - **LFSR** feedback uses XOR over $\text{GF}(2)$: $s_t = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{n} c_i s_{t-i}$. - **Characteristic polynomial** $p(x) = 1 + c_1 x + \dots + c_n x^n$ must be **primitive** to achieve max period $2^n - 1$. - **Linear complexity** is the smallest LFSR length that reproduces the sequence; high linear complexity = harder to attack. - **RC4** has two phases: KSA (initial permutation, 256 iterations) and PRGA (keystream generation). Output keystream byte $S[(S[i]+S[j]) \bmod 256]$. - **Berlekamp–Massey algorithm** can recover LFSR complexity from $2L$ consecutive keystream bits — so a single LFSR is insecure alone. - Modern solutions: **Multiple LFSRs + Non-linear combiner**, or dedicated designs like **ChaCha20, Salsa20**. - **One-Time Pad** = perfect stream cipher but **impractical** due to key length and single-use constraints.

topics.title: Principles of Public-Key Cryptosystems: Public-Key Cryptosystems, Applications for Public-Key Cryptosystems, Requirements for Public-Key Cryptography, The RSA Algorithm, Description of the Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Principles of Public-Key Cryptosystems: Public-Key Cryptosystems, Applications for Public-Key Cryptosystems, Requirements for Public-Key Cryptography, The RSA Algorithm, Description of the Algorithm # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Public-Key Cryptosystems — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Public-Key Cryptography (Asymmetric Cryptography)** is a cryptographic framework that uses a mathematically related pair of keys — a **public key** (freely distributed) and a **private key** (kept secret) — to perform encryption, decryption, digital signing, and key exchange operations, fundamentally eliminating the need to share a single secret key over an insecure channel. The concept was first introduced publicly by **Whitfield Diffie** and **Martin Hellman** in their seminal 1976 paper *"New Directions in Cryptography."* It solves the most critical weakness of symmetric cryptography: **the secure distribution of the shared secret key**. ### 1.1.1 Conventional vs. Public-Key Cryptography | Feature | Conventional (Symmetric) | Public-Key (Asymmetric) | |---|---|---| | Key Used | Single shared secret key | Pair: Public + Private | | Key Distribution | Major problem; must be secret | Public key is openly shared | | Number of Keys for *n* users | $n(n-1)/2$ keys | $2n$ keys | | Speed | **Fast** | **Slower** (100–1000×) | | Primary Use | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, signatures | | Confidentiality Basis | Shared secret | Mathematical one-way function | ### 1.1.2 Components of a Public-Key System A public-key cryptosystem is formally defined as a **sextuple** of components: $$(M, C, K, E, D, \text{protocol})$$ Where: - **$M$** = Set of plaintext messages - **$C$** = Set of ciphertext messages - **$K$** = Key space (set of all possible key pairs) - **$E$** = Encryption algorithm (uses public key) - **$D$** = Decryption algorithm (uses private key) - **Protocol** = Rules for key usage ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mailbox Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — Think of a Public Key as a Mailbox Slot:** > > Imagine a transparent mailbox with two slots: > > 1. **The Open Slot (Public Key):** Anyone in the world can drop a letter through it — but no one can retrieve anything from it. > 2. **The Private Compartment (Private Key):** Only the owner with the right key can open the back and collect the letters. > > Just like the mailbox, the public key lets everyone **send**, but only the private key lets one **receive**. Mathematically, encrypting is easy, but reversing it without the private key is computationally infeasible. ### 1.3 The Six Essential Requirements for a Public-Key Cryptosystem > [!IMPORTANT] > For a cryptosystem to be considered secure and functional, **Diffie and Hellman** stated that it must satisfy the following six conditions: 1. **Efficient Key Pair Generation:** It is computationally easy to generate a key pair $(PU_b, PR_b)$ for any user $b$. 2. **Efficient Encryption:** Given the public key $PU_b$ and plaintext $M$, it is easy for sender $A$ to compute $C = E(PU_b, M)$. 3. **Efficient Decryption:** Given the ciphertext $C$ and private key $PR_b$, it is easy for receiver $B$ to recover $M = D(PR_b, C)$. 4. **Infeasible Key Recovery:** It is computationally infeasible for an opponent to determine the private key $PR_b$ given the public key $PU_b$. 5. **Infeasible Plaintext Recovery:** It is computationally infeasible for an opponent to recover $M$ from $C$ and $PU_b$. 6. **Key Symmetry (Optional but Desirable):** Either key can be used for encryption with the other used for decryption: $$M = D(PU_b, E(PR_b, M)) = D(PR_b, E(PU_b, M))$$ ### 1.4 Applications of Public-Key Cryptosystems Public-key cryptography supports **three primary categories** of applications: > [!NOTE] > **The 3 Pillars of Public-Key Applications:** > > 1. **Encryption/Decryption** — Confidentiality of messages > 2. **Digital Signatures** — Authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation > 3. **Key Exchange / Key Agreement** — Secure establishment of shared secrets #### Detailed Applications: | Application Category | Purpose | Typical Algorithms | |---|---|---| | **Confidentiality (Encryption)** | Hide message contents from eavesdroppers | RSA, ElGamal | | **Digital Signatures** | Verify sender identity and message integrity | RSA, DSA, ECDSA | | **Key Exchange** | Securely share a symmetric session key | Diffie-Hellman, RSA | | **Identity Authentication** | Prove identity without revealing secrets | Challenge-Response | | **Non-Repudiation** | Prevent sender from denying they sent a message | RSA Signatures | ### 1.5 RSA Algorithm — Formal Introduction > [!IMPORTANT] > **RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman)** is the most widely deployed public-key cryptosystem in the world. It was invented in **1977** by Ron **R**ivest, Adi **S**hamir, and Leonard **A**dleman at MIT. Its security is based on the **practical difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers**. RSA supports all three application categories (encryption, signatures, key exchange) and operates entirely on modular exponentiation over the integers modulo $n = p \times q$. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Public-Key Cryptography The entire framework of public-key cryptography rests on **one-way trapdoor functions**. ### 2.1.1 One-Way Functions > [!NOTE] > A **One-Way Function** $f$ is a function that is easy to compute in the forward direction ($y = f(x)$) but computationally infeasible to invert ($x = f^{-1}(y)$) for almost all $y$. **Examples of One-Way Functions:** - **Integer Factorization:** Multiplying two large primes is easy; factoring their product is hard. - **Discrete Logarithm:** Computing $a^x \bmod p$ is easy; finding $x$ given $a$, $y$, and $p$ is hard. - **Hashing:** Computing SHA-256 of data is easy; reversing it to find original data is hard. ### 2.1.2 Trapdoor One-Way Functions A **trapdoor one-way function** is a one-way function whose inverse becomes **easy to compute** if you possess a specific secret piece of information (the **trapdoor**). - **RSA Trapdoor:** Factoring $n = p \times q$ is hard. BUT if you know $p$ and $q$ (the trapdoor), you can easily decrypt. ### 2.2 Public-Key Cryptosystem — Structural Workflow The operational flow of a public-key system involves **three distinct phases**: 1. **Key Generation Phase:** Each user independently generates a public-private key pair. 2. **Encryption Phase:** Sender uses recipient's public key to encrypt. 3. **Decryption Phase:** Recipient uses their private key to decrypt. ### 2.3 RSA — Detailed Algorithm Steps #### Step 1: Key Generation (Performed by Recipient, say Bob) | Step | Operation | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | Choose primes | Select two large random primes $p$ and $q$ | | 2 | Compute $n$ | $n = p \times q$ (modulus for both keys) | | 3 | Compute $\phi(n)$ | Euler's totient: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | | 4 | Choose $e$ | Pick $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | | 5 | Compute $d$ | Find $d$ as modular inverse: $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | | 6 | Publish | Public key: $PU = \{e, n\}$; Private key: $PR = \{d, n\}$ | #### Step 2: Encryption (Performed by Sender, say Alice) For plaintext $M$ (where $0 < M < n$): $$C = M^e \bmod n$$ #### Step 3: Decryption (Performed by Recipient Bob) For received ciphertext $C$: $$M = C^d \bmod n$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD RSA FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THIS TABLE:** | # | Formula | Meaning | Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $n = p \cdot q$ | RSA modulus | $p, q$ are large primes | | 2 | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's totient function | Used for key generation | | 3 | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | Public exponent coprime to $\phi(n)$ | $1 < e < \phi(n)$ | | 4 | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Public-private key relation | Defines the trapdoor | | 5 | $C = M^e \bmod n$ | Encryption | $0 < M < n$ | | 6 | $M = C^d \bmod n$ | Decryption | $0 < C < n$ | | 7 | $M^{ed} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ | Correctness (Euler's theorem) | Always holds for valid keys | | 8 | $\gcd(M, n) = 1$ | Required for Euler's theorem | If not, padding (OAEP) used | | 9 | $\text{Key Size} = \vert k \vert$ | $k$-bit security | Modern standard: $k \geq 2048$ | | 10 | $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ | Extended Euclidean algorithm | Computed once at keygen | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of RSA in Engineering RSA is not just an academic curiosity — it is the backbone of modern internet security: > [!NOTE] > **Where RSA is Used in Real Engineering Systems:** > > - **HTTPS/TLS Handshake:** RSA encrypts the symmetric session key exchanged during the initial TLS handshake. > - **Digital Certificates (X.509):** RSA signatures authenticate websites and software publishers. > - **Email Security (PGP/GPG):** RSA encrypts email session keys and signs messages. > - **Code Signing:** Software updates are signed with RSA to verify authenticity. > - **Blockchain & Cryptocurrencies:** Early Bitcoin used ECDSA (related); some alt-coins use RSA. > - **Smart Cards & IoT:** RSA authenticates devices in industrial control systems. ### 2.6 Why RSA Works — Euler's Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Euler's Theorem:** For any integer $a$ coprime to $n$: > $$a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$ **Proof sketch of RSA correctness:** Since $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$: $$M^{ed} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv M \cdot 1^k \equiv M \pmod{n}$$ --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Example ## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Complete RSA Lifecycle > [!NOTE] > **Example Setup:** Bob wants to receive secure messages. He generates an RSA key pair using small primes $p = 17$ and $q = 11$ (educational purposes only). Alice sends him a secret message $M = 88$. ### 3.1.1 Phase 1: Key Generation by Bob **Step 1: Choose two large random primes.** Bob selects $p = 17$ and $q = 11$. Both are prime numbers. **Step 2: Compute the modulus $n$.** $$ \begin{aligned} n &= p \times q \\ &= 17 \times 11 \\ &= 187 \end{aligned} $$ > *Result:* $n = 187$. This will appear in both public and private keys. **Step 3: Compute Euler's totient function $\phi(n)$.** $$ \begin{aligned} \phi(n) &= (p - 1)(q - 1) \\ &= (17 - 1)(11 - 1) \\ &= 16 \times 10 \\ &= 160 \end{aligned} $$ > *Result:* $\phi(n) = 160$. This is the count of integers in $[1, 187]$ that are coprime to $187$. **Step 4: Choose the public exponent $e$.** We need $e$ such that $1 < e < 160$ and $\gcd(e, 160) = 1$. Testing candidates: - $\gcd(3, 160) = 1$ ✓ - $\gcd(5, 160) = 5$ ✗ - $\gcd(7, 160) = 1$ ✓ - $\gcd(11, 160) = 1$ ✓ - $\gcd(13, 160) = 1$ ✓ Bob chooses $e = 7$ (commonly used small prime). **Step 5: Compute the private exponent $d$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.** We need $d$ such that: $$ \begin{aligned} e \cdot d &\equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} \\ 7 \cdot d &\equiv 1 \pmod{160} \end{aligned} $$ Apply Extended Euclidean Algorithm: $$ \begin{aligned} 160 &= 7 \times 22 + 6 \quad &\text{(since } 7 \times 22 = 154,\ 160 - 154 = 6) \\ 7 &= 6 \times 1 + 1 \quad &\text{(since } 6 \times 1 = 6,\ 7 - 6 = 1) \\ 6 &= 1 \times 6 + 0 \quad &\text{(algorithm terminates)} \end{aligned} $$ **Back-substitute to find the inverse:** $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 7 - 6 \times 1 \\ &= 7 - (160 - 7 \times 22) \times 1 \\ &= 7 - 160 + 7 \times 22 \\ &= 7 \times 23 - 160 \times 1 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $7 \times 23 \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$ So: $d = 23$ > *Verification:* $7 \times 23 = 161 = 160 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$ ✓ **Step 6: Publish the keys.** $$PU = \{e = 7,\ n = 187\}$$ $$PR = \{d = 23,\ n = 187\}$$ ### 3.1.2 Phase 2: Encryption by Alice Alice looks up Bob's public key $(e = 7, n = 187)$ and encrypts $M = 88$. **Step 1: Verify $M < n$.** $88 < 187$ ✓ **Step 2: Compute ciphertext $C = M^e \bmod n$.** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= 88^7 \bmod 187 \end{aligned} $$ Compute using **successive squaring** to avoid huge numbers: **Square 1:** $88^2 \bmod 187$ $$ \begin{aligned} 88^2 &= 7744 \\ 7744 \bmod 187 &= 7744 - 41 \times 187 = 7744 - 7667 = 77 \end{aligned} $$ So $88^2 \equiv 77 \pmod{187}$. **Square 2:** $88^4 \bmod 187 = (88^2)^2 \bmod 187 = 77^2 \bmod 187$ $$ \begin{aligned} 77^2 &= 5929 \\ 5929 \bmod 187 &= 5929 - 31 \times 187 = 5929 - 5797 = 132 \end{aligned} $$ So $88^4 \equiv 132 \pmod{187}$. **Express $88^7 = 88^4 \times 88^2 \times 88^1$ (binary of 7 = 111).** $$ \begin{aligned} 88^7 &\equiv 88^4 \times 88^2 \times 88^1 \pmod{187} \\ &\equiv 132 \times 77 \times 88 \pmod{187} \end{aligned} $$ **Intermediate product 1:** $132 \times 77 = 10164$ $$ 10164 \bmod 187 = 10164 - 54 \times 187 = 10164 - 10098 = 66 $$ **Intermediate product 2:** $66 \times 88 = 5808$ $$ 5808 \bmod 187 = 5808 - 31 \times 187 = 5808 - 5797 = 11 $$ > *Result:* $C = 11$. Alice transmits $C = 11$ over the insecure channel. ### 3.1.3 Phase 3: Decryption by Bob Bob receives $C = 11$ and uses his private key $(d = 23, n = 187)$. **Compute $M = C^d \bmod n = 11^{23} \bmod 187$.** Express $23$ in binary: $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = (10111)_2$. **Successive squaring of 11:** **$11^1 \equiv 11$** **$11^2 \bmod 187$:** $121 \bmod 187 = 121$ **$11^4 \bmod 187$:** $121^2 = 14641$ $$ 14641 \bmod 187 = 14641 - 78 \times 187 = 14641 - 14586 = 55 $$ So $11^4 \equiv 55 \pmod{187}$. **$11^8 \bmod 187$:** $55^2 = 3025$ $$ 3025 \bmod 187 = 3025 - 16 \times 187 = 3025 - 2992 = 33 $$ So $11^8 \equiv 33 \pmod{187}$. **$11^{16} \bmod 187$:** $33^2 = 1089$ $$ 1089 \bmod 187 = 1089 - 5 \times 187 = 1089 - 935 = 154 $$ So $11^{16} \equiv 154 \pmod{187}$. **Combine for $11^{23} = 11^{16} \times 11^4 \times 11^2 \times 11^1$:** $$ \begin{aligned} 11^{23} &\equiv 154 \times 55 \times 121 \times 11 \pmod{187} \end{aligned} $$ **Product 1:** $154 \times 55 = 8470$ $$ 8470 \bmod 187 = 8470 - 45 \times 187 = 8470 - 8415 = 55 $$ **Product 2:** $55 \times 121 = 6655$ $$ 6655 \bmod 187 = 6655 - 35 \times 187 = 6655 - 6545 = 110 $$ **Product 3:** $110 \times 11 = 1210$ $$ 1210 \bmod 187 = 1210 - 6 \times 187 = 1210 - 1122 = 88 $$ > **Result:** $M = 88$ ✓ — Bob successfully recovered Alice's original message! ### 3.2 Verification of the Trapdoor Property Notice that without knowing $d = 23$, an attacker seeing $C = 11$ and the public key $(e = 7, n = 187)$ must solve $M^7 \equiv 11 \pmod{187}$ to recover $M$. This requires either: 1. Factoring $n = 187 = 17 \times 11$ (easy here because of small primes, but **infeasible** for 2048-bit primes), OR 2. Computing the discrete logarithm $7 \cdot \log_M(11) \pmod{187}$ (also infeasible for large $n$). ### 3.3 Summary Table of the Complete Example | Stage | Value | Symbol | |---|---|---| | Primes | $17, 11$ | $p, q$ | | Modulus | $187$ | $n$ | | Totient | $160$ | $\phi(n)$ | | Public exponent | $7$ | $e$ | | Private exponent | $23$ | $d$ | | Public Key | $\{7, 187\}$ | $PU$ | | Private Key | $\{23, 187\}$ | $PR$ | | Plaintext | $88$ | $M$ | | Ciphertext | $11$ | $C$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Diagram — Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Key Distribution This diagram illustrates the fundamental difference between symmetric and asymmetric cryptography, particularly the key distribution challenge. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SK["SYMMETRIC KEY MODEL"] A1["Alice
Sender"] -- "Same Secret Key K
(must be shared securely)" --> B1["Bob
Receiver"] B1 -. "Eve intercepts K?" .-> E1["Eve
Eavesdropper"] end subgraph PK["PUBLIC-KEY MODEL"] A2["Alice
Sender"] -- "Encrypts with
Bob's PUBLIC key e" --> B2["Bob
Receiver"] B2 -- "Decrypts with
PRIVATE key d" --> A2 PUB["Public Key Directory
PU = e, n"] -. "Bob publishes e,n" .-> A2 E2["Eve
Eavesdropper"] -. "Knows e but NOT d" .-> B2 end style SK fill:#FFE4E1 style PK fill:#E0FFE0 style A1 fill:#FFD700 style B1 fill:#FFD700 style A2 fill:#87CEEB style B2 fill:#87CEEB style E1 fill:#FF6347 style E2 fill:#FF6347 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Left (Symmetric):** Alice and Bob share ONE secret key $K$. The vulnerability is that Eve could intercept $K$ during distribution. - **Right (Asymmetric):** Alice freely obtains Bob's public key $e$ from a public directory. Eve can also see $e$, but without the private $d$, she cannot decrypt. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — RSA Algorithm Lifecycle The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete functional architecture of the RSA algorithm across key generation, encryption, and decryption phases. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph KG["PHASE 1: KEY GENERATION BY BOB"] K1["Generate two large
random primes p and q"] --> K2["Compute modulus
n = p x q"] K2 --> K3["Compute totient
phi n = p-1 x q-1"] K3 --> K4["Choose public exponent e
such that gcd e phi n = 1"] K4 --> K5["Compute private exponent d
using Extended Euclidean
e x d mod phi n = 1"] K5 --> K6["Output Public Key PU = e,n
and Private Key PR = d,n"] end subgraph EN["PHASE 2: ENCRYPTION BY ALICE"] E1["Obtain Bob's public key
PU = e,n from directory"] --> E2["Read plaintext message M
where 0 less than M less than n"] E2 --> E3["Compute ciphertext
C = M power e mod n"] E3 --> E4["Transmit C over
insecure channel"] end subgraph DE["PHASE 3: DECRYPTION BY BOB"] D1["Receive ciphertext C"] --> D2["Use private key PR = d,n"] D2 --> D3["Recover plaintext
M = C power d mod n"] D3 --> D4["Read original message M"] end K6 -- "Publish PU = e,n to directory" --> E1 E4 -- "Send C to Bob" --> D1 style KG fill:#FFF8DC style EN fill:#E6F3FF style DE fill:#F0FFF0 style K1 fill:#FFD700 style K6 fill:#FFA500 style E3 fill:#4682B4 style D3 fill:#228B22 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The three colored subgraphs represent the **three sequential phases** of an RSA session. - Arrows crossing subgraphs (e.g., from K6 to E1) show **inter-phase data transfer** (public key publication, ciphertext transmission). - Note that the **private key $d$ never leaves Bob's system** — it is only used inside Phase 3. ## 4.3 Process Flow — Modular Exponentiation (Square-and-Multiply) This diagram shows how $M^e \bmod n$ is computed efficiently using repeated squaring (essential for large exponents). ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["Start with M
and exponent e
in binary form"] --> P1["Initialize result = 1"] P1 --> P2{"Next bit of e?"} P2 -- "Bit = 1" --> P3["Square the result
result = result x result mod n"] P3 --> P4["Multiply by base
result = result x M mod n"] P4 --> P5{"More bits
remaining?"} P2 -- "Bit = 0" --> P6["Square the result
result = result x result mod n"] P6 --> P5 P5 -- "Yes" --> P2 P5 -- "No" --> P7["Output final
result = M power e mod n"] style P0 fill:#FFE4B5 style P7 fill:#90EE90 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - This **square-and-multiply** technique converts an $O(e)$ multiplication process into an $O(\log_2 e)$ process. - Example: Computing $M^{23}$ requires only 5 squarings + 3 multiplications instead of 22 multiplications. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three applications of public-key cryptosystems.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Three applications of Public-Key Cryptosystems:** > > 1. **Encryption/Decryption:** Used to provide confidentiality. The sender encrypts the plaintext using the recipient's public key, and only the recipient's private key can decrypt it (e.g., RSA). > > 2. **Digital Signatures:** Used to provide authentication, data integrity, and non-repudiation. The sender signs a message using their private key, and anyone can verify the signature using the sender's public key (e.g., RSA signatures). > > 3. **Key Exchange:** Used to securely establish a shared symmetric session key over an insecure channel (e.g., Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange, RSA-based key transport). ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What are the essential requirements for a public-key cryptosystem?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Diffie-Hellman Requirements for Public-Key Cryptography:** > > 1. It is computationally easy for a party $B$ to generate a public-private key pair $(PU_b, PR_b)$. > 2. It is computationally easy for a sender $A$, knowing the public key $PU_b$ and plaintext $M$, to generate the corresponding ciphertext $C = E(PU_b, M)$. > 3. It is computationally easy for the receiver $B$ to recover the original message $M = D(PR_b, C)$ using the private key. > 4. It is computationally infeasible for an opponent, knowing the public key $PU_b$, to determine the private key $PR_b$. > 5. It is computationally infeasible for an opponent, knowing $PU_b$ and $C$, to recover the original message $M$. > 6. The encryption and decryption functions can be applied in either order: $M = D(PU_b, E(PR_b, M)) = D(PR_b, E(PU_b, M))$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the RSA algorithm with a neat block diagram. Describe the key generation, encryption, and decryption phases clearly. #### Model Solution: **Introduction:** RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman) is a public-key cryptosystem invented in 1977. It is based on the computational difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers. **Phase 1: Key Generation by Receiver (Bob)** The following table summarizes Bob's key generation steps: | Step | Operation | Explanation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Select $p, q$ | Pick two large random primes $p$ and $q$ | | 2 | $n = p \times q$ | Compute the modulus $n$ | | 3 | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Compute Euler's totient | | 4 | Choose $e$ | $1 < e < \phi(n)$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | | 5 | $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ | Compute $d$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm | | 6 | Publish $PU = \{e, n\}$ | Public key is announced openly | **Phase 2: Encryption by Sender (Alice)** Using Bob's public key $(e, n)$, Alice computes: $$C = M^e \bmod n$$ Where $M$ is the plaintext message satisfying $0 < M < n$. **Phase 3: Decryption by Receiver (Bob)** Using his private key $(d, n)$, Bob computes: $$M = C^d \bmod n$$ **Block Diagram (textual representation):** ``` BOB (Receiver) DIRECTORY ALICE (Sender) +---------------+ +-----------+ +---------------+ | 1. Choose p,q | | | | | | 2. n = p*q | | PUBLIC | | | | 3. phi(n) | | KEY | | | | 4. Choose e |------> | e, n | <----- | 1. Lookup e,n | | 5. Compute d | publish | | fetch | 2. C = M^e%n | | 6. Keep d | | | | 3. Send C --|----+ +---------------+ +-----------+ +---------------+ | ^ | | v +------------------ M = C^d % n <---- Receive C -------------+ ``` **Mathematical Correctness (using Euler's Theorem):** Since $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, we have $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$. Therefore: $$C^d = (M^e)^d = M^{ed} = M^{1+k\phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^k \equiv M \cdot 1^k \equiv M \pmod{n}$$ This proves RSA correctly recovers the original plaintext. **(b) [7 Marks]** In an RSA cryptosystem, given $p = 61$, $q = 53$, and $e = 17$, perform key generation, encrypt $M = 65$, and then decrypt the ciphertext. #### Model Solution: **Step 1: Compute modulus $n$.** $$ \begin{aligned} n &= p \times q = 61 \times 53 \\ &= 61 \times 50 + 61 \times 3 = 3050 + 183 = 3233 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$.** $$ \begin{aligned} \phi(n) &= (p-1)(q-1) = (60)(52) = 3120 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.** $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$ ✓ (since 17 is prime and 17 does not divide 3120) **Step 4: Compute $d$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.** We need $d$ such that $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$. Apply Euclidean algorithm: $$ \begin{aligned} 3120 &= 17 \times 183 + 9 \\ 17 &= 9 \times 1 + 8 \\ 9 &= 8 \times 1 + 1 \\ 8 &= 1 \times 8 + 0 \end{aligned} $$ Back-substitution: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 9 - 8 \times 1 \\ &= 9 - (17 - 9 \times 1) \times 1 = 2 \times 9 - 17 \\ &= 2(3120 - 17 \times 183) - 17 \\ &= 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17 \end{aligned} $$ So $-367 \times 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$ Therefore: $d \equiv -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 = 2753 \pmod{3120}$ > **Verification:** $17 \times 2753 = 46801 = 15 \times 3120 + 1 = 46800 + 1 = 46801$ ✓ **Public Key:** $PU = \{e = 17, n = 3233\}$ **Private Key:** $PR = \{d = 2753, n = 3233\}$ **Step 5: Encrypt $M = 65$.** $$ C = M^e \bmod n = 65^{17} \bmod 3233 $$ Using successive squaring: - $65^1 = 65$ - $65^2 = 4225 \bmod 3233 = 992$ - $65^4 = 992^2 = 984064 \bmod 3233$. Compute: $984064 / 3233 \approx 304.4$, $304 \times 3233 = 982832$, $984064 - 982832 = 1232$. So $65^4 \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$. - $65^8 = 1232^2 = 1517824 \bmod 3233$. $1517824 / 3233 \approx 469.4$, $469 \times 3233 = 1516277$, $1517824 - 1516277 = 1547$. So $65^8 \equiv 1547 \pmod{3233}$. - $65^{16} = 1547^2 = 2393209 \bmod 3233$. $2393209 / 3233 \approx 740.3$, $740 \times 3233 = 2392420$, $2393209 - 2392420 = 789$. So $65^{16} \equiv 789 \pmod{3233}$. Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so: $$ 65^{17} = 65^{16} \times 65^1 \equiv 789 \times 65 \pmod{3233} $$ $$ 789 \times 65 = 51285 \bmod 3233 $$ $51285 / 3233 \approx 15.86$, $15 \times 3233 = 48495$, $51285 - 48495 = 2790$. > **Ciphertext:** $C = 2790$ **Step 6: Decrypt $C = 2790$.** $$ M = C^d \bmod n = 2790^{2753} \bmod 3233 $$ Using Euler's theorem: $M = 2790^{2753} \bmod 3233 \equiv 65$ (verified by the mathematical proof of correctness). > **Recovered plaintext:** $M = 65$ ✓ --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of public-key cryptography. Compare it with conventional (symmetric) cryptography. List its applications. #### Model Solution: **Concept of Public-Key Cryptography:** Public-key cryptography (also called asymmetric cryptography) is a cryptographic system that uses **two mathematically related but distinct keys**: a public key (advertised openly) and a private key (kept secret). Any message encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted by the corresponding private key. The concept was proposed by Diffie and Hellman in 1976 to solve the key distribution problem inherent in symmetric cryptography. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Conventional (Symmetric) | Public-Key (Asymmetric) | |---|---|---| | **Keys Used** | Single shared secret key | Key pair: public + private | | **Key Distribution** | Major challenge — must be secure | Public key distributed openly | | **Number of Keys (n users)** | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2n$ | | **Algorithms** | AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish | RSA, ECC, ElGamal | | **Speed** | Fast (suitable for bulk data) | Slower (100–1000× slower) | | **Confidentiality Basis** | Shared secret knowledge | Mathematical one-way function | | **Primary Use** | Bulk encryption of data | Key exchange, digital signatures | | **Authentication** | Not inherent | Inherent via digital signatures | | **Key Length for Security** | 128 bits sufficient | 2048+ bits needed | **Three Applications:** 1. **Confidentiality through Encryption:** Alice retrieves Bob's public key, encrypts a message with it, and only Bob can decrypt using his private key. Example: RSA encryption in HTTPS. 2. **Digital Signatures for Authentication and Non-Repudiation:** Alice signs a document using her private key. Anyone with Alice's public key can verify the signature. This proves authenticity and prevents Alice from denying she sent it. Example: RSA signatures in PGP email. 3. **Key Exchange / Secure Key Establishment:** Two parties establish a shared symmetric key over an insecure channel without prior shared secrets. Example: Diffie-Hellman key exchange is used to derive a symmetric session key for TLS. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the requirements that must be satisfied by a public-key cryptosystem. Explain with a suitable example why RSA satisfies these requirements. #### Model Solution: **The Six Requirements of Public-Key Cryptography:** > [!IMPORTANT] > For a cryptosystem to qualify as a public-key system, **Diffie and Hellman** specified six requirements: 1. **Efficient Key Pair Generation:** Generating a key pair $(PU_b, PR_b)$ must be computationally easy. *In RSA:* Generating random primes $p, q$ and computing $n, e, d$ is straightforward using probabilistic primality tests (e.g., Miller-Rabin). 2. **Efficient Encryption:** Computing $C = E(PU_b, M)$ must be easy. *In RSA:* Computing $M^e \bmod n$ uses the square-and-multiply algorithm in $O(\log e)$ time. 3. **Efficient Decryption:** Computing $M = D(PR_b, C)$ must be easy. *In RSA:* Computing $C^d \bmod n$ similarly takes $O(\log d)$ time. 4. **Infeasible Key Recovery:** An attacker knowing $PU_b$ cannot determine $PR_b$. *In RSA:* Computing $d$ from $(e, n)$ requires knowledge of $\phi(n)$, which in turn requires factoring $n$. Factoring a 2048-bit modulus is computationally infeasible with current technology. 5. **Infeasible Plaintext Recovery:** An attacker knowing $C$ and $PU_b$ cannot recover $M$. *In RSA:* Without $d$, recovering $M$ requires either solving the RSA problem directly or factoring $n$ — both infeasible. 6. **Key Order Symmetry:** Either key can encrypt with the other decrypting. *In RSA:* Both $M^{ed} \bmod n$ and $M^{de} \bmod n$ yield $M$. **Why RSA Specifically Satisfies These:** - **Easy key generation:** Primes can be found quickly; multiplication is fast. - **Hard factoring:** While multiplying $p \times q = n$ is trivial, the reverse (factoring $n$) has no known polynomial-time algorithm for large numbers. - **Trapdoor:** The factorization $p \times q$ serves as the **trapdoor**. Knowing $p$ and $q$ allows immediate computation of $d$, but without them, $d$ is unreachable. - **Correctness guaranteed by Euler's theorem:** The relation $M^{ed} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ holds whenever $\gcd(M, n) = 1$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing which key does what:** Students often mix up that the **public key encrypts** and the **private key decrypts** in confidentiality mode. (Reversed for digital signatures!) > 2. **Forgetting the totient step:** Many students compute $d$ directly without first computing $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$. > 3. **Wrong modulus verification:** In RSA decryption, the result is taken $\bmod n$, not $\bmod \phi(n)$. The decryption formula is $M = C^d \bmod n$. > 4. **Missing the $M < n$ condition:** Always verify plaintext is less than $n$ before encryption; otherwise, use padding (OAEP). > 5. **Listing only 4–5 requirements:** KTU expects **all six** requirements to be enumerated for full 7 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3: Principles of Public-Key Cryptosystems** ### Core Concepts to Remember - **Public-Key Cryptography** uses **two keys**: a public key (shared) and a private key (secret). - Each user has a **unique key pair**; this eliminates the symmetric key distribution problem. - The system is based on **one-way trapdoor functions** — easy to compute forward, infeasible to invert without the trapdoor. - **RSA** stands for **R**ivest, **S**hamir, **A**dleman; invented in **1977**. - RSA security relies on the **integer factorization problem** (factoring $n = p \times q$). ### Six Requirements (must enumerate all) 1. Easy key generation 2. Easy encryption 3. Easy decryption 4. Infeasible to find private key from public key 5. Infeasible to recover plaintext from ciphertext + public key 6. Either key can encrypt, other decrypts (key symmetry) ### Three Application Categories 1. **Encryption/Decryption** → Confidentiality 2. **Digital Signatures** → Authentication, integrity, non-repudiation 3. **Key Exchange** → Secure session key establishment ### RSA Algorithm in One Glance - **Key Generation:** $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ - **Public Key:** $(e, n)$ - **Private Key:** $(d, n)$ - **Encryption:** $C = M^e \bmod n$ - **Decryption:** $M = C^d \bmod n$ - **Correctness:** $M^{ed} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ (Euler's theorem) ### Numerical Must-Knows - For $p = 17, q = 11$: $n = 187$, $\phi(n) = 160$, choose $e = 7$, then $d = 23$. - Always verify $ed \bmod \phi(n) = 1$. - Use **square-and-multiply** for modular exponentiation. - Modern RSA uses **2048-bit keys minimum** (NIST recommendation). ### Exam Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Using $\bmod \phi(n)$ instead of $\bmod n$ in decryption. - ❌ Confusing $e$ and $d$ roles in encryption vs. digital signatures. - ❌ Forgetting to state $\gcd(M, n) = 1$ assumption. - ❌ Skipping the proof of correctness using Euler's theorem. - ❌ Not mentioning OAEP padding in modern practical RSA.

topics.title: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔐 Module 3 — Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (DHKE)** is a public-key cryptographic protocol proposed by **Whitfield Diffie** and **Martin Hellman** in **1976**. It enables two communicating parties — traditionally named **Alice** and **Bob** — to derive a **shared symmetric secret key** over an **insecure (public) communication channel** *without* having met previously to exchange any secret material.

The security of the protocol rests on the **computational intractability of the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in a finite cyclic group. In its most common form, computations are performed in the **multiplicative group of integers modulo a large prime** $p$, denoted $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PECST637 (Fundamentals of Cryptography) treats DHKE as the canonical example of a *key-establishment* protocol built on number-theoretic hardness. The 2024 scheme places it alongside RSA, ElGamal, and elliptic-curve variants under "Public-Key Cryptosystems."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Paint Mixing Puzzle" 🎨

Imagine Alice and Bob are in two separate rooms connected by a hallway where an eavesdropper **Eve** can watch every exchange.

1. Alice and Bob **publicly agree on a common paint color** — say, **YELLOW** (this is the public parameter $g$).
2. Alice secretly picks a color (her **private key** $a$) and mixes it with YELLOW → sends the **mixture** to Bob (this is her public value $A$).
3. Bob secretly picks a color (his **private key** $b$) and mixes it with YELLOW → sends the **mixture** to Alice (this is his public value $B$).
4. Eve sees both mixtures but **cannot** easily separate the agreed YELLOW from each person's secret color (the **DLP**).
5. Alice takes Bob's mixture and **adds her secret color** to it.
6. Bob takes Alice's mixture and **adds his secret color** to it.
7. Both arrive at the **same final secret color** — the **shared key** $K$.

The magic: although Alice and Bob each add a *different* secret color, the final mixture is identical because mixing is **commutative** ($Y + a + b = Y + b + a$).

### 1.3 Notation & Terminology

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $p$ | A large **prime number** (public modulus) |
| $g$ | A **primitive root** (generator) modulo $p$ (public base) |
| $a$ | Alice's **private key** (random integer, $1 \leq a \leq p-2$) |
| $b$ | Bob's **private key** (random integer, $1 \leq b \leq p-2$) |
| $A$ | Alice's **public value** $= g^{a} \bmod p$ |
| $B$ | Bob's **public value** $= g^{b} \bmod p$ |
| $K$ | **Shared secret key** $= g^{ab} \bmod p$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Constants in DHKE are the prime $p$ (typically $\geq 2048$ bits in modern security) and generator $g$. Both are **public** and may be transmitted in the clear.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps — "The Why & The How"

The protocol proceeds in **three logical phases**: *Setup*, *Key Generation & Exchange*, and *Shared Secret Derivation*.

### Phase 1 — Public Parameter Setup
- A trusted authority (or Alice and Bob mutually) selects a **large prime** $p$ and a **primitive root** $g \bmod p$.
- *Why:* The multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ generated by $g$ is **cyclic** of order $p-1$, providing the algebraic structure that makes DLP hard.
- *How:* $g$ is primitive if its powers $\{g^{1}, g^{2}, \dots, g^{p-1}\}$ generate every non-zero residue modulo $p$.

### Phase 2 — Private & Public Key Generation
- Alice chooses a **random secret** $a \in [1, p-2]$ and computes $A \equiv g^{a} \pmod{p}$.
- Bob chooses a **random secret** $b \in [1, p-2]$ and computes $B \equiv g^{b} \pmod{p}$.
- *Why:* Raising $g$ to a private exponent is a **trapdoor one-way function** — easy to compute, hard to invert (DLP).
- *How:* Modular exponentiation via **square-and-multiply** runs in $O(\log p)$ time.

### Phase 3 — Exchange & Shared Secret Derivation
- Alice sends $A$ to Bob; Bob sends $B$ to Alice. Both are public.
- Alice computes $K_{A} = B^{a} \bmod p = (g^{b})^{a} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
- Bob computes $K_{B} = A^{b} \bmod p = (g^{a})^{b} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
- *Why:* The shared secret is symmetric because exponentiation in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ is **commutative** — the order of exponentiation does not matter.
- *How:* Eve sees $g, p, A, B$ but cannot extract $a$ or $b$ without solving the DLP, which is **exponentially hard** for well-chosen $p$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A \equiv g^{a} \pmod{p}$ | Alice's public key | $1 \le A \le p-1$ |
| 2 | $B \equiv g^{b} \pmod{p}$ | Bob's public key | $1 \le B \le p-1$ |
| 3 | $K \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p}$ | Shared secret (Alice) | $1 \le K \le p-1$ |
| 4 | $K \equiv g^{ba} \pmod{p}$ | Shared secret (Bob) | $1 \le K \le p-1$ |
| 5 | $K_{A} \equiv B^{a} \pmod{p}$ | Alice's derivation | equals $K$ |
| 6 | $K_{B} \equiv A^{b} \pmod{p}$ | Bob's derivation | equals $K$ |
| 7 | $\text{ord}(g) = p-1$ | Primitive root order | used to define $g$ |
| 8 | $\text{Security} = O(e^{\sqrt[3]{\ln p \cdot (\ln \ln p)^{2}}})$ | Best known DLP cost (Index Calculus) | sub-exponential in $\log p$ |
| 9 | $\phi(p) = p-1$ | Euler's totient for prime $p$ | used in group analysis |
| 10 | $g^{ab} = (g^{a})^{b} = (g^{b})^{a} \pmod{p}$ | Commutativity property | guarantees $K_{A} = K_{B}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** The vertical bar in conditional congruences $x \equiv y \pmod{p}$ is **part of the operator name**, not a markdown table separator. The table syntax remains valid because the bar is **inside** the math expression, not acting as a column delimiter.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **TLS/SSL Handshakes (historically):** DHKE (and ECDHE) negotiates the session key for HTTPS.
- **IPSec / VPN Tunnels:** IKE (Internet Key Exchange) protocol uses DH groups (e.g., Group 14 = 2048-bit MODP).
- **SSH (Diffie-Hellman Group Exchange):** Secures remote shell sessions.
- **Signal Protocol (Messaging Apps):** X3DH (Extended Triple Diffie-Hellman) underlies Signal, WhatsApp, and Facebook Messenger.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of Symmetry — Why Both Parties Arrive at the Same Secret

> **Claim:** $K_{A} = K_{B}$, i.e., $B^{a} \equiv A^{b} \pmod{p}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{A} &\equiv B^{a} \pmod{p} && \text{(Alice computes Bob's public value raised to her private key)} \\
     &\equiv (g^{b})^{a} \pmod{p} && \text{(Substitute } B = g^{b} \pmod{p}\text{)} \\
     &\equiv g^{b \cdot a} \pmod{p} && \text{(Law of exponents: } (x^{m})^{n} = x^{mn}\text{)} \\
     &\equiv g^{a \cdot b} \pmod{p} && \text{(Commutativity of integer multiplication)} \\
     &\equiv (g^{a})^{b} \pmod{p} && \text{(Law of exponents)} \\
     &\equiv A^{b} \pmod{p} && \text{(Substitute } A = g^{a} \pmod{p}\text{)} \\
     &\equiv K_{B} \pmod{p} && \text{(Bob's computation)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** $K_{A} = K_{B} = g^{ab} \pmod{p}$. The shared secret is identical. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Security Argument — Reduction to the Discrete Logarithm Problem

> **Claim:** An eavesdropper knowing $\{p, g, A, B\}$ but not $\{a, b\}$ cannot feasibly compute $K$.

- Eve can compute $A = g^{a} \bmod p$, but **recovering $a$ from $A$** is the DLP.
- Similarly recovering $b$ from $B = g^{b} \bmod p$ is the DLP.
- Computing $g^{ab} \bmod p$ directly from $A$ and $B$ is the **Diffie-Hellman Problem (DHP)**.
- The DHP is computationally equivalent to DLP under standard assumptions (in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$).
- Best known classical algorithm (Index Calculus / Number Field Sieve) runs in **sub-exponential** time $\approx e^{c \cdot (\ln p)^{1/3} (\ln \ln p)^{2/3}}$.
- Therefore for $p \ge 2048$ bits, the attack is infeasible with current technology.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Given (public):** $p = 23$, $g = 5$.

> **Step 1 — Verify $g = 5$ is a primitive root mod $23$:** We need $\text{ord}(5) = 22 = p-1$. The factorization of $p-1 = 2 \times 11$. Check $5^{2} \bmod 23 = 25 \bmod 23 = 2 \neq 1$ and $5^{11} \bmod 23 = ?$. Computing powers: $5^1 = 5$, $5^2 = 2$, $5^4 = 4$, $5^{11} = 5^8 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 = (5^4)^2 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 4^2 \cdot 10 = 16 \cdot 10 = 160 \bmod 23$. $160 / 23 = 6$ remainder $22$, so $5^{11} \equiv 22 \equiv -1 \pmod{23} \neq 1$. Hence $g = 5$ is a valid generator. ✓

> **Step 2 — Alice's private key:** Let $a = 6$.

**Step 3 — Compute Alice's public value $A$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\equiv g^{a} \pmod{p} \\
  &\equiv 5^{6} \pmod{23} \\
  &\equiv 15625 \pmod{23}
\end{aligned}
$$

Division: $15625 \div 23 = 679$ remainder $15625 - (679 \times 23) = 15625 - 15617 = 8$.

$$
A \equiv 8 \pmod{23}
$$

> **Step 4 — Bob's private key:** Let $b = 15$.

**Step 5 — Compute Bob's public value $B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &\equiv g^{b} \pmod{p} \\
  &\equiv 5^{15} \pmod{23}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using repeated squaring:
- $5^{1} \equiv 5$
- $5^{2} \equiv 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$
- $5^{4} \equiv 2^{2} = 4 \pmod{23}$
- $5^{8} \equiv 4^{2} = 16 \pmod{23}$

Now $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$, so:
- $5^{15} \equiv 5^{8} \cdot 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1} \pmod{23}$
- $5^{15} \equiv 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \pmod{23}$
- $16 \cdot 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 46 = 18 \pmod{23}$
- $18 \cdot 2 = 36 \equiv 36 - 23 = 13 \pmod{23}$
- $13 \cdot 5 = 65 \equiv 65 - 46 = 19 \pmod{23}$

$$
B \equiv 19 \pmod{23}
$$

> **Step 6 — Exchange:** Alice sends $A = 8$; Bob sends $B = 19$ (both public).

**Step 7 — Alice derives shared secret $K_{A}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{A} &\equiv B^{a} \pmod{p} \\
      &\equiv 19^{6} \pmod{23}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using repeated squaring:
- $19^{1} \equiv 19$
- $19^{2} \equiv 361 \equiv 361 - (15 \times 23) = 361 - 345 = 16 \pmod{23}$
- $19^{4} \equiv 16^{2} = 256 \equiv 256 - (11 \times 23) = 256 - 253 = 3 \pmod{23}$

Now $6 = 4 + 2$, so:
- $19^{6} \equiv 19^{4} \cdot 19^{2} \equiv 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \equiv 48 - 46 = 2 \pmod{23}$

$$
K_{A} \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

**Step 8 — Bob derives shared secret $K_{B}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{B} &\equiv A^{b} \pmod{p} \\
      &\equiv 8^{15} \pmod{23}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using repeated squaring:
- $8^{1} \equiv 8$
- $8^{2} \equiv 64 \equiv 64 - 46 = 18 \pmod{23}$
- $8^{4} \equiv 18^{2} = 324 \equiv 324 - (14 \times 23) = 324 - 322 = 2 \pmod{23}$
- $8^{8} \equiv 2^{2} = 4 \pmod{23}$

Now $15 = 8 + 4 + 2 + 1$, so:
- $8^{15} \equiv 8^{8} \cdot 8^{4} \cdot 8^{2} \cdot 8^{1} \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \pmod{23}$
- $4 \cdot 2 = 8 \pmod{23}$
- $8 \cdot 18 = 144 \equiv 144 - (6 \times 23) = 144 - 138 = 6 \pmod{23}$
- $6 \cdot 8 = 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$

$$
K_{B} \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

> **Step 9 — Verification:** $K_{A} = K_{B} = 2$. ✓ The shared secret is correctly established.

## 3.4 The Discrete Logarithm Problem (Formal Statement)

> [!NOTE]
> **DLP:** Given a prime $p$, a generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, and an element $y \in \mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$, find the unique integer $x \in [0, p-2]$ such that $g^{x} \equiv y \pmod{p}$.

**Difficulty comparison table for KTU exams:**

| Group | Problem | Classical Best Attack | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ (DH) | DLP | Index Calculus / NFS | Sub-exponential |
| $\mathbb{Z}_{n}^{*}$ (RSA) | Integer Factorization | General Number Field Sieve | Sub-exponential |
| Elliptic Curve (ECDH) | ECDLP | Pollard-rho, Pollard-kangaroo | Fully exponential |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Protocol Flow (Mermaid)

The following diagram captures the **message-passing architecture** between Alice, Bob, and the eavesdropper Eve across the insecure channel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["ALICE Pick private key a"] --> A2["ALICE Compute A equals g power a mod p"]
    A2 --> A3["ALICE Send A to BOB over public channel"]
    B1["BOB Pick private key b"] --> B2["BOB Compute B equals g power b mod p"]
    B2 --> B3["BOB Send B to ALICE over public channel"]
    A3 --> B4["BOB Receive A from ALICE"]
    B3 --> A4["ALICE Receive B from BOB"]
    B4 --> B5["BOB Compute K equals A power b mod p"]
    A4 --> A5["ALICE Compute K equals B power a mod p"]
    A5 --> A6["ALICE Shared secret K"]
    B5 --> B6["BOB Shared secret K"]
    A6 --> VERIFY["VERIFY K equals g power ab mod p for both parties"]
    B6 --> VERIFY
    E1["EVE Eavesdropper"] -. observes .-> A3
    E1 -. observes .-> B3
    E1 -. cannot invert DLP .-> A6
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUBLIC["PUBLIC CHANNEL Eve can read everything here"]
        PUB1["Public parameters p and g"]
        PUB2["Alice public value A"]
        PUB3["Bob public value B"]
    end
    subgraph ALICE["ALICE SIDE Private workspace"]
        A_PRIV["Private key a random in 1 to p-2"]
        A_PUB["Compute A equals g power a mod p"]
        A_KEY["Derive K equals B power a mod p"]
    end
    subgraph BOB["BOB SIDE Private workspace"]
        B_PRIV["Private key b random in 1 to p-2"]
        B_PUB["Compute B equals g power b mod p"]
        B_KEY["Derive K equals A power b mod p"]
    end
    PUB1 --> A_PUB
    PUB1 --> B_PUB
    A_PRIV --> A_PUB
    B_PRIV --> B_PUB
    A_PUB --> PUB2
    B_PUB --> PUB3
    PUB2 --> B_KEY
    PUB3 --> A_KEY
    A_KEY --> SHARED["SHARED SECRET K equals g power ab mod p"]
    B_KEY --> SHARED
```

## 4.3 Man-in-the-Middle Attack — Vulnerability of Plain DHKE

> [!WARNING]
> Plain (unauthenticated) Diffie-Hellman is **vulnerable to a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack**. The diagram below illustrates why authentication (digital signatures / certificates) is mandatory in practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1["ALICE wants to talk to BOB"] --> N2["Eve intercepts A from Alice"]
    N3["BOB wants to talk to ALICE"] --> N4["Eve intercepts B from Bob"]
    N2 --> N5["Eve picks e and sends g power e mod p to Bob"]
    N4 --> N6["Eve picks f and sends g power f mod p to Alice"]
    N5 --> N7["Bob computes shared secret with Eve equals g power be mod p"]
    N6 --> N8["Alice computes shared secret with Eve equals g power af mod p"]
    N7 --> MITM["MITM SUCCESS Eve can now decrypt and re encrypt all traffic"]
    N8 --> MITM
```

> **Defense:** Authenticate the exchanged values using **digital signatures**, **certificates (PKI)**, or use **authenticated DH variants** like **Station-to-Station (STS)** protocol or **HMAC-based key confirmation**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange problem. State the mathematical assumption on which its security is based.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange is a cryptographic protocol that allows two parties communicating over an insecure public channel to jointly establish a shared secret key without any prior shared secret information. The protocol is parameterized by a public prime $p$ and a generator $g$ of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$.
>
> **Mathematical Assumption (1.5 marks):** Security rests on the **computational intractability of the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$. Given $y = g^{x} \bmod p$, recovering the exponent $x$ is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large $p$ (typically $\geq 2048$ bits). The closely related **Computational Diffie-Hellman (CDH) problem** — computing $g^{ab}$ from $g^{a}$ and $g^{b}$ — is also assumed to be hard.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. List the public and private parameters in the Diffie-Hellman protocol. Why is the shared secret $K$ never transmitted?** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **Public parameters (1 mark):** Prime modulus $p$, generator $g$, Alice's public value $A = g^{a} \bmod p$, Bob's public value $B = g^{b} \bmod p$.
>
> **Private parameters (1 mark):** Alice's private key $a$ and Bob's private key $b$, each chosen uniformly at random from $\{1, 2, \dots, p-2\}$.
>
> **Why $K$ is never transmitted (1 mark):** The shared secret $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ is **derived independently** by each party using only their own private key and the other party's public value. Transmitting $K$ would defeat the purpose, as any eavesdropper could intercept it. By keeping $K$ local to Alice and Bob, the protocol's security depends solely on the hardness of the DLP applied to the public values $A$ and $B$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange protocol** in detail. Clearly state the public and private parameters and the steps involved. **Assume $p = 11$, $g = 2$**, and let Alice's private key be $a = 7$ and Bob's private key be $b = 4$. Compute the shared secret $K$ that both parties derive. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain why the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** is central to the security of Diffie-Hellman. Compare the security of DH with that of **RSA**. What is a **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** on plain DHKE, and how is it prevented? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Public parameters:** $p = 11$, $g = 2$. (Both public.)

**Step 2 — Verify $g = 2$ is a primitive root mod $11$:** $p-1 = 10 = 2 \times 5$. Compute $2^{2} = 4 \neq 1$ and $2^{5} = 32 \equiv 10 \equiv -1 \pmod{11}$. So $\text{ord}(2) = 10 = p-1$. Hence $g = 2$ is a valid primitive root. ✓

**Step 3 — Alice computes her public value $A$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\equiv g^{a} \pmod{p} \\
  &\equiv 2^{7} \pmod{11} \\
  &\equiv 128 \pmod{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

$128 \div 11 = 11$ remainder $128 - 121 = 7$.

$$
A \equiv 7 \pmod{11}
$$

**Step 4 — Bob computes his public value $B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &\equiv g^{b} \pmod{p} \\
  &\equiv 2^{4} \pmod{11} \\
  &\equiv 16 \pmod{11} \\
  &\equiv 5 \pmod{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Exchange:** Alice sends $A = 7$ to Bob; Bob sends $B = 5$ to Alice. (Both values are public.)

**Step 6 — Alice derives $K_{A}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{A} &\equiv B^{a} \pmod{p} \\
      &\equiv 5^{7} \pmod{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $5^{1} \equiv 5$
- $5^{2} \equiv 25 \equiv 3 \pmod{11}$
- $5^{4} \equiv 3^{2} = 9 \pmod{11}$
- $7 = 4 + 2 + 1$, so $5^{7} \equiv 9 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 135 \pmod{11}$

$135 \div 11 = 12$ remainder $135 - 132 = 3$.

$$
K_{A} \equiv 3 \pmod{11}
$$

**Step 7 — Bob derives $K_{B}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{B} &\equiv A^{b} \pmod{p} \\
      &\equiv 7^{4} \pmod{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $7^{2} \equiv 49 \equiv 49 - 44 = 5 \pmod{11}$
- $7^{4} \equiv 5^{2} = 25 \equiv 3 \pmod{11}$

$$
K_{B} \equiv 3 \pmod{11}
$$

**Step 8 — Verification:** $K_{A} = K_{B} = 3 \pmod{11}$. ✓ Shared secret is $K = 3$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Why DLP is central (2 marks):** The security of DHKE depends on the assumption that given $A = g^{a} \bmod p$, an adversary cannot efficiently compute $a$ (the DLP). If Eve could solve DLP, she would recover $a$ from the publicly transmitted $A$, then compute $K = B^{a} \bmod p$ herself and break the protocol. The best known classical algorithm for DLP in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ — the **Number Field Sieve** — has sub-exponential complexity, so for primes $p$ of 2048+ bits, the DLP is computationally infeasible.

**Comparison with RSA (2 marks):**

| Aspect | DHKE | RSA |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying hard problem | DLP in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ | Integer Factorization of $n = p \cdot q$ |
| Best classical attack | Number Field Sieve (NFS) | General NFS (GNFS) |
| Key sizes for equivalent security | 2048-bit prime | 2048-bit modulus |
| Primary use | Key exchange / agreement | Encryption & digital signatures |
| Forward secrecy | Yes (if ephemeral keys used) | No (in standard textbook RSA) |

**MITM Attack (2 marks):** In a plain DHKE without authentication, an active adversary **Mallory** can sit between Alice and Bob, intercept $A$ and $B$, replace them with her own public values $g^{e}$ and $g^{f}$ (using her own private keys $e$ and $f$), and compute two separate shared secrets — one with Alice and one with Bob. Mallory can then decrypt, read, and re-encrypt all traffic transparently, while Alice and Bob each believe they share a key with each other.

**Prevention (1 mark):** The MITM attack is prevented by **authentication of the public values** $A$ and $B$. Practical solutions include:
- **Digital signatures** on the exchanged values.
- **Public-key certificates** issued by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)** within a **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)**.
- **Station-to-Station (STS)** protocol that signs the DH exchange.
- **Password-Authenticated Key Exchange (PAKE)** when the parties share a low-entropy password.

---

> **OR**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange protocol** between two users Alice and Bob. Given $p = 17$, $g = 3$, $a = 5$ (Alice's private key), and $b = 9$ (Bob's private key), compute the shared secret key $K$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **strengths and weaknesses** of the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange protocol. Briefly explain how **Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH)** differs from classical DHKE. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Step 1 — Public parameters:** $p = 17$, $g = 3$. Both are publicly known.

**Step 2 — Verify generator:** $p-1 = 16 = 2^{4}$. Prime factors of 16 are just 2. Need $3^{8} \bmod 17 \neq 1$. $3^{2} = 9$, $3^{4} = 81 \equiv 81 - 68 = 13 \pmod{17}$, $3^{8} \equiv 13^{2} = 169 \equiv 169 - 170 = -1 \equiv 16 \pmod{17} \neq 1$. So $g = 3$ is primitive. ✓

**Step 3 — Alice's public value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\equiv 3^{5} \pmod{17} \\
  &\equiv 243 \pmod{17}
\end{aligned}
$$

$243 \div 17 = 14$ remainder $243 - 238 = 5$.

$$
A \equiv 5 \pmod{17}
$$

**Step 4 — Bob's public value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &\equiv 3^{9} \pmod{17}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $3^{1} \equiv 3$
- $3^{2} \equiv 9$
- $3^{4} \equiv 9^{2} = 81 \equiv 81 - 68 = 13 \pmod{17}$
- $3^{8} \equiv 13^{2} = 169 \equiv 16 \equiv -1 \pmod{17}$
- $9 = 8 + 1$, so $3^{9} \equiv 3^{8} \cdot 3^{1} \equiv (-1) \cdot 3 = -3 \equiv 14 \pmod{17}$

$$
B \equiv 14 \pmod{17}
$$

**Step 5 — Exchange:** $A = 5$ sent to Bob; $B = 14$ sent to Alice.

**Step 6 — Alice's derivation $K_{A}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{A} &\equiv 14^{5} \pmod{17}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $14^{1} \equiv 14$
- $14^{2} \equiv 196 \equiv 196 - 187 = 9 \pmod{17}$ (since $17 \times 11 = 187$)
- $14^{4} \equiv 9^{2} = 81 \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$
- $5 = 4 + 1$, so $14^{5} \equiv 13 \cdot 14 = 182 \pmod{17}$

$182 \div 17 = 10$ remainder $182 - 170 = 12$.

$$
K_{A} \equiv 12 \pmod{17}
$$

**Step 7 — Bob's derivation $K_{B}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{B} &\equiv 5^{9} \pmod{17}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $5^{1} \equiv 5$
- $5^{2} \equiv 25 \equiv 8 \pmod{17}$
- $5^{4} \equiv 8^{2} = 64 \equiv 64 - 51 = 13 \pmod{17}$
- $5^{8} \equiv 13^{2} = 169 \equiv 16 \pmod{17}$
- $9 = 8 + 1$, so $5^{9} \equiv 16 \cdot 5 = 80 \pmod{17}$

$80 \div 17 = 4$ remainder $80 - 68 = 12$.

$$
K_{B} \equiv 12 \pmod{17}
$$

**Step 8 — Verification:** $K_{A} = K_{B} = 12 \pmod{17}$. ✓ Shared secret is $K = 12$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Strengths of DHKE (2.5 marks):**
1. **Secure key establishment over insecure channels** without prior shared secrets.
2. **Computational efficiency** — modular exponentiation is fast even for large primes using square-and-multiply ($O(\log p)$ multiplications).
3. **Forward secrecy** when ephemeral keys (e.g., DHE or ECDHE in TLS) are used — past sessions remain secure even if long-term keys are later compromised.
4. **Mathematical elegance** — the protocol is simple, well-studied, and built on a 40+ year-old hardness assumption.
5. **Foundation for advanced protocols** — forms the basis of ElGamal encryption, DSA signatures, and ECDH.

**Weaknesses of DHKE (2.5 marks):**
1. **No authentication** — plain DH is vulnerable to **Man-in-the-Middle attacks** without digital signatures or certificates.
2. **Computational cost** — modular exponentiation with large primes is expensive on low-power devices (motivates ECDH).
3. **Sub-exponential attacks** — Index Calculus and Number Field Sieve reduce the effective security of classical DH; a 2048-bit prime gives roughly 112 bits of security, not 2048.
4. **No built-in key confirmation** — neither party proves possession of the derived key to the other (needs HMAC or signature).
5. **Discrete logarithm in small subgroups** — if $p-1$ has small factors, attackers can exploit Pohlig-Hellman; hence $p$ must be a **safe prime** ($p = 2q + 1$ where $q$ is also prime).

**ECDH vs Classical DHKE (2 marks):**

| Feature | Classical DHKE | ECDH |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying group | $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ (multiplicative) | Elliptic curve $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ (additive) |
| Hard problem | DLP in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$ | ECDLP on elliptic curve |
| Best known attack | Sub-exponential (NFS) | Fully exponential (Pollard-rho) |
| Key size for 128-bit security | 3072 bits | 256 bits |
| Computational cost | Higher | ~10× faster |
| Bandwidth | Larger public values | Compact public keys |

> **Key insight:** ECDH achieves the same security level as classical DH with much smaller keys because no sub-exponential attack is known for the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**. This is why modern protocols (TLS 1.3, Signal, Bitcoin) prefer ECDHE.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in DHKE Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the modulus at intermediate steps** — students often write $5^7 = 128$ and stop, or fail to reduce at every multiplication. Always reduce modulo $p$ after every multiplication to keep numbers small. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Wrong generator verification** — examiners often give a non-primitive $g$. If you blindly compute $g^a \bmod p$ and use it as a public value, the protocol still "works" arithmetically but is insecure. Always verify $\text{ord}(g) = p-1$ first. **(Lose 1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Confusing CDH and DDH** — the **Computational DH (CDH)** problem asks to compute $g^{ab}$ from $(g^a, g^b)$. The **Decisional DH (DDH)** problem asks to distinguish $g^{ab}$ from a random element. Plain DH's security reduces to CDH, not DDH (DDH is *easy* in some groups). **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Forgetting the public-key nature** — DHKE itself provides **key exchange**, not encryption or signatures. If a question asks to "encrypt a message using DH," the answer is "DH is not an encryption scheme; use it to derive a key for AES." **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Skipping the MITM discussion** — many answers describe DHKE correctly but omit the MITM attack and the need for authentication. In 7-mark questions, this is a **mandatory** discussion point. **(Lose 2 marks)**
>
> 6. **Private key range** — private keys must satisfy $1 \le a, b \le p-2$. Using $a = 0$ or $a = p-1$ collapses the protocol (gives public value = 1 or 0).

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **DHKE** is a **key-establishment protocol**, *not* an encryption or signature scheme.
- ✅ Security relies on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$.
- ✅ **Public:** $p$, $g$, $A = g^a \bmod p$, $B = g^b \bmod p$.
- ✅ **Private:** $a$, $b$ (random, $\in [1, p-2]$).
- ✅ **Shared secret:** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$, derived as $K_A = B^a \bmod p = K_B = A^b \bmod p$.
- ✅ **Commutativity of exponentiation** guarantees $K_A = K_B$.
- ✅ **Primitive root** $g$ of prime $p$ generates the entire group $\mathbb{Z}_p^{*}$; always verify it.
- ✅ **No authentication in plain DH** → vulnerable to **MITM attack** → must use digital signatures, certificates, or STS.
- ✅ **Forward secrecy** is achieved only with **ephemeral** keys (DHE / ECDHE).
- ✅ **ECDH** is the modern preferred variant — smaller keys, faster, no sub-exponential attack.
- ✅ **Safe primes** ($p = 2q + 1$) are recommended to prevent Pohlig-Hellman attacks.
- ✅ **Recommended key size today:** at least **2048 bits** for classical DH, **256 bits** for ECDH.
- ✅ **Practical deployments:** TLS, IPsec/IKE, SSH, Signal (X3DH).
- ✅ **Year of invention:** 1976 — the first published public-key protocol in open literature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptographic Hash Functions: Applications of Cryptographic Hash Functions, Secure Hash Algorithm (SHA), SHA-3
Digital Signatures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST637 - FUNDAMENTALS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Digital Signatures # Module 4: Digital Signatures ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > A **Digital Signature** is a cryptographic mechanism that authenticates the identity of the sender of a message and guarantees the integrity of the message content. It is a mathematical scheme that produces a unique, non-repudiable value (the signature) bound to a specific message and a specific sender, using asymmetric (public-key) cryptography. The signature is generated using the sender's **private key** and verified by anyone using the sender's publicly available **public key**. A digital signature is the cryptographic equivalent of a handwritten signature (or a sealed notarized document), but with vastly superior security properties. It must be: - **Authentic** – convinces the recipient that the signer deliberately signed the document. - **Unforgeable** – only the signer can produce the signature. - **Non-repudiable** – the signer cannot later falsely deny signing. - **Tamper-evident** – any modification to the signed data invalidates the signature. - **Reusable** – the signature is bound to a specific document and cannot be transferred. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Wax-Sealed Letter** > Imagine you write a letter in a transparent glass box. You lock the box with a unique padlock whose *key is held only by the recipient*, but the *lock itself (and a copy of the key) is available to everyone*. You drop the locked box into a special slot that mechanically stamps the box with your personal embossed seal *before* it gets locked. Now: > 1. Anyone who sees the box can verify it has *your* seal (authentication). > 2. If someone tries to switch the letter inside, the seal breaks and the lock-key combination no longer matches (integrity). > 3. You cannot deny sending it because only your seal pattern was used (non-repudiation). > 4. The recipient opens the box with their private key, but anyone holding the public key can re-lock and forward it. > > In a digital signature scheme, the *seal* is the mathematical operation applied using the **sender's private key**, and the *lock/key pair* is the public-key infrastructure. ### Fundamental Properties Table | Property | Description | |----------|-------------| | **Authenticity** | Verifies the claimed identity of the signer. | | **Integrity** | Detects any alteration of the data after signing. | | **Non-repudiation** | Signer cannot deny the signature later. | | **Unforgeability** | Computationally infeasible to forge without the private key. | | **Verifiability** | Any third party can verify the signature using the public key. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Differences from Message Authentication Codes (MACs)** > Unlike MACs (which use a shared symmetric secret), digital signatures provide **non-repudiation** because the verification key is public, while the signing key remains private. This is the most exam-relevant distinction in KTU questions. ### Requirements of a Digital Signature Scheme A digital signature scheme consists of three polynomial-time algorithms **(Gen, Sign, Vrfy)**: 1. **Key Generation (Gen)** – outputs a key pair `(pk, sk)` where `pk` is public and `sk` is private. 2. **Signing Algorithm (Sign)** – takes the message `m` and the private key `sk`; outputs a signature `σ = Sign(sk, m)`. 3. **Verification Algorithm (Vrfy)** – takes `(m, σ, pk)`; outputs `1` (valid) or `0` (invalid). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point** > A valid signature must always satisfy: `Vrfy(pk, m, Sign(sk, m)) = 1` for all `m ∈ M` with probability **1** (perfect correctness). ### Classification of Digital Signatures ``` Digital Signatures ├── Direct Digital Signature │ ├── RSA Signature Scheme │ └── ElGamal Signature Scheme ├── Arbitrated Digital Signature └── Standardized Schemes ├── Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA / DSS) ├── RSA-PSS └── ECDSA (Elliptic Curve DSA) ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Direct vs. Arbitrated Digital Signatures ### A. Direct Digital Signature In a direct digital signature, the sender signs the message using their private key, and the receiver verifies it using the sender's public key. **No third party is involved**. **Workflow:** 1. Sender computes a hash of the message: `h = H(m)`. 2. Sender encrypts the hash with their private key: `σ = E(KR_sender, h)`. This is the signature. 3. The signature `σ` is appended to the message `m` and sent. 4. The receiver decrypts the signature using the sender's public key: `h' = D(KU_sender, σ)`. 5. The receiver independently hashes the received message: `h'' = H(m)`. 6. If `h' = h''`, the signature is valid. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Direct digital signatures are vulnerable to **forgery** if the sender's private key is compromised. The scheme also faces the problem of **repudiation by the sender** (claiming their key was stolen). ### B. Arbitrated Digital Signature To overcome the vulnerabilities of direct schemes, an **arbitrator (trusted third party)** is introduced. The arbitrator verifies every signed message before forwarding it to the receiver. **Workflow (Conventional Encryption Arbiter):** 1. `X → A: M \vert\vert E(Kxa, [IDx \vert\vert H(M)])` 2. `A → Y: E(Kay, [IDx \vert\vert M \vert\vert E(Kxa, [IDx \vert\vert H(M)]) \vert\vert T])` Where: - `Kxa` = shared key between X and Arbiter - `Kay` = shared key between Arbiter and Y - `T` = timestamp - `\vert\vert` denotes concatenation > [!NOTE] > **Why Arbitration Helps** > - Prevents the sender from disowning the message (the arbiter witnessed it). > - Prevents the receiver from forging a signature (the arbiter authenticates the source). > - Introduces a single point of failure: the arbiter itself. ## 2.2 RSA Digital Signature Scheme > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Scheme – Most Frequently Asked** The RSA signature scheme directly applies the RSA encryption/decryption primitives, but with the roles of public and private keys swapped in the signing step. ### Key Generation 1. Choose two large primes `p` and `q`. 2. Compute `n = p × q`. 3. Compute Euler's totient: `φ(n) = (p-1)(q-1)`. 4. Choose `e` such that `gcd(e, φ(n)) = 1`. This is the public exponent. 5. Compute `d ≡ e^{-1} (mod φ(n))`. This is the private exponent. **Public Key:** `(n, e)` **Private Key:** `d` ### Signing The sender computes the signature on message `m` (where `m` is treated as an integer in `[0, n-1]`): $$\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$$ ### Verification The receiver recovers the message hash from the signature: $$m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n$$ Then checks if `m' = m` (or equivalently, compares the recovered hash to the hash of the received message). > [!WARNING] > **Critical Security Flaw – Textbook RSA Signing** > Signing the raw message `m` (not its hash) makes RSA signatures **malleable** and vulnerable to existential forgery. Always sign `H(m)` where `H` is a cryptographic hash like **SHA-256**. ## 2.3 RSA Signature with Hash (Recommended Practice) The KTU syllabus emphasizes hash-based signing. **Signing:** $$S = H(M)^{d} \bmod n$$ where `H(M)` is the hash of message `M` (e.g., SHA-256 output truncated/expanded to `n`'s bit length). **Verification:** 1. Extract `S` from the signed message. 2. Compute `H' = S^{e} \bmod n`. 3. Compute `H(M)` independently. 4. Accept signature iff `H' = H(M)`. ## 2.4 ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme The ElGamal signature scheme is a **non-deterministic** scheme – each signature on a given message is different (because of a random per-message value `k`). ### Key Generation 1. Choose a large prime `p` and a primitive root `g` of `p`. 2. Choose a random integer `x` such that `1 ≤ x ≤ p-2`. This is the **private key**. 3. Compute `y = g^{x} \bmod p`. This is the **public key**. **Public Key:** `(p, g, y)` **Private Key:** `x` ### Signing (per message) 1. Choose a random per-message integer `k` with `gcd(k, p-1) = 1`. 2. Compute `r = g^{k} \bmod p`. 3. Compute `K^{-1} \bmod (p-1)`, where `K = k`. 4. Compute `s = K^{-1}(H(m) - x \cdot r) \bmod (p-1)`. **Signature:** the pair `(r, s)`. ### Verification 1. Compute `v_1 = (y^{r} \cdot r^{s}) \bmod p`. 2. Compute `v_2 = g^{H(m)} \bmod p`. 3. Accept signature iff `v_1 = v_2`. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Works (Correctness Proof Idea)** > Given `s ≡ k^{-1}(H(m) - xr) (mod p-1)`, multiplying both sides by `k`: > `k·s ≡ H(m) - x·r (mod p-1)` > `H(m) ≡ x·r + k·s (mod p-1)` > Therefore: `g^{H(m)} ≡ g^{x·r} · g^{k·s} ≡ (g^x)^r · (g^k)^s ≡ y^r · r^s (mod p)`. ## 2.5 Digital Signature Standard (DSS) / Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) The **DSS** is a U.S. Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS 186) for digital signatures. It uses the **DSA** algorithm. ### DSS/DSA Parameters - `p` = a prime modulus of `L` bits, where `L` is a multiple of 64, with **512 ≤ L ≤ 1024** (original); modern implementations use **2048 or 3072 bits**. - `q` = a 160-bit prime divisor of `p - 1`. - `g = h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p`, where `h` is any integer with `1 < h < p-1` and `h^{(p-1)/q} \bmod p > 1`. - `x` = private key, a random integer in `[1, q-1]`. - `y = g^x \bmod p` = public key. - `H(m)` = SHA hash of the message `m`. ### Signing (per message) 1. Choose a random per-message secret `k` in `[1, q-1]`. 2. `r = (g^k \bmod p) \bmod q`. 3. `s = k^{-1}(H(m) + x \cdot r) \bmod q`. **Signature:** `(r, s)`. ### Verification 1. Compute `w = s^{-1} \bmod q`. 2. `u_1 = (H(m) \cdot w) \bmod q`. 3. `u_2 = (r \cdot w) \bmod q`. 4. `v = (g^{u_1} \cdot y^{u_2} \bmod p) \bmod q`. 5. Accept iff `v = r`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Difference from ElGamal** > In ElGamal, the verification involves `H(m)` directly. In DSA, the hash is mixed with `s` and `r` during signing, making DSA **more efficient** because `g^{H(m)}` (an expensive modular exponentiation) is avoided during verification. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Algorithm** | **Signing Equation** | **Verification Equation** | **Signature Length** | |---|---|---|---| | **RSA** | `S = H(m)^d mod n` | `H(m) ≟ S^e mod n` | `\vert n \vert` bits | | **ElGamal** | `r = g^k mod p`
`s = k^{-1}(H(m) - xr) mod (p-1)` | `y^r · r^s mod p ≟ g^{H(m)} mod p` | `2 × \vert p \vert` bits | | **DSA/DSS** | `r = (g^k mod p) mod q`
`s = k^{-1}(H(m) + xr) mod q` | `v = (g^{u_1} y^{u_2} mod p) mod q` | `2 × 160` bits | | **Key Constants** | **Value/Range** | |---|---| | RSA modulus `n` | ≥ **2048 bits** (modern) | | DSA `p` | **1024 to 3072 bits** (multiple of 64) | | DSA `q` | **160 or 256 bits** | | Per-message secret `k` | Must be unique and random (critical for security) | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Reusing `k` across two DSA/ElGamal signatures leaks the private key `x`. This is a famous real-world attack (Sony PS3 breach, 2010). ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Digital signatures are foundational to: - **TLS/SSL Handshakes** – server certificates signed by CAs. - **Code Signing** – operating systems verify software integrity. - **E-Governance & E-Contracts** – legally binding under India's IT Act 2000. - **Blockchain / Bitcoin** – ECDSA signs every transaction. - **Document Workflows** – PDF Digital Signatures, Aadhaar eSign. - **Software Updates** – Microsoft, Apple, and Linux distributions sign their patches. ## 2.8 Attacks and Forgeries | **Attack Type** | **Description** | **Defense** | |---|---|---| | **Key-Only Attack** | Adversary knows only the public key. | Use secure key sizes (≥ 2048 bits). | | **Known Message Attack** | Adversary has signatures on chosen messages. | Use hash-then-sign paradigm. | | **Chosen Message Attack** | Adversary obtains signatures on attacker-chosen messages. | Strong hash function + PKCS#1 v2 padding. | | **Existential Forgery** | Forges a signature for *some* message (not chosen). | Provably secure schemes (RSA-PSS, DSA). | | **Universal Forgery** | Forges signature for *any* chosen message. | Strong schemes; never sign arbitrary inputs. | | **Selective Forgery** | Forges signature for a *specific* chosen message. | Strong hash function. | | **Total Break** | Recovers the private key. | Long keys, secure key storage. | | **Replay Attack** | Reuses an old valid signature. | Include timestamps or nonces. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Example: RSA Digital Signature > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Alice signs the message `m = 5` using RSA with `n = 119`, `e = 5`, `d = 77`. Compute and verify the signature. ### Step 1: Verify the Key Pair We need `e · d ≡ 1 (mod φ(n))`. Since `n = 119 = 7 × 11`, we have: $$\phi(n) = (7-1)(11-1) = 6 \times 10 = 60$$ Check: `e · d = 5 × 77 = 385`. Now compute `385 mod 60`: $$385 = 6 \times 60 + 5 = 365, \quad 385 - 365 = 20$$ Wait, let me recompute. We need `5 × 77 mod 60`: $$5 \times 77 = 385$$ $$385 / 60 = 6.41..., \quad 6 \times 60 = 360, \quad 385 - 360 = 25$$ So `5 × 77 mod 60 = 25 ≠ 1`. The given key pair is invalid for the stated primes. Let us instead use a valid textbook example with `n = 91 = 7 × 13`, `e = 5`, `d = 29`. **Recompute:** $$\phi(91) = (7-1)(13-1) = 6 \times 12 = 72$$ Check: `5 × 29 = 145`, `145 mod 72 = 145 - 2·72 = 145 - 144 = 1`. ✓ Valid. ### Step 2: Sign the Message `m = 26` Compute `S = m^d mod n = 26^{29} mod 91`. Use repeated squaring modulo 91: - `26^1 = 26` - `26^2 = 676 mod 91 = 676 - 7·91 = 676 - 637 = 39` - `26^4 = 39^2 = 1521 mod 91 = 1521 - 16·91 = 1521 - 1456 = 65` - `26^8 = 65^2 = 4225 mod 91 = 4225 - 46·91 = 4225 - 4186 = 39` - `26^{16} = 39^2 = 1521 mod 91 = 65` - `26^{32} mod 91`: But we need only up to `26^{29} = 26^{16} · 26^{8} · 26^{4} · 26^{1}`. Decompose `29 = 16 + 8 + 4 + 1` (binary: 11101). So: $$26^{29} \equiv 26^{16} \cdot 26^{8} \cdot 26^{4} \cdot 26^{1} \pmod{91}$$ $$26^{29} \equiv 65 \cdot 39 \cdot 65 \cdot 26 \pmod{91}$$ Compute step by step: - `65 × 39 = 2535 mod 91`: `91 × 27 = 2457`, so `2535 - 2457 = 78`. - `78 × 65 = 5070 mod 91`: `91 × 55 = 5005`, so `5070 - 5005 = 65`. - `65 × 26 = 1690 mod 91`: `91 × 18 = 1638`, so `1690 - 1638 = 52`. $$\boxed{S = 52}$$ ### Step 3: Verify the Signature Receiver computes `S^e mod n = 52^5 mod 91`. - `52^2 = 2704 mod 91`: `91 × 29 = 2639`, so `2704 - 2639 = 65`. - `52^4 = 65^2 = 4225 mod 91 = 39` (from before). - `52^5 = 52^4 · 52^1 = 39 × 52 = 2028 mod 91`: `91 × 22 = 2002`, so `2028 - 2002 = 26`. $$S^e \bmod n = 26 = m \quad \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification confirmed.** The signature `S = 52` is valid for message `m = 26`. ## 3.2 Worked-Out Example: ElGamal Digital Signature > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** ElGamal parameters: `p = 11`, primitive root `g = 2`, private key `x = 8`, so `y = 2^8 mod 11`. Sign `m = 5` (assume `H(m) = 5`) using per-message secret `k = 9`. ### Step 1: Compute Public Key $$y = 2^8 \bmod 11 = 256 \bmod 11$$ Powers of 2 mod 11: `2, 4, 8, 5, 10, 9, 7, 3` (after 8 steps we get `2^8 mod 11 = 3`). $$y = 3$$ ### Step 2: Compute `r` $$r = g^k \bmod p = 2^9 \bmod 11 = 2 \cdot 2^8 \bmod 11 = 2 \cdot 3 = 6 \bmod 11 = 6$$ $$r = 6$$ ### Step 3: Compute `s` We need `K^{-1} mod (p-1) = 9^{-1} mod 10`. Using the extended Euclidean algorithm: `9u + 10v = 1`. - `10 = 1·9 + 1` - `9 = 9·1 + 0` Back-substitute: `1 = 10 - 1·9`, so `9·(-1) + 10·(1) = 1`. Thus `9^{-1} ≡ -1 ≡ 9 (mod 10)`. $$s = 9^{-1}(H(m) - x \cdot r) \bmod (p-1) = 9 \cdot (5 - 8 \cdot 6) \bmod 10$$ $$s = 9 \cdot (5 - 48) \bmod 10 = 9 \cdot (-43) \bmod 10$$ $$-43 \bmod 10 = -43 + 5\cdot 10 = 7, \text{ so } s = 9 \cdot 7 \bmod 10 = 63 \bmod 10 = 3$$ $$\boxed{(r, s) = (6, 3)}$$ ### Step 4: Verify the Signature Compute `v_1 = y^r · r^s mod p`: - `y^r = 3^6 mod 11`: `3^2 = 9`, `3^4 = 81 mod 11 = 4`, `3^6 = 3^4 · 3^2 = 4 · 9 = 36 mod 11 = 3`. - `r^s = 6^3 mod 11 = 216 mod 11`: `11·19 = 209`, so `216 - 209 = 7`. - `v_1 = 3 · 7 mod 11 = 21 mod 11 = 10`.
